definition: An account of a phenomenon constitutes a scientific explanation if and only if (i) it cites a general law under which the phenomenon can be subsumed, (ii) it specifies the initial conditions from which, together with the law, the phenomenon can be deduced, and (iii) the deduction is logically valid.
conditions: cites a general law under which the phenomenon is subsumed
specifies initial conditions from which the phenomenon can be deduced
the deduction is logically valid
counterexample: The evolutionary explanation of the peacock's tail does not deduce the tail from a general law and initial conditions in any strict logical sense. Rather, it describes a selective mechanism — sexual selection — that renders the trait intelligible without entailing it. The first and third conditions fail, since one cannot validly deduce the peacock's tail from the principles of natural selection, yet the explanation is widely regarded as among the finest achievements of biological science.
missing_condition: The definition requires deduction from general laws, but many scientific explanations operate by describing mechanisms or selective processes that render phenomena intelligible without logically entailing them.
passage: One might propose that an account of a phenomenon constitutes a scientific explanation if and only if it cites a general law under which the phenomenon can be subsumed, it specifies the initial conditions from which, together with the law, the phenomenon can be deduced, and the deduction is logically valid. This definition captures a good many explanations in physics and chemistry. When one explains the boiling of water at a certain temperature by citing the relevant gas laws and the atmospheric pressure, one has subsumed the phenomenon under a general law, specified the initial conditions, and produced a valid deduction. Yet consider the evolutionary explanation of the peacock's tail. Darwin accounted for this extravagant appendage by appealing to sexual selection: peahens prefer males with larger, more colourful tails, and this preference, operating over many generations, has produced the elaborate plumage we observe. This explanation is universally regarded as one of the great achievements of biological science. But it does not cite a law from which the peacock's tail can be deduced. The principles of natural and sexual selection do not entail that any particular species will develop any particular trait; they describe a mechanism that makes the trait intelligible without rendering it logically necessary. One cannot write down premises and derive the peacock's tail as a conclusion. The first and third conditions fail. The definition has taken the deductive structure of certain physical explanations and elevated it to a requirement for all scientific explanation, when in truth some of the most powerful explanations in science proceed by describing causal mechanisms rather than by subsuming phenomena under exceptionless laws.

definition: A generalisation constitutes a scientific law if and only if (i) it is universally quantified, (ii) it admits of no exceptions, and (iii) it is supported by empirical evidence gathered through controlled experimentation.
conditions: universally quantified
admits of no exceptions
supported by evidence from controlled experimentation
counterexample: The laws of planetary motion formulated by Kepler are universally quantified and admit of no exceptions within their domain, yet they were not established through controlled experimentation. Kepler derived them from the painstaking analysis of Tycho Brahe's astronomical observations — data gathered by passive observation of the heavens rather than by any experimental intervention. The third condition fails, yet Kepler's laws are paradigmatic scientific laws.
missing_condition: The definition requires controlled experimentation, but many scientific laws — particularly in astronomy and geology — are established through systematic observation of naturally occurring phenomena rather than through experimental manipulation.
passage: It is sometimes held that a generalisation constitutes a scientific law if and only if it is universally quantified, it admits of no exceptions, and it is supported by empirical evidence gathered through controlled experimentation. This account captures the paradigmatic laws of laboratory science: Boyle's law relating pressure and volume was established through careful experimental manipulation, and Ohm's law relating voltage, current, and resistance was similarly grounded in controlled measurement. Yet consider the laws of planetary motion set forth by Kepler in the early seventeenth century. These laws — that the planets move in ellipses with the sun at one focus, that the radius vector sweeps out equal areas in equal times, and that the squares of the periods are proportional to the cubes of the semi-major axes — are universally quantified and admit of no exceptions within their domain. They are among the most celebrated generalisations in the history of science. But they were not established through controlled experimentation. Kepler did not manipulate the orbits of the planets; he could not vary the conditions under which they moved. He derived his laws from the observational records compiled by Tycho Brahe, who had spent decades measuring the positions of the planets with unprecedented accuracy. These were data gathered by passive observation of the heavens, not by experimental intervention. The third condition fails. The definition has mistaken the method of the laboratory for a necessary condition of scientific lawhood, when some of the greatest laws of science have been established through the systematic analysis of naturally occurring phenomena that no human agent could control.

definition: A body of propositions constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) it provides a unified explanation of a range of phenomena, (ii) it is formulated in precise mathematical language, and (iii) it is the product of a single coherent intellectual programme.
conditions: provides unified explanation of a range of phenomena
formulated in precise mathematical language
product of a single coherent intellectual programme
counterexample: Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection provides a unified explanation of the diversity, adaptation, and distribution of living organisms, yet it was not formulated in precise mathematical language. Darwin's arguments were qualitative, discursive, and illustrated by examples rather than expressed in equations. The second condition fails, yet the theory of evolution is one of the most important scientific theories ever proposed.
missing_condition: The definition demands mathematical formulation, but some of the most powerful scientific theories — particularly in biology and the earth sciences — achieve their explanatory force through qualitative concepts and causal reasoning rather than through mathematical formalisms.
passage: One might hold that a body of propositions constitutes a scientific theory if and only if it provides a unified explanation of a range of phenomena, it is formulated in precise mathematical language, and it is the product of a single coherent intellectual programme. This definition captures certain theories admirably: Newton's mechanics, Maxwell's electrodynamics, and Einstein's general relativity are each formulated in mathematical language, each explains a range of phenomena in a unified manner, and each was developed as a coherent programme. Yet consider Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection. It provides a unified explanation of an astonishing range of phenomena — the diversity of species, the adaptation of organisms to their environments, the geographical distribution of plants and animals, the existence of vestigial structures, the patterns revealed by comparative anatomy and embryology. No one would deny that it is a scientific theory of the first importance. But it was not formulated in precise mathematical language. Darwin's arguments in the Origin of Species are qualitative and discursive; they proceed by analogy, by the accumulation of examples, and by careful reasoning about causes and effects. There are no equations, no formal derivations, no mathematical models. The second condition fails. The definition has taken the mathematical character of the physical sciences and imposed it as a requirement upon all scientific theories, when in truth some of the most profound theories in the history of science have achieved their explanatory power through qualitative insight and causal reasoning rather than through mathematical formalism.

definition: A report constitutes a scientific observation if and only if (i) it describes a state of affairs directly apprehended by the senses, (ii) the observer is in a position to perceive the phenomenon without the mediation of instruments, and (iii) any competent observer placed in the same circumstances would report the same thing.
conditions: describes a state of affairs directly apprehended by the senses
observer perceives without mediation of instruments
any competent observer would report the same
counterexample: The observation of a distant galaxy through a radio telescope is universally accepted as a scientific observation, yet the observer does not directly apprehend the galaxy by her senses. She perceives a pattern of signals on a screen, translated by complex instrumentation from radio waves that no human sense organ can detect. The first and second conditions fail, yet the observation is foundational to modern astrophysics.
missing_condition: The definition restricts observation to unaided sense perception, but much of modern science depends upon instrument-mediated observation, in which the observer perceives the output of instruments that detect phenomena lying beyond the range of the unaided senses.
passage: A natural account holds that a report constitutes a scientific observation if and only if it describes a state of affairs directly apprehended by the senses, the observer is in a position to perceive the phenomenon without the mediation of instruments, and any competent observer placed in the same circumstances would report the same thing. This definition captures the observations of early natural history well enough: a botanist who describes the colour and shape of a flower, a zoologist who records the behaviour of a bird, an astronomer who notes the position of a star with the naked eye. In each case the observer apprehends the phenomenon directly, without instrumental mediation, and any competent observer could confirm the report. Yet consider the observation of a distant galaxy through a radio telescope. The astronomer does not see the galaxy; the radio waves it emits lie far outside the visible spectrum. What she perceives is a pattern of signals displayed on a screen, signals that have been collected by a large antenna, amplified by electronic circuits, and processed by sophisticated software before they appear in any form intelligible to human perception. No unaided sense organ can detect radio waves at all. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the observation is accepted without hesitation by the scientific community; entire branches of astrophysics rest upon such instrument-mediated observations. The definition has identified observation with naked-eye perception, when the great expansion of scientific observation in the modern era has been achieved precisely by extending perception through instruments that detect what the unaided senses cannot.

definition: A scientific theory T1 has been reduced to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every law of T1 can be derived as a logical consequence of the laws of T2 together with appropriate bridge principles, (ii) every term of T1 can be defined in terms of the vocabulary of T2, and (iii) T2 explains everything that T1 explains and more besides.
conditions: every law of T1 derivable from laws of T2 with bridge principles
every term of T1 definable in T2 vocabulary
T2 explains everything T1 explains and more
counterexample: Classical thermodynamics is often said to have been reduced to statistical mechanics, yet the reduction is not straightforward in the manner the definition requires. The second law of thermodynamics, which states that entropy never decreases, corresponds in statistical mechanics only to an overwhelmingly probable tendency, not to a strict law. The thermodynamic concept of temperature does not translate precisely into a single quantity in statistical mechanics without idealising assumptions. The first and second conditions fail in their strict form, yet the relationship between thermodynamics and statistical mechanics is the most celebrated case of reduction in the history of science.
missing_condition: The definition requires exact derivability and precise translation of terms, but actual reductions in science are typically approximate, involving idealisations, limiting conditions, and correspondences that fall short of strict logical derivation.
passage: It is sometimes proposed that a scientific theory T1 has been reduced to a theory T2 if and only if every law of T1 can be derived as a logical consequence of the laws of T2 together with appropriate bridge principles, every term of T1 can be defined in terms of the vocabulary of T2, and T2 explains everything that T1 explains and more besides. This definition has the virtue of clarity: reduction is a matter of logical derivation and definitional translation. It appears to capture the spirit of reductionism — the idea that one science is nothing over and above another, more fundamental science. Yet consider the most celebrated case of reduction in the history of science: the reduction of classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. One might suppose that the laws of thermodynamics are simply derivable from the mechanics of molecules. But the situation is more complicated than the definition allows. The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of an isolated system never decreases. In statistical mechanics, however, this corresponds not to a strict law but to an overwhelmingly probable tendency; there is always a minute but non-zero probability that entropy will spontaneously decrease. The thermodynamic law is not derivable as a logical consequence of statistical mechanics without additional idealising assumptions. Similarly, the concept of temperature in thermodynamics does not translate precisely into a single statistical-mechanical quantity without approximations that hold only in certain limiting cases. The first and second conditions fail in their strict form. Yet no philosopher of science would deny that statistical mechanics has provided a reductive account of thermodynamics. The definition has demanded a logical precision that actual scientific reductions do not exhibit, overlooking the approximate and idealised character of the correspondences that constitute real cases of reduction.

definition: A procedure constitutes a scientific experiment if and only if (i) the investigator deliberately manipulates an independent variable, (ii) all other relevant variables are held constant or controlled, and (iii) the results are quantitatively measured.
conditions: investigator deliberately manipulates an independent variable
all other relevant variables held constant or controlled
results are quantitatively measured
counterexample: Darwin's experiments on the pollination of orchids involved placing various insects near flowers and observing which ones effected pollination and by what means. He did not hold all other relevant variables constant — the orchids grew in natural conditions, subject to weather, soil variation, and the presence of other organisms — and many of his results were recorded as qualitative descriptions of the insects' behaviour rather than as quantitative measurements. The second and third conditions fail, yet Darwin's orchid experiments are rightly regarded as exemplary scientific work.
missing_condition: The definition requires strict control of variables and quantitative measurement, but many legitimate experiments — particularly in field biology and natural history — operate under conditions that cannot be fully controlled and yield qualitative results that are nonetheless scientifically valuable.
passage: One might define a scientific experiment as a procedure in which the investigator deliberately manipulates an independent variable, all other relevant variables are held constant or controlled, and the results are quantitatively measured. This definition captures the controlled experiment of the physics laboratory admirably: one varies the voltage and measures the current, holding temperature and resistance constant; one alters the angle of incidence and records the angle of refraction. Yet consider Darwin's experiments on the pollination of orchids, published in his remarkable study of 1862. Darwin placed various insects in proximity to orchid flowers and observed which insects visited which flowers, how the pollen was attached to the insect's body, and by what mechanism it was transferred to another flower. He manipulated the situation by introducing insects and by removing parts of the flower to test their function. But he did not hold all other relevant variables constant. His orchids grew in their natural environment, subject to the vagaries of weather, variation in soil, and the unpredictable presence of other organisms. And many of his results were not quantitatively measured; they were recorded as careful qualitative descriptions of the behaviour of insects and the morphology of flowers. The second and third conditions fail. Yet Darwin's orchid experiments are rightly celebrated as exemplary science. The definition has taken the conditions of the physics laboratory — tight control and precise measurement — and imposed them upon all scientific experimentation, when in truth some of the most illuminating experiments in biology proceed under natural conditions and yield qualitative insights of the highest importance.

definition: A procedure constitutes a scientific measurement if and only if (i) it assigns a numerical value to a property of the object under study, (ii) the assignment is carried out by means of a calibrated instrument, and (iii) the procedure is repeatable — the same property measured under the same conditions yields the same value.
conditions: assigns a numerical value to a property
carried out by means of a calibrated instrument
repeatable under the same conditions
counterexample: The Mohs hardness scale assigns a numerical value to the hardness of a mineral not by means of a calibrated instrument but by a scratch test: a mineral is harder than another if it can scratch the other but not be scratched by it. The procedure uses no instrument in the ordinary sense; it employs only the minerals themselves. The second condition fails, yet the Mohs scale has been a standard measurement in mineralogy for two centuries.
missing_condition: The definition requires a calibrated instrument, but some well-established measurements are carried out through direct comparison between objects, without any instrument mediating the assignment of value.
passage: A plausible account holds that a procedure constitutes a scientific measurement if and only if it assigns a numerical value to a property of the object under study, the assignment is carried out by means of a calibrated instrument, and the procedure is repeatable — the same property measured under the same conditions yields the same value. This captures many familiar measurements: one measures temperature with a thermometer, mass with a balance, electrical resistance with a multimeter. In each case a calibrated instrument mediates the assignment of a number to a property, and the measurement can be repeated. Yet consider the Mohs hardness scale, which has been employed in mineralogy since Friedrich Mohs introduced it in 1812. The scale assigns a number from one to ten to the hardness of a mineral. But the assignment is not carried out by means of a calibrated instrument. Instead, one simply determines whether one mineral can scratch another. If mineral A scratches mineral B but B cannot scratch A, then A is harder. The minerals themselves serve as the standard of comparison; there is no instrument in the ordinary sense of the word — no device with a dial, no calibrated mechanism. The second condition fails. Yet the Mohs scale is universally accepted as a measurement of hardness and has been used by geologists and mineralogists for over two hundred years. The definition has insisted upon instrumental mediation as a necessary feature of measurement, when some well-established measurements proceed by direct comparison between objects, without any instrument intervening between the observer and the property measured.

definition: A statement constitutes a scientific prediction if and only if (i) it is derived deductively from a well-confirmed theory, (ii) it specifies a definite outcome that has not yet been observed, and (iii) it is precise enough to be confirmed or refuted by a single crucial observation or experiment.
conditions: derived deductively from well-confirmed theory
specifies a definite unobserved outcome
precise enough to be confirmed or refuted by single crucial test
counterexample: Darwin's prediction that transitional fossils connecting major groups of organisms would eventually be discovered was not deduced from a formalised theory in any strict sense; it was inferred qualitatively from the hypothesis of common descent. Moreover, it could not be confirmed or refuted by a single crucial observation — it required the gradual accumulation of fossil discoveries over many decades. The first and third conditions fail, yet this prediction is one of the most celebrated in the history of biology, and its progressive confirmation has been a major source of support for evolutionary theory.
missing_condition: The definition requires deductive derivation and a single decisive test, but many important scientific predictions are qualitative inferences from theoretical principles whose confirmation is cumulative rather than instantaneous.
passage: One might hold that a statement constitutes a scientific prediction if and only if it is derived deductively from a well-confirmed theory, it specifies a definite outcome that has not yet been observed, and it is precise enough to be confirmed or refuted by a single crucial observation or experiment. This definition captures the classic predictions of mathematical physics: the prediction of Neptune's position from perturbations in the orbit of Uranus, or the prediction of the bending of light by the sun from general relativity. In each case a precise numerical result was deduced from a theory and confirmed by a single decisive observation. Yet consider Darwin's prediction that transitional fossils — organisms intermediate in form between major groups — would be found in the geological record. Darwin argued that if all species have descended from common ancestors through gradual modification, then the geological record ought to contain fossils exhibiting intermediate characteristics. This prediction was not derived deductively from a formalised theory; Darwin's reasoning was qualitative, proceeding from the general hypothesis of descent with modification to a broad expectation about the fossil record. And it could not be confirmed or refuted by a single crucial observation. The discovery of one transitional fossil — Archaeopteryx in 1861 — was encouraging, but the prediction called for a pattern of discoveries across many groups of organisms, and its confirmation has been a gradual process extending over more than a century. The first and third conditions fail. The definition has modelled all prediction on the deductive predictions of mathematical physics, when many of the most important predictions in science are qualitative inferences whose confirmation is cumulative.

definition: A system of classification is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) it groups objects according to a single unifying principle, (ii) every object in the domain belongs to exactly one class, and (iii) the classes are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive.
conditions: groups objects according to single unifying principle
every object belongs to exactly one class
classes are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive
counterexample: The periodic table of the elements groups elements according to atomic number, and one might suppose it satisfies the definition. Yet consider the classification of biological species. The Linnaean system groups organisms into a nested hierarchy — kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species — but it does not group according to a single unifying principle; it combines morphological similarity, reproductive isolation, and evolutionary descent. Moreover, instances of hybridisation produce organisms that belong to no single species in an unambiguous manner. The first and second conditions fail, yet the Linnaean system is the foundational classification of biology.
missing_condition: The definition requires a single unifying principle and unambiguous assignment, but the most important scientific classifications often employ multiple criteria and tolerate borderline cases where assignment is indeterminate.
passage: A natural account holds that a system of classification is scientifically adequate if and only if it groups objects according to a single unifying principle, every object in the domain belongs to exactly one class, and the classes are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive. This definition captures the neatness of certain classifications in chemistry and physics: the periodic table groups elements by atomic number, and each element belongs to exactly one place. Yet consider the classification of biological species. The Linnaean system, which has served as the foundation of biological taxonomy since the eighteenth century, groups organisms into a nested hierarchy of kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, and species. But it does not classify according to a single unifying principle. At different levels of the hierarchy, and even at the same level, taxonomists employ morphological similarity, reproductive isolation, genetic distance, and evolutionary descent as criteria, and these criteria do not always coincide. Furthermore, the biological world presents cases — hybridisation between species, ring species, asexual organisms — in which an organism cannot be assigned unambiguously to a single species. The mule, offspring of a horse and a donkey, belongs fully to neither species. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the Linnaean system remains the indispensable framework of biological science. The definition has demanded a logical tidiness that the complexity of the natural world does not permit, overlooking the fact that the most important scientific classifications may employ multiple criteria and tolerate genuine indeterminacy at their boundaries.

definition: A representation constitutes a scientific model if and only if (i) it is structurally isomorphic to the system it represents, (ii) it simplifies the system by omitting irrelevant features, and (iii) it enables the derivation of quantitative predictions about the system's behaviour.
conditions: structurally isomorphic to the system it represents
simplifies by omitting irrelevant features
enables derivation of quantitative predictions
counterexample: Watson and Crick's physical model of the DNA double helix was a construction of metal rods and clamps arranged to represent the spatial relationships among the atoms of the molecule. It was not structurally isomorphic to DNA in any precise mathematical sense — it was a three-dimensional sculpture, not a mapping preserving formal relations. And while it suggested that DNA could replicate by strand separation, it did not by itself enable the derivation of quantitative predictions. The first and third conditions fail, yet this model is one of the most important in the history of science.
missing_condition: The definition requires structural isomorphism and quantitative predictive power, but some of the most fruitful scientific models are physical or conceptual representations that capture qualitative features of a system — its geometry, its mechanism, its causal structure — without satisfying any strict formal correspondence or yielding quantitative predictions.
passage: One might propose that a representation constitutes a scientific model if and only if it is structurally isomorphic to the system it represents, it simplifies the system by omitting irrelevant features, and it enables the derivation of quantitative predictions about the system's behaviour. This definition captures the mathematical models of physics well: a set of differential equations may be structurally isomorphic to a physical system, simplified by suitable approximations, and capable of yielding precise numerical predictions. Yet consider Watson and Crick's celebrated model of the DNA double helix. Their model was a physical construction of metal rods and clamps, built in a Cambridge laboratory in 1953, arranged so as to represent the spatial relationships among the atoms of the deoxyribonucleic acid molecule. It was a three-dimensional sculpture, not a mathematical structure, and it was not isomorphic to DNA in any precise formal sense. One could not define a mapping from the elements of the model to the elements of the molecule that preserved the relevant structural relations in the manner that a mathematical isomorphism requires. The model simplified, to be sure, but its chief virtue was that it captured the geometry of the molecule — the helical twist, the complementary base-pairing, the anti-parallel orientation of the strands — in a manner that suggested a mechanism of replication. It did not, by itself, enable the derivation of quantitative predictions. The first and third conditions fail. Yet no one would deny that Watson and Crick's model is one of the most important in the history of science. The definition has identified modelling with mathematical isomorphism and quantitative prediction, when some of the most illuminating models operate by capturing qualitative, spatial, and mechanistic features of a system.

definition: A hypothesis is scientifically meaningful if and only if (i) it is logically possible to specify an observation that would refute it, (ii) the hypothesis forbids at least one empirically observable state of affairs, and (iii) the proponent of the hypothesis would accept a negative result as grounds for abandoning the hypothesis.
conditions: logically possible to specify a refuting observation
hypothesis forbids at least one observable state of affairs
proponent would accept negative result as grounds for abandonment
counterexample: The hypothesis of continental drift, as proposed by Wegener in 1912, forbade certain observations and was in principle refutable. Yet Wegener and his supporters did not accept the many negative results that were urged against them — the absence of a plausible mechanism for the movement of continents, the geophysical calculations that seemed to rule it out. They continued to maintain the hypothesis in the face of what appeared to be decisive refutation. The third condition fails, yet the hypothesis was profoundly scientific and was ultimately vindicated by the theory of plate tectonics.
missing_condition: The definition requires that proponents accept negative results as conclusive, but the history of science shows that a hypothesis may be legitimately maintained in the face of apparent refutation if its proponents have reason to suspect that the negative results are themselves flawed or incomplete.
passage: It appears natural to hold that a hypothesis is scientifically meaningful if and only if it is logically possible to specify an observation that would refute it, the hypothesis forbids at least one empirically observable state of affairs, and the proponent of the hypothesis would accept a negative result as grounds for abandoning the hypothesis. This definition captures a sensible intuition: a genuine scientific hypothesis must put itself at risk, and the scientist must be willing to accept the verdict of experience. Yet consider the hypothesis of continental drift, as proposed by Alfred Wegener in 1912. Wegener held that the continents had once been joined in a single landmass and had since drifted apart. The hypothesis forbade certain observations — for example, it predicted a systematic pattern of geological and palaeontological correspondences between the coastlines of Africa and South America. It was in principle refutable. But Wegener and his supporters did not accept the negative results that were marshalled against them. The geophysicists of the day produced calculations showing that no known force could move a continent through the ocean floor; the mechanical objections seemed decisive. Yet Wegener persisted. He judged that the circumstantial evidence — the fit of the coastlines, the matching fossils, the geological correspondences — was too compelling to be overturned by the absence of a mechanism. The third condition fails. Yet the hypothesis was profoundly scientific, and it was ultimately vindicated when the theory of plate tectonics provided the mechanism that Wegener had lacked. The definition has demanded a readiness to capitulate to negative results that the actual practice of science does not always exhibit, for sometimes the scientist is right to maintain a hypothesis in the face of apparent refutation, trusting that the anomalies will eventually be resolved.

definition: A body of scientific practice constitutes a paradigm if and only if (i) it is explicitly articulated in a foundational text, (ii) it is accepted unanimously by the relevant scientific community, and (iii) it provides solutions to all the outstanding problems of the field.
conditions: explicitly articulated in a foundational text
accepted unanimously by the relevant scientific community
provides solutions to all outstanding problems
counterexample: Newtonian mechanics functioned as the paradigm of physics for over two centuries, yet it was never accepted unanimously — there were persistent dissenters, particularly among the Leibnizian and Cartesian traditions on the Continent. Nor did it provide solutions to all outstanding problems; the precession of the perihelion of Mercury was an unsolved anomaly throughout the nineteenth century. The second and third conditions fail, yet Newtonian mechanics is the most frequently cited example of a scientific paradigm.
missing_condition: The definition demands unanimity and completeness, but actual paradigms are characterised by broad but not universal acceptance and by the toleration of unsolved anomalies that are set aside as puzzles for future work.
passage: One might suppose that a body of scientific practice constitutes a paradigm if and only if it is explicitly articulated in a foundational text, it is accepted unanimously by the relevant scientific community, and it provides solutions to all the outstanding problems of the field. This definition captures the idea that a paradigm provides a comprehensive framework. Yet consider Newtonian mechanics, which is perhaps the most frequently cited example of a paradigm in discussions of the structure of science. Newton's Principia Mathematica of 1687 certainly served as a foundational text, and one might think that Newtonian mechanics satisfied the remaining conditions. But the history is more complicated. Newtonian mechanics was never accepted unanimously. On the Continent, the Cartesian and Leibnizian traditions offered competing accounts of the foundations of mechanics, and the disputes continued well into the eighteenth century. Even after the Newtonian framework became dominant, there were persistent questions about the intelligibility of action at a distance. Furthermore, Newtonian mechanics did not provide solutions to all outstanding problems. The precession of the perihelion of Mercury, for example, remained an unsolved anomaly throughout the entire nineteenth century. Astronomers noted the discrepancy, attempted various explanations, and ultimately left it as an unresolved puzzle. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has idealized the notion of a paradigm, demanding a unanimity and a completeness that actual paradigms never achieve. In practice, a paradigm commands broad assent while tolerating both dissent at the margins and anomalies that are set aside for future investigation.

definition: An explanation of a phenomenon is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) it renders the phenomenon predictable in advance, (ii) it identifies a cause temporally prior to the effect, and (iii) the cause is spatially contiguous with the effect.
conditions: renders the phenomenon predictable in advance
identifies a temporally prior cause
cause is spatially contiguous with the effect
counterexample: The explanation of the correlation between the spin measurements of entangled quantum particles involves no spatially contiguous cause. When two particles are prepared in an entangled state and separated by a great distance, a measurement on one particle is correlated with the outcome of a measurement on the other. The explanation provided by quantum mechanics does not identify any cause that travels through the intervening space. The third condition fails, yet quantum mechanics provides the accepted scientific explanation of these correlations.
missing_condition: The definition requires spatial contiguity between cause and effect, but quantum mechanics furnishes explanations involving non-local correlations that do not depend upon any spatially contiguous causal process.
passage: It might be proposed that an explanation of a phenomenon is scientifically adequate if and only if it renders the phenomenon predictable in advance, it identifies a cause temporally prior to the effect, and the cause is spatially contiguous with the effect. This definition encapsulates a venerable picture of causal explanation: one event produces another through a chain of spatially connected intermediate steps, and the explanation consists in tracing this chain. In much of classical physics, this picture obtains. The collision of billiard balls, the propagation of waves through a medium, the transmission of heat by conduction — in each case the cause is contiguous with the effect. Yet consider the explanation of the correlations observed between entangled quantum particles. Two particles are prepared in an entangled state in a laboratory and then separated by a vast distance. When a measurement is performed on one particle, the outcome of a measurement on the other is found to be correlated in a manner that cannot be accounted for by any common cause or by any signal travelling between the particles. The explanation furnished by quantum mechanics appeals to the entangled state of the pair, a state that is not localised in space and that does not involve any spatially contiguous causal process connecting the two measurement events. The third condition fails. Yet quantum mechanics provides the accepted and experimentally confirmed explanation of these correlations. The definition has insisted upon spatial contiguity as a requirement of scientific explanation, but the quantum world presents phenomena whose explanation must transcend the classical picture of causes propagating through contiguous regions of space.

definition: A procedure constitutes an experiment if and only if (i) it is designed to test a specific hypothesis, (ii) it is conducted under conditions that can be reproduced by other investigators, and (iii) its outcome is determined prior to execution — that is, the experimenter specifies in advance what result would confirm and what result would disconfirm the hypothesis.
conditions: designed to test a specific hypothesis
conducted under reproducible conditions
outcome criteria specified in advance
counterexample: The experiments of the early microscopists — Leeuwenhoek, Hooke, Malpighi — were exploratory rather than hypothesis-testing. Leeuwenhoek did not look through his microscope to test a specific hypothesis about the existence of microorganisms; he examined drops of water, scrapings of teeth, and infusions of pepper out of curiosity, and was astonished by what he found. His outcome criteria were not specified in advance because he had no prior conception of what he would observe. The first and third conditions fail, yet his investigations are rightly regarded as among the most important experiments in the history of biology.
missing_condition: The definition requires a prior hypothesis and pre-specified outcome criteria, but some of the most consequential experiments in science have been exploratory investigations undertaken without a specific hypothesis, in which the significance of the results was recognised only after the fact.
passage: A plausible definition holds that a procedure constitutes an experiment if and only if it is designed to test a specific hypothesis, it is conducted under conditions that can be reproduced by other investigators, and its outcome is determined prior to execution — that is, the experimenter specifies in advance what result would confirm and what result would disconfirm the hypothesis. This definition captures the ideal of the controlled test: one formulates a hypothesis, designs a procedure to test it, states in advance what would count as confirmation or disconfirmation, and carries out the procedure under reproducible conditions. Yet consider the experiments of the early microscopists. Antonie van Leeuwenhoek, in the late seventeenth century, examined drops of pond water, scrapings from his own teeth, and infusions of pepper under his meticulously crafted microscopes. He was not testing a specific hypothesis about the existence of microscopic organisms; he had no such hypothesis. He was exploring a world that no human eye had ever seen, driven by curiosity rather than by any theoretical expectation. He could not have specified in advance what would count as a confirming or disconfirming result, because he had no prior conception of the phenomena he was about to discover. The first and third conditions fail. Yet Leeuwenhoek's investigations are among the most important in the history of science; his discovery of bacteria, protozoa, and spermatozoa opened an entirely new domain of biological inquiry. The definition has modelled all experimentation on the hypothesis-testing ideal, when some of the most fruitful experiments have been voyages of discovery, undertaken without a specific hypothesis and yielding results that no one had anticipated.

definition: A scientific theory is acceptable if and only if (i) it is consistent with all known empirical data, (ii) it is simpler than its rivals, and (iii) it was arrived at by a rational process of hypothesis formation and testing.
conditions: consistent with all known empirical data
simpler than its rivals
arrived at by rational hypothesis formation and testing
counterexample: Kekulé's theory of the ring structure of benzene was famously inspired by a dream or reverie in which she saw a snake seizing its own tail. The theory proved extraordinarily fruitful — it was consistent with the known data and simpler than the alternatives — but it was not arrived at by a rational process of hypothesis formation and testing. The third condition fails, yet the theory was one of the most important breakthroughs in organic chemistry.
missing_condition: The definition demands a rational genesis, but the psychological process by which a theory is conceived — whether by dream, accident, or sudden intuition — is irrelevant to its scientific acceptability; what matters is how the theory performs when tested, not how it was discovered.
passage: One might hold that a scientific theory is acceptable if and only if it is consistent with all known empirical data, it is simpler than its rivals, and it was arrived at by a rational process of hypothesis formation and testing. This definition seems reasonable: we want our theories to fit the data, to be as simple as possible, and to have been developed through rigorous scientific reasoning. Yet consider the case of August Kekulé and the ring structure of benzene. In 1865, Kekulé proposed that the benzene molecule consists of six carbon atoms arranged in a ring, with alternating single and double bonds. This theory was consistent with the known chemical data, it was simpler than the various chain-structure alternatives that had been proposed, and it proved extraordinarily fruitful, opening up the entire field of aromatic chemistry. But it was not arrived at by a rational process of hypothesis formation and testing. Kekulé herself reported that the idea came to her in a dream or reverie, in which she saw atoms dancing and arranging themselves into chains, and then a chain forming a ring, like a snake seizing its own tail. The insight was an act of unconscious imagination, not of methodical reasoning. The third condition fails. Yet the theory was one of the most important in the history of organic chemistry. The definition has confused the context of discovery with the context of justification. The manner in which a theory is conceived — whether by careful reasoning, by happy accident, or by a dream — has no bearing upon its scientific acceptability; what matters is how the theory fares when confronted with the evidence, not how it first came to mind.

definition: An observation is scientifically reliable if and only if (i) the observer has no theoretical preconceptions about what they will observe, (ii) the observation is made under conditions that are free from any artificial interference, and (iii) the observer records their findings immediately and without interpretation.
conditions: observer has no theoretical preconceptions
observation made under conditions free from artificial interference
findings recorded immediately without interpretation
counterexample: When an experienced radiologist examines a chest X-ray, she brings to the image a vast body of theoretical and clinical preconceptions: she knows what a normal lung looks like, what tuberculosis and pneumonia produce on film, and what patterns suggest malignancy. Her theoretical training is not a source of error but the very condition of her ability to see what is there. She does not record her findings without interpretation; her report is itself an interpretation. The first and third conditions fail, yet the radiologist's observation is among the most reliable forms of clinical observation.
missing_condition: The definition treats theoretical preconceptions as a source of contamination, but in practice, trained observation depends upon theoretical knowledge; the observer's background understanding is what enables their to perceive and report features that an untrained observer would miss entirely.
passage: It might be held that an observation is scientifically reliable if and only if the observer has no theoretical preconceptions about what she will observe, the observation is made under conditions free from artificial interference, and the observer records her findings immediately and without interpretation. This definition embodies an ideal of pure observation — the scientist as a blank receptacle, passively registering the deliverances of the senses. Yet consider the practice of a trained radiologist examining a chest X-ray. She brings to the image an immense body of theoretical and clinical knowledge. She knows the anatomy of the thorax, the appearance of the normal lung on film, and the characteristic patterns produced by tuberculosis, pneumonia, emphysema, and malignant tumours. When she looks at the X-ray, her perception is thoroughly informed by this knowledge; she sees features that an untrained observer would not notice. Her theoretical preconceptions are not a source of contamination but the very condition of her competence. And she does not record her findings without interpretation; her report — that there is a shadow consistent with a tumour in the right upper lobe — is itself an act of interpretation, drawing upon years of training. The first and third conditions fail. Yet the radiologist's observation is among the most reliable forms of clinical observation in medicine. The definition has treated theoretical preconceptions as obstacles to reliable observation, when in many domains of science, trained and theoretically informed observation is vastly more reliable than naive, unschooled perception.

definition: A statement constitutes a law of nature if and only if (i) it describes a regularity that holds without exception throughout the universe, (ii) it is expressible as a mathematical equation, and (iii) it is derivable from more fundamental principles.
conditions: describes an exceptionless regularity throughout the universe
expressible as a mathematical equation
derivable from more fundamental principles
counterexample: The law of natural selection — that organisms possessing heritable traits better suited to their environment tend to leave more offspring — describes a regularity of profound generality, yet it is not expressible as a mathematical equation in any straightforward sense and it is not derivable from more fundamental principles of physics or chemistry. The second and third conditions fail, yet natural selection is universally regarded as a law of the biological sciences.
missing_condition: The definition requires mathematical expressibility and derivability from more fundamental principles, but some laws — particularly in the biological and social sciences — describe robust regularities that resist mathematical formalisation and stand as autonomous principles not reducible to the laws of physics.
passage: One might define a law of nature as a statement that describes a regularity holding without exception throughout the universe, that is expressible as a mathematical equation, and that is derivable from more fundamental principles. This definition fits the laws of physics well: Coulomb's law of electrostatic force is expressible as an equation, holds universally, and is derivable from Maxwell's equations. Yet consider the law of natural selection. This law states, in its most general form, that organisms possessing heritable traits better suited to their environment tend to leave more offspring than those less well suited, and that over time these traits become more prevalent in the population. It describes a regularity of extraordinary generality — it applies wherever there is heritable variation and differential reproduction, whether among bacteria, beetles, or birds. Yet it is not expressible as a mathematical equation in any straightforward sense. One can construct mathematical models of particular instances of selection, but the law itself is a qualitative principle. And it is not derivable from more fundamental principles of physics or chemistry; the concepts of fitness, adaptation, and selection are not reducible to the vocabulary of fundamental physics. The second and third conditions fail. Yet no biologist would deny that natural selection is a law. The definition has taken the mathematical, reductive character of the laws of physics as the standard for all scientific law, overlooking the autonomous laws of biology that describe regularities of a different kind.

definition: A scientific model is adequate if and only if (i) it accurately represents the target system in all relevant respects, (ii) it is internally consistent, and (iii) it can be applied to every instance of the type of system it purports to represent.
conditions: accurately represents target system in all relevant respects
internally consistent
applicable to every instance of the type of system
counterexample: The ideal gas model in thermodynamics treats gas molecules as point particles with no intermolecular forces. It does not accurately represent real gases in all relevant respects — real molecules have finite volume and do attract one another. Nor can it be applied to every instance; it fails badly for gases at high pressures and low temperatures. The first and third conditions fail, yet the ideal gas model is one of the most useful and widely employed models in physics and chemistry.
missing_condition: The definition demands comprehensive accuracy and universal applicability, but the utility of scientific models often derives precisely from their deliberate inaccuracies — their strategic omission of complicating factors — which makes them tractable and illuminating within a defined range of conditions.
passage: It is sometimes proposed that a scientific model is adequate if and only if it accurately represents the target system in all relevant respects, it is internally consistent, and it can be applied to every instance of the type of system it purports to represent. This definition expresses a natural aspiration: a model should be a faithful portrait of the world. Yet consider the ideal gas model, one of the most widely employed models in physics and chemistry. It treats gas molecules as dimensionless point particles that exert no forces on one another except during perfectly elastic collisions. This is not an accurate representation of real gases in all relevant respects: real molecules have finite volume, they attract one another at moderate distances and repel at very short distances, and their collisions are not perfectly elastic. The model cannot be applied to every instance of a gas; it fails badly for gases near their condensation point, at high pressures, or at very low temperatures, where intermolecular forces become significant. The first and third conditions fail. Yet the ideal gas model is indispensable. Within its range of applicability — moderate temperatures and low pressures — it provides remarkably accurate predictions, and its simplicity makes it a powerful tool for understanding the basic behaviour of gases. The definition has demanded a comprehensive fidelity that would render the very concept of a model superfluous, for if a model were accurate in all relevant respects and applicable to all instances, it would not be a model but a complete theory. The utility of models derives precisely from their strategic inaccuracies — their deliberate simplifications that make the intractable tractable.

definition: A measurement is objective if and only if (i) it is independent of the observer's theoretical commitments, (ii) it yields a result that is entirely determined by the state of the system being measured, and (iii) the act of measurement does not alter the system being measured.
conditions: independent of observer's theoretical commitments
result entirely determined by state of system
act of measurement does not alter system
counterexample: In quantum mechanics, the measurement of a particle's position necessarily disturbs the particle: the act of measurement collapses the wave function and alters the state of the system. The result is not entirely determined by the prior state of the system — quantum mechanics assigns only probabilities to measurement outcomes. The second and third conditions fail, yet quantum-mechanical measurements are accepted as objective by the entire physics community.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that measurement can be passive and deterministic, but quantum mechanics demonstrates that measurement may constitutively alter the system and yield results that are probabilistic rather than uniquely determined by the system's prior state.
passage: A plausible definition holds that a measurement is objective if and only if it is independent of the observer's theoretical commitments, it yields a result that is entirely determined by the state of the system being measured, and the act of measurement does not alter the system. This captures the classical picture of measurement: one observes a thermometer, and the mercury's height is determined entirely by the temperature of the substance; the act of reading the thermometer does not change the temperature. Yet consider measurement in quantum mechanics. When one measures the position of a subatomic particle, the act of measurement is not a passive registration of a pre-existing state. According to the standard interpretation, the particle does not possess a definite position prior to measurement; the wave function assigns only a probability distribution over possible positions. The measurement collapses the wave function, and the particle is found at a definite position, but this outcome is not uniquely determined by the prior state of the system — only the probabilities are determined. Moreover, the act of measurement fundamentally alters the system: after a position measurement, the wave function is changed, and a subsequent measurement of momentum will yield a different distribution of outcomes than it would have before the position measurement. The second and third conditions fail. Yet quantum-mechanical measurements are accepted as objective throughout physics; entire technologies depend upon them. The definition has assumed a classical picture of measurement in which the observer passively reads off a determinate fact, but quantum mechanics reveals that measurement may be constitutive rather than passive and probabilistic rather than deterministic.

definition: A theory possesses predictive power if and only if (i) it generates novel predictions that go beyond the data it was designed to explain, (ii) these predictions are quantitatively precise, and (iii) they concern observable phenomena that can be detected with existing technology.
conditions: generates novel predictions beyond the data it was designed to explain
predictions are quantitatively precise
predictions concern phenomena detectable with existing technology
counterexample: Einstein's general relativity predicted the existence of gravitational waves in 1916 — ripples in the fabric of spacetime produced by accelerating masses. This was a novel prediction that went beyond the data the theory was designed to explain. But the prediction was not quantitatively precise in any readily testable way — the expected amplitude of gravitational waves from astronomical sources was so small that Einstein himself doubted they could ever be detected. Moreover, the technology to detect gravitational waves did not exist until nearly a century later. The second and third conditions fail at the time the prediction was made, yet the prediction is among the most celebrated in the history of physics and was spectacularly confirmed in 2015.
missing_condition: The definition restricts predictive power to quantitatively precise predictions testable with existing technology, but some of the most impressive predictions in science are qualitative or imprecise at the time they are made and concern phenomena that only become detectable with technologies developed decades or centuries later.
passage: One might hold that a theory possesses predictive power if and only if it generates novel predictions that go beyond the data it was designed to explain, these predictions are quantitatively precise, and they concern observable phenomena that can be detected with existing technology. This captures the ideal case: a theory predicts a specific numerical value for a quantity that has not yet been measured, and the measurement is then carried out and confirms the prediction. Yet consider Einstein's prediction of gravitational waves. In 1916, Einstein showed that his general theory of relativity implied the existence of ripples in the fabric of spacetime, produced by the acceleration of massive bodies. This was a novel prediction that went far beyond the phenomena — the precession of Mercury's perihelion, the bending of starlight — that the theory had been designed to explain. But the prediction was not quantitatively precise in any readily testable way. The amplitude of gravitational waves from astronomical sources was calculated to be extraordinarily small, and Einstein himself was uncertain whether such waves were physically real or merely artefacts of the mathematical formalism. Moreover, the technology to detect gravitational waves did not exist in 1916, nor did it exist for many decades thereafter. It was not until 2015 — nearly a century later — that the LIGO detectors achieved the sensitivity necessary to observe gravitational waves directly. The second and third conditions fail at the time the prediction was made. Yet the prediction of gravitational waves is rightly regarded as one of the most remarkable in the history of science. The definition has demanded an immediacy of testability that some of the greatest predictions do not possess, overlooking the profound predictive achievements of theories whose implications outrun the technological capacities of their era.

definition: A procedure P constitutes a scientific experiment if and only if (i) P involves the deliberate manipulation of a single independent variable, (ii) all other variables are held constant, and (iii) the outcome is measured by quantitative instruments yielding numerical data.
conditions: P involves the deliberate manipulation of a single independent variable
all other variables are held constant
the outcome is measured by quantitative instruments yielding numerical data
counterexample: Consider Darwin's experiments on the pollination of orchids, in which she observed the interactions between insects and flowers under natural conditions, manipulating several variables simultaneously — the presence of different insect species, the arrangement of the flowers, the time of day — and recorded her results largely through qualitative descriptions and drawings rather than through quantitative measurement. No single variable was isolated, other variables were not held constant, and the data were not numerical in the conventional sense. Yet these investigations are universally regarded as experiments of the highest scientific value.
missing_condition: The definition imposes the idealised structure of the controlled laboratory experiment upon all scientific experimentation, but experiments may involve the simultaneous variation of multiple factors, may be conducted in natural rather than controlled settings, and may yield qualitative rather than quantitative data.
passage: One might propose that a procedure constitutes a scientific experiment if and only if it involves the deliberate manipulation of a single independent variable, all other variables are held constant, and the outcome is measured by quantitative instruments yielding numerical data. This definition captures the idealised structure of the controlled laboratory experiment. One thinks of the chemist who varies the concentration of a reagent whilst holding temperature, pressure, and volume constant, and who records the result as a numerical reading on a spectrophotometer. The definition has a satisfying precision. Yet one must consider Darwin's celebrated experiments on the pollination of orchids. Darwin observed the interactions between insects and flowers, sometimes in the field and sometimes in her greenhouse, manipulating several factors simultaneously — the presence of different insect species, the arrangement of the floral parts, the orientation of the plants. She did not isolate a single independent variable; the phenomena she studied were too complex and too deeply embedded in natural conditions to permit such isolation. She did not hold all other variables constant; the wind blew, the light changed, and the insects behaved unpredictably. And her results were recorded largely through qualitative descriptions, detailed drawings, and narrative accounts rather than through numerical measurement. The three conditions of the definition fail. Yet Darwin's orchid experiments are universally acknowledged as experiments of the highest scientific merit, experiments that transformed our understanding of plant biology. The definition has imposed the structure of the idealised laboratory trial upon all scientific experimentation, when in truth some of the most illuminating experiments in the history of science have been conducted under natural conditions, with multiple variables in play, and with qualitative observation as the primary mode of data collection.

definition: A procedure P constitutes a genuine scientific measurement if and only if (i) P assigns a numerical value to a property of a physical system, (ii) P is repeatable — that is, the same procedure applied to the same system yields the same value, and (iii) the numerical value assigned is independent of the observer performing the measurement.
conditions: P assigns a numerical value to a property of a physical system
P is repeatable — the same procedure applied to the same system yields the same value
the numerical value assigned is independent of the observer
counterexample: Consider the measurement of the brightness of a star by a nineteenth-century astronomer using the method of visual estimation. The astronomer compares the star's brightness with that of reference stars and assigns it a magnitude. This procedure assigns a numerical value to a physical property and is certainly a measurement. Yet it is not perfectly repeatable: the same astronomer may assign slightly different magnitudes on different nights, depending upon atmospheric conditions and the state of his eyesight. Nor is the value wholly independent of the observer; different astronomers, even well-trained ones, may disagree by a fraction of a magnitude. The second and third conditions fail, yet stellar magnitude estimation is recognised as a legitimate and historically important form of scientific measurement.
missing_condition: The definition requires perfect repeatability and observer-independence, but scientific measurement is often subject to irreducible variability and observer-dependent factors. What is wanted is a notion of measurement that allows for degrees of precision and acknowledges the role of the observer without excluding imperfect but genuine measurements.
passage: One might hold that a procedure constitutes a genuine scientific measurement if and only if it assigns a numerical value to a property of a physical system, it is repeatable in the sense that the same procedure applied to the same system yields the same value, and the numerical value is independent of the observer. The definition captures a certain ideal of measurement. When one measures the length of a rod with a calibrated ruler, one expects the same result each time, regardless of who performs the measurement. Yet one must consider the measurement of stellar brightness by the method of visual estimation, as practised by astronomers throughout the nineteenth century. The astronomer stands at the eyepiece of a telescope, compares the brightness of a star with that of a set of reference stars of known magnitude, and assigns the star a magnitude. This is a measurement: it assigns a numerical value — a magnitude — to a physical property — brightness — of a physical system — the star. Yet it is not perfectly repeatable. The same astronomer may assign a slightly different magnitude on different nights, owing to variations in atmospheric transparency, the state of his dark adaptation, and the fatigue of his eye. Nor is the value independent of the observer: two well-trained astronomers observing the same star on the same night may disagree by a fraction of a magnitude, and systematic differences between observers — personal equations, as they were called — were a recognised phenomenon. The second and third conditions fail. Yet stellar magnitude estimation was for more than a century the principal method by which the brightness of stars was recorded, and the catalogues produced by this method remain of scientific value. The definition has confused the ideal of measurement with the reality of measurement as it is actually practised, demanding a degree of repeatability and observer-independence that many legitimate measurements do not attain.

definition: A scientific theory T yields a genuine prediction if and only if (i) T logically entails a statement about a specific future observable event, (ii) the event has not yet been observed at the time the prediction is made, and (iii) the prediction specifies the time and place at which the event will occur.
conditions: T logically entails a statement about a specific future observable event
the event has not yet been observed at the time the prediction is made
the prediction specifies the time and place at which the event will occur
counterexample: Consider the prediction derived from the theory of evolution by natural selection that transitional fossils between fish and amphibians should exist. When this prediction was first articulated, no such fossil had been observed, and the prediction was subsequently confirmed by the discovery of specimens such as Tiktaalik. Yet the prediction did not specify a time and place at which the fossil would be found, nor did it concern a future event; it concerned the existence of something already in the ground, waiting to be discovered. The first and third conditions fail, yet this is a paradigmatic case of a successful scientific prediction.
missing_condition: The definition restricts genuine prediction to temporally specific forecasts of future events, but scientific predictions may concern the existence of as-yet-unobserved entities or phenomena without specifying when or where they will be found. What is wanted is a broader notion of empirical consequence rather than temporal forecast.
passage: One might propose that a scientific theory yields a genuine prediction if and only if it logically entails a statement about a specific future observable event, the event has not yet been observed at the time the prediction is made, and the prediction specifies the time and place at which the event will occur. This definition captures the most dramatic cases of scientific prediction: the return of Halley's comet at the predicted time, the appearance of a planet at the predicted position in the sky. Such predictions are rightly celebrated as triumphs of theoretical science. Yet one must consider a rather different kind of prediction. The theory of evolution by natural selection, as it was developed in the latter half of the nineteenth century, entailed that there should exist transitional forms between major groups of organisms. In particular, it predicted that transitional fossils linking fish and amphibians should exist somewhere in the geological record. This prediction was not a forecast of a future event; the fossils, if they existed, were already in the ground. Nor did it specify a time and place at which they would be found; it merely asserted that such fossils ought to exist. Yet the prediction was subsequently confirmed — spectacularly so, in the discovery of specimens such as Tiktaalik in the Canadian Arctic. The confirmation of this prediction is regarded as one of the great successes of evolutionary theory. The first and third conditions fail. The definition has taken the structure of temporal forecasting — the prediction of specific events at specific times and places — and treated it as necessary for all scientific prediction, when in truth some of the most important predictions in science concern the existence of entities or phenomena that have not yet been observed, without specifying when or where observation will occur.

definition: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) every entity in the domain is assigned to exactly one class, (ii) the classes are defined by necessary and sufficient conditions, and (iii) the boundaries between classes are sharp — that is, there are no borderline cases.
conditions: every entity in the domain is assigned to exactly one class
the classes are defined by necessary and sufficient conditions
the boundaries between classes are sharp — no borderline cases exist
counterexample: Consider the classification of biological species. Biologists regularly encounter organisms that resist assignment to exactly one species — hybrids, ring species, and populations in the early stages of speciation. The boundaries between species are not always sharp; there are genuine borderline cases. And the concept of a species is notoriously resistant to definition by necessary and sufficient conditions. Yet the biological species classification is among the most important and productive systems of classification in all of science.
missing_condition: The definition demands sharp boundaries and definition by necessary and sufficient conditions, but scientifically productive classifications may involve vague boundaries, family resemblance, and borderline cases. What is wanted is an account of classificatory adequacy that accommodates the graduated character of natural variation.
passage: Suppose one holds that a system of classification is scientifically adequate if and only if every entity in the domain is assigned to exactly one class, the classes are defined by necessary and sufficient conditions, and the boundaries between classes are sharp. The definition reflects a logical ideal of classification: each thing has its place, that place is uniquely determined, and the criteria for assignment are perfectly precise. In certain domains this ideal is achievable — the classification of chemical elements by atomic number, for example, assigns each element to exactly one place in the periodic table, with sharp boundaries and no borderline cases. Yet one must consider the classification of biological species. Biologists have classified organisms into species for centuries, and the resulting taxonomy is one of the great intellectual achievements of the natural sciences. Yet the classification resists the conditions of the definition at every turn. Organisms frequently resist assignment to exactly one species: hybrids between closely related species are common, ring species present continuous gradations, and populations in the early stages of speciation may be neither clearly one species nor clearly two. The boundaries between species are not sharp; there are genuine borderline cases that defy unambiguous assignment. And the concept of a species has proved notoriously resistant to definition by necessary and sufficient conditions; biologists have proposed dozens of species concepts, none of which captures all and only the groups that competent taxonomists recognise as species. The three conditions fail. The definition has imposed a logical ideal of precision upon scientific classification that the most important classificatory system in biology does not meet and perhaps cannot meet, given the graduated and branching character of biological variation.

definition: A representation M constitutes a scientific model if and only if (i) M is isomorphic to the system it represents — that is, there is a one-to-one correspondence between the elements of M and the elements of the system, (ii) M preserves all the structural relations obtaining among the elements of the system, and (iii) M makes no idealisations or simplifications.
conditions: M is isomorphic to the system it represents
M preserves all structural relations among the elements of the system
M makes no idealisations or simplifications
counterexample: Consider the ideal gas model in thermodynamics. This model represents gas molecules as point particles with no intermolecular forces, moving in perfectly random directions. It is not isomorphic to any real gas; real gas molecules have volume and do exert forces upon one another. It does not preserve all the structural relations of the actual system; it omits the very features — molecular volume and intermolecular attraction — that distinguish real gases from ideal ones. And it makes extensive idealisations. Yet the ideal gas model is one of the most successful and widely used models in the history of physical science.
missing_condition: The definition requires exact isomorphism and the absence of idealisation, but the most useful scientific models are precisely those that abstract away from certain features of reality in order to render complex systems tractable. What is wanted is an account that recognises idealisation and selective representation as virtues rather than defects of scientific modelling.
passage: One might define a scientific model as follows: a representation constitutes a scientific model if and only if it is isomorphic to the system it represents, it preserves all the structural relations obtaining among the elements of the system, and it makes no idealisations or simplifications. The definition has a certain rigour: a perfect model would be a perfect replica of reality, capturing every feature and every relation without distortion. Yet one must consider the ideal gas model in thermodynamics, one of the most successful models in the history of physical science. This model represents gas molecules as dimensionless point particles possessing mass but no spatial extension, exerting no intermolecular forces upon one another, and moving in perfectly random directions within a container. The model is not isomorphic to any real gas. Real gas molecules have volume; they occupy space and cannot overlap. Real gas molecules do exert forces upon one another — van der Waals forces, for instance — and these forces become significant at high pressures and low temperatures. The model does not preserve all the structural relations of the actual system; it omits precisely those features that distinguish real gases from ideal ones. And it makes extensive idealisations: the assumption of zero molecular volume, the neglect of intermolecular forces, the postulation of perfectly elastic collisions. Every condition of the definition fails. Yet the ideal gas model is extraordinarily useful. It yields predictions of remarkable accuracy under a wide range of conditions, and it provides the conceptual foundation upon which more refined models are built. The definition has confused the ideal of a perfect replica with the nature of scientific modelling, which proceeds precisely by abstracting away from the full complexity of reality.

definition: A theory T is scientific if and only if (i) T makes at least one prediction that could, in principle, be shown to be false by observation, (ii) the practitioners of T are willing to abandon T if a falsifying observation is produced, and (iii) no auxiliary hypotheses are invoked to protect T from falsification.
conditions: T makes at least one prediction that could be shown false by observation
the practitioners of T are willing to abandon T if a falsifying observation is produced
no auxiliary hypotheses are invoked to protect T from falsification
counterexample: Consider Newtonian mechanics in the mid-nineteenth century. When the observed orbit of Uranus deviated from the predictions of Newton's theory, practitioners did not abandon the theory. Instead, they invoked an auxiliary hypothesis — the existence of an as-yet-undiscovered planet — to explain the discrepancy. The third condition was violated. Yet the strategy was vindicated by the subsequent discovery of Neptune, and Newtonian mechanics remained the paradigm of scientific theorising. The invocation of auxiliary hypotheses to protect a theory from apparent falsification is not, it seems, invariably a mark of unscientific conduct.
