missing_condition: The definition prohibits the use of auxiliary hypotheses to protect a theory, but legitimate scientific practice regularly employs auxiliary hypotheses to accommodate anomalous observations. What is wanted is a distinction between ad hoc modifications that merely save appearances and well-motivated auxiliary hypotheses that lead to new discoveries.
passage: Suppose one holds that a theory is scientific if and only if it makes at least one prediction that could be shown false by observation, its practitioners are willing to abandon it if a falsifying observation is produced, and no auxiliary hypotheses are invoked to protect it from falsification. The definition captures something important about scientific rationality: a theory that cannot be tested, or whose adherents refuse to accept any evidence against it, forfeits its claim to scientific status. Yet one must consider the case of Newtonian mechanics and the planet Uranus. By the early nineteenth century, Newton's theory of universal gravitation was the most successful theory in the history of science. It predicted the motions of the planets with extraordinary accuracy. But the observed orbit of Uranus refused to conform to the Newtonian prediction; the planet deviated from its calculated path in ways that the theory, applied to the known bodies of the solar system, could not account for. The practitioners of Newtonian mechanics did not, however, abandon the theory. Instead, they invoked an auxiliary hypothesis: perhaps there existed an as-yet-undiscovered planet whose gravitational influence was perturbing the orbit of Uranus. This auxiliary hypothesis was used to protect Newtonian mechanics from falsification. The third condition of the definition was violated. Yet the strategy was brilliantly successful: the hypothesised planet was sought and found — it was Neptune — and the discovery was hailed as one of the greatest triumphs of Newtonian theory. The definition has treated the invocation of auxiliary hypotheses as invariably unscientific, when in truth it is a standard and often productive feature of scientific practice.

definition: An account A is a scientific explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) A subsumes P under a general law — that is, A shows that P is an instance of a lawful regularity, (ii) A enables the prediction of P — that is, given A, one could have predicted P before it occurred, and (iii) A makes essential reference to the antecedent conditions that, together with the law, entail the occurrence of P.
conditions: A subsumes P under a general law
A enables the prediction of P
A makes essential reference to the antecedent conditions that, together with the law, entail the occurrence of P
counterexample: Consider the explanation of the length of a shadow cast by a flagpole. One may subsume the shadow's length under the laws of optics: given the height of the pole, the angle of elevation of the sun, and the rectilinear propagation of light, one can deduce and predict the length of the shadow. This is a perfectly good explanation. But now consider the reverse derivation: given the length of the shadow, the angle of the sun, and the same laws, one can deduce the height of the flagpole. The derivation satisfies all three conditions — it subsumes the height under a law, it enables prediction, and it cites antecedent conditions. Yet the length of the shadow does not explain the height of the pole. The second derivation is not an explanation at all. The conditions are satisfied but no explanation is given.
missing_condition: The definition identifies explanation with subsumption under law and predictive power, but some derivations that meet these criteria run in the wrong direction — they derive the explanandum from factors that do not stand in the right explanatory relation to it. What is wanted is a condition of explanatory relevance or asymmetry that distinguishes genuine explanations from mere derivations.
passage: One might propose that an account is a scientific explanation of a phenomenon if and only if it subsumes the phenomenon under a general law, enables the prediction of the phenomenon, and makes essential reference to the antecedent conditions that, together with the law, entail the phenomenon's occurrence. The definition has been enormously influential, for it captures a central feature of scientific practice: the physicist explains the fall of a stone by citing the law of gravitation and the antecedent conditions — the stone's mass, its height, the absence of impediments — and from these the fall can be predicted. The explanation consists in showing that the phenomenon is a particular instance of a general lawful pattern. Yet one must consider the case of the flagpole and its shadow. The shadow's length can be explained by citing the laws of optics — specifically, the rectilinear propagation of light — together with the antecedent conditions: the height of the flagpole, the angle of the sun above the horizon, and the absence of obstructions. From these, the shadow's length can be deduced and predicted. This is a genuine explanation. But now consider the reverse derivation. Given the length of the shadow, the angle of the sun, and the same laws of optics, one can deduce the height of the flagpole. The derivation subsumes the height under a general law, it makes reference to antecedent conditions — the shadow's length and the sun's angle — and it enables one to predict the height, in the sense that the height follows deductively from the premises. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the length of the shadow does not explain the height of the flagpole. The flagpole is fifty feet tall because it was manufactured and erected to that height, not because its shadow happens to be of a certain length. The derivation from the shadow runs in the wrong direction; it is a calculation, not an explanation. The definition has identified explanation with deductive subsumption under law and predictive power, without attending to the asymmetry that is essential to explanation — the fact that causes explain their effects but effects do not explain their causes.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is a deductively closed system — that is, every logical consequence of the propositions in T is also a member of T, (ii) T includes at least one general law, and (iii) T has been confirmed by observational evidence — that is, predictions derived from T have been found to agree with observation.
conditions: T is a deductively closed system
T includes at least one general law
T has been confirmed by observational evidence
counterexample: Consider the kinetic theory of gases at the time of its first proposal. The theory held that a gas consists of a vast number of minute particles in rapid random motion, and that the pressure of a gas upon the walls of its container is the result of the aggregate impact of these particles. The theory included general laws — the relation of pressure to temperature and volume. But at the time of its proposal, the theory had not been confirmed by observation in any direct sense: no one had observed the particles or their motions, and the agreement of the theory with the known gas laws was regarded by many as an interesting coincidence rather than a confirmation. The third condition fails, at least at the stage of initial proposal, yet the kinetic theory was, at that stage, a theory.
missing_condition: The definition requires observational confirmation, but a body of propositions may constitute a genuine theory before it has been confirmed and even whilst it remains speculative. What is wanted is an account that distinguishes theories from other bodies of propositions without requiring that they have already been tested.
passage: One might hold that a body of propositions constitutes a scientific theory if and only if it is a deductively closed system, includes at least one general law, and has been confirmed by observational evidence. The definition captures the mature state of many successful theories. Newtonian mechanics is a deductively organised system that includes the laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation, and it has been confirmed by a vast body of observational evidence — the motions of the planets, the trajectories of projectiles, the behaviour of pendulums. One might suppose that these features are jointly sufficient and necessary for a body of propositions to count as a theory. Yet one must consider the kinetic theory of gases at the time of its first articulation. The theory proposed that a gas is composed of an immense number of minute material particles in rapid, random motion, that the particles collide with one another and with the walls of the containing vessel, and that the macroscopic properties of the gas — its pressure, its temperature, its volume — are the statistical outcomes of these microscopic motions. The theory was deductively organised and included general laws relating the macroscopic and the microscopic. But at the time of its proposal, it had not been confirmed by observational evidence in any straightforward sense. No one had observed the particles; no microscope could reveal them. The agreement of the theory's predictions with the empirically known gas laws — Boyle's law, Charles's law — was regarded by many natural philosophers not as a confirmation but as a suggestive coincidence that might well prove misleading. The theory was, at that stage, a bold speculation, an unconfirmed hypothesis. Yet it was a theory. The scientific community recognised it as such, debated its merits as a theory, and subjected it to theoretical criticism long before the decisive observations were available. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has confused a theory in its mature, confirmed state with a theory as such, overlooking the fact that a body of propositions may be a genuine theory — coherent, law-like, explanatory — at a stage when it remains unconfirmed and even widely doubted.

definition: A report R is an observational report if and only if (i) R describes a state of affairs that is directly perceived by the senses without the aid of instruments, (ii) R is expressed in terms that do not presuppose any theoretical framework, and (iii) R can be verified by any competent observer who is present at the appropriate time and place.
conditions: R describes a state of affairs directly perceived by the senses without instruments
R is expressed in terms that do not presuppose any theoretical framework
R can be verified by any competent observer present at the appropriate time and place
counterexample: Consider the astronomer's report that a particular star has a parallax of 0.76 arc seconds. This is, in the practice of astronomy, an observational report — the raw datum from which the distance to the star is calculated. Yet it is not directly perceived by the unaided senses; it is obtained through a telescope and a micrometer. It is expressed in terms — 'parallax,' 'arc seconds' — that presuppose a theoretical framework of positional astronomy. And it cannot be verified by any competent observer; it requires specialised instruments and training. All three conditions fail, yet the report is observational.
missing_condition: The definition restricts observation to the unaided senses and theory-free language, but scientific observation typically involves instruments and is couched in theoretical terms. What is wanted is an account that acknowledges the role of instruments and theory in scientific observation.
passage: Suppose one holds that a report is an observational report if and only if it describes a state of affairs directly perceived by the senses without the aid of instruments, it is expressed in terms that do not presuppose any theoretical framework, and it can be verified by any competent observer present at the appropriate time and place. The definition embodies a picture of observation as the unmediated encounter of the senses with the world, expressed in language that is innocent of theory. The report 'the litmus paper has turned red' might be thought to satisfy these conditions: the change of colour is perceived by the naked eye, the language is non-technical, and any person present could verify the colour change. Yet one must consider the astronomer's report that a certain star has a parallax of 0.76 arc seconds. In the practice of observational astronomy, this report functions as a basic observational datum; it is the starting-point from which the distance to the star is inferred. It is recorded in the observatory logbook alongside other observations and is treated as the deliverances of experience rather than of theory. But it satisfies none of the conditions of the definition. The parallax is not directly perceived by the unaided senses; it is measured by means of a telescope, a clock-driven mounting, and a filar micrometer, and it is extracted from a series of photographic plates by a process of careful measurement and calculation. The terms in which the report is expressed — 'parallax,' 'arc seconds' — are theoretical terms that presuppose the framework of positional astronomy; a person ignorant of that framework could not understand the report, let alone make it. And the report cannot be verified by any competent observer; it requires not only presence at the observatory but specialised instruments and years of training. All three conditions fail, yet the report is observational by the standards of the science. The definition has taken the features of the simplest perceptual reports — those made with the naked eye and expressed in everyday language — and elevated them to necessary conditions of observational reports as such, when in truth scientific observation is almost always mediated by instruments and framed in theoretical terms.

definition: A procedure M is a measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M assigns a numerical value to Q, (ii) M is repeatable — that is, the same procedure applied to the same object under the same conditions yields the same numerical value, and (iii) M is independent of the observer — that is, any competent person who performs M obtains the same result.
conditions: M assigns a numerical value to Q
M is repeatable — the same procedure yields the same value under the same conditions
M is independent of the observer — any competent person obtains the same result
counterexample: Consider the measurement of the hardness of a mineral by Mohs's scale. The geologist scratches one mineral against another and assigns a number from one to ten according to which mineral scratches which. This is a measurement — universally recognised as such in mineralogy. But it is not perfectly repeatable: the result depends upon the force applied, the angle of the scratch, and the condition of the specimens, so that the same procedure applied under nominally identical conditions may yield slightly different results. Moreover, it is not wholly independent of the observer; the judgement whether a scratch has been made is, in borderline cases, a matter of the observer's eyesight and experience. The second and third conditions fail in their strict form, yet the procedure is a measurement.
missing_condition: The definition requires perfect repeatability and observer-independence, but many accepted measurements involve an element of judgement and yield results that vary slightly from trial to trial. What is wanted is an account that allows for the imperfect repeatability and observer-dependence that characterise much actual measurement.
passage: One might propose that a procedure is a measurement of a quantity if and only if it assigns a numerical value to the quantity, it is repeatable, and it is independent of the observer. The definition captures an ideal that is central to the scientific enterprise: the measured value should be determined by the object and the procedure, not by the idiosyncrasies of the person who performs the measurement. When two physicists measure the boiling point of water with the same thermometer under the same conditions, they obtain the same value; the measurement is repeatable and observer-independent. Yet one must consider the measurement of mineral hardness by Mohs's scale. The geologist takes two mineral specimens and scratches one against the other. If the first scratches the second, the first is harder; the hardness of each mineral is assigned a number from one to ten according to its position in a standard series — talc at one, diamond at ten. This procedure is a measurement; it is universally recognised as such in the earth sciences, and Mohs numbers appear in every mineralogical reference work. But the procedure is not perfectly repeatable. The result depends upon the force with which the scratch is applied, the angle at which the specimens are held, the freshness of the surfaces, and the condition of the edges. Two trials under nominally identical conditions may yield different results, particularly when the minerals are close in hardness. Nor is the procedure wholly independent of the observer. Whether a scratch has been made is, in borderline cases, a matter of judgement: one observer may see a faint mark where another sees none, and the assessment depends upon the acuity of the observer's eyesight and the extent of his experience. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has taken the features of the most precise and mechanised measurements — those in which human judgement plays no role — and imposed them as necessary conditions of all measurement, when in truth a great deal of scientific measurement involves an ineliminable element of judgement and an imperfect degree of repeatability.

definition: A proposition E explains a phenomenon P if and only if (i) E is a general law or is deducible from general laws, (ii) P is logically deducible from E together with a statement of antecedent conditions, and (iii) E has been independently confirmed by observation or experiment.
conditions: E is a general law or is deducible from general laws
P is logically deducible from E together with a statement of antecedent conditions
E has been independently confirmed by observation or experiment
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the period of a simple pendulum is derivable from the law of gravitation together with a statement of the pendulum's length. The law of gravitation is a general law, the period is logically deducible from it given the length, and the law has been independently confirmed. Yet if one asks why the pendulum has a certain period and is offered as explanation the conjunction of Newton's law with the irrelevant but confirmed proposition that all ravens are black, the conjunction still satisfies the three conditions. All three conditions are met, yet one would not say that the colour of ravens explains the pendulum's behaviour.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the explanatory proposition be relevant to the phenomenon in question; a relevance or causal-connection condition is needed to exclude the parasitic attachment of confirmed but irrelevant generalisations.
passage: One might propose that a proposition E explains a phenomenon P if and only if E is a general law or is deducible from general laws, P is logically deducible from E together with a statement of antecedent conditions, and E has been independently confirmed by observation or experiment. This definition captures much of what we ordinarily expect of scientific explanation: generality, deductive connection, and empirical support. The requirement that E be a law, or derivable from laws, ensures that explanation is not merely narrative or anecdotal but rests upon regularities of nature. The deductive condition guarantees that the explanation genuinely entails the phenomenon, so that the occurrence of P is rendered, in some sense, necessary given E and the initial conditions. And the confirmational requirement excludes mere speculation from the ranks of explanation. Yet one might observe that the definition is, in a certain respect, too permissive. Suppose one takes the law of gravitation — a proposition amply confirmed — and conjoins to it the independently confirmed generalisation that all emeralds examined before a certain date have been green. The conjunction remains a confirmed generalisation from which the pendulum's period is deducible. But it appears deeply unsatisfying to say that the colour of emeralds, even when packaged within a confirmed conjunction, contributes to the explanation of a pendulum's period. The difficulty is that the definition contains no requirement that E stand in a relation of relevance to P. Any confirmed law, however remote from the phenomenon, may be conjoined to a genuine explanans without violating the three conditions. What is wanted, it seems, is a further condition requiring that the law invoked bear some appropriate connection — perhaps causal, perhaps structural — to the phenomenon it purports to explain.

definition: A generalisation G is a scientific law if and only if (i) G is a universally quantified conditional of the form 'For all x, if Fx then Gx', (ii) G has no known exceptions — that is, no observed instance of Fx conjoined with not-Gx, and (iii) G has been derived from, or is consistent with, an accepted body of theory.
conditions: G is a universally quantified conditional of the form 'For all x, if Fx then Gx'
G has no known exceptions
G has been derived from or is consistent with an accepted body of theory
counterexample: Consider the generalisation that all the coins in a particular man's pocket on a Tuesday afternoon are copper. This generalisation is universally quantified in the requisite form, it has no known exceptions — every coin inspected is indeed copper — and it is perfectly consistent with accepted physical and metallurgical theory. Yet one would hesitate to call it a scientific law. It is merely an accidental truth about a particular collection of coins, and its truth would not support the claim that any coin placed in that pocket must be copper.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that a scientific law support counterfactual conditionals — that is, that it sustain claims about what would be the case under conditions that have not been realised. Accidental generalisations satisfy the stated conditions but lack this modal or counterfactual force.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a generalisation qualifies as a scientific law if and only if it takes the form of a universally quantified conditional, it has no known exceptions, and it has been derived from or is consistent with an accepted body of theory. Such a definition appears to capture the essential features of the paradigmatic laws of physics: they are universal in scope, they have survived every attempt at refutation, and they are embedded within a larger theoretical framework. The requirement of theoretical embedding is intended to exclude merely empirical summaries that happen to have escaped falsification, for it insists that the generalisation occupy a place within a coherent system of propositions. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken jointly, remain insufficient to distinguish laws from certain accidental truths. Consider the proposition that every coin presently residing in a particular gentleman's waistcoat pocket is made of copper. This generalisation is of the requisite logical form, it has no known exceptions, and nothing in accepted metallurgical or economic theory contradicts it. Nevertheless, it would be absurd to call it a law. We should hesitate to say that if a silver sixpence were placed in the pocket it would thereby become copper, yet a genuine law would sustain precisely such counterfactual reasoning. The definition, it appears, has failed to capture the modal or counterfactual dimension of lawhood — the feature by which a law tells us not merely what has happened, but what must happen under specified conditions.

definition: A set of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is internally consistent — no proposition in T contradicts any other, (ii) T entails a range of observable predictions that have been verified, and (iii) T is expressible in a formal or semi-formal language with well-defined terms.
conditions: T is internally consistent
T entails a range of observable predictions that have been verified
T is expressible in a formal or semi-formal language with well-defined terms
counterexample: Consider a conjunction of two unrelated but internally consistent and empirically successful theories — say, the kinetic theory of gases and the germ theory of disease — arbitrarily merged into a single set of propositions. The combined set is internally consistent, it entails a vast range of verified observable predictions drawn from both thermodynamics and medicine, and each component is expressible in well-defined formal terms. Yet we should hesitate to call this conjunction a single scientific theory. It lacks the unity and coherence that distinguishes a theory from a mere collection of successful propositions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the propositions of the theory be unified by a common subject-matter or explanatory principle. Without a condition of thematic or explanatory unity, any arbitrary conjunction of successful theories would count as a single theory.
passage: One might define a scientific theory as a set of propositions that is internally consistent, that entails a range of observable predictions which have been verified, and that is expressible in a formal or semi-formal language with well-defined terms. This proposal has evident virtues. Internal consistency ensures that the theory does not undermine itself by asserting contradictory claims. The requirement that verified predictions be derivable from the theory captures the empiricist intuition that a theory must answer to experience. And the demand for formal expressibility guards against vagueness and equivocation, which have so often been the refuge of pseudo-scientific speculation. Yet one might observe that these conditions, though individually necessary or at least highly desirable, are not jointly sufficient to characterise what we ordinarily mean by a theory. Consider what happens if one takes two quite unrelated bodies of successful science — the kinetic theory of gases and the germ theory of disease, let us say — and forms their conjunction into a single set of propositions. The resulting set is internally consistent, since neither component contradicts the other. It entails a very large range of verified predictions, drawn from thermodynamics on the one hand and from medicine on the other. And both components are expressible in well-defined formal terms. Yet it would be strange, and indeed misleading, to call the conjunction a single scientific theory. What is missing is the unity that we expect of a theory — the sense that its propositions hang together, that they address a common domain and illuminate it by means of a common set of principles. The definition, it appears, requires a further condition concerning the systematic or explanatory unity of the propositions.

definition: A report R is an observation report if and only if (i) R describes a state of affairs directly apprehended through the senses, (ii) R is expressed in terms that any competent observer could understand without specialised training, and (iii) R could in principle be confirmed or disconfirmed by any suitably placed observer.
conditions: R describes a state of affairs directly apprehended through the senses
R is expressed in terms that any competent observer could understand without specialised training
R could in principle be confirmed or disconfirmed by any suitably placed observer
counterexample: Consider the report 'The litmus paper has turned red.' The state of affairs — the colour change — is directly apprehended through the senses; a normally sighted person needs no specialised training to understand the claim that a piece of paper is red; and any observer present in the laboratory could confirm or disconfirm the report. Yet the significance of the report — that the solution is acidic — is inseparable from the theoretical context in which the observation is made, and the very identification of the paper as litmus paper already presupposes a body of chemical theory. The report satisfies the three conditions but is, in an important sense, theory-laden.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the possibility that observation reports are always to some degree shaped by theoretical presuppositions, so that 'pure' observation free from theory is not available; a condition addressing the degree or kind of theoretical loading would be needed.
passage: It is tempting to define an observation report as one that describes a state of affairs directly apprehended through the senses, that is expressed in terms any competent observer could understand without specialised training, and that could in principle be confirmed or disconfirmed by any suitably placed observer. This definition appeals to a picture of observation as a transparent encounter between the perceiver and the world — a picture in which the senses deliver raw data that is subsequently organised by the understanding. The requirement that the report be expressed in ordinary terms is meant to secure its independence from theoretical commitments, and the condition of public confirmability is intended to guarantee objectivity. Yet one might observe that even the most apparently simple observation report may carry a considerable theoretical burden. Consider the statement that the litmus paper has turned red. The change of colour is apprehended through the senses; the terms are familiar to any competent speaker of the language; and the observation is in principle available to anyone present. But the identification of the paper as litmus paper already presupposes a body of chemical theory, and the very act of attending to the colour change rather than to the paper's texture or shape is guided by expectations formed within a theoretical framework. It appears, then, that the three conditions may be satisfied by reports that are not observational in the pure, theory-free sense the definition was intended to capture. What the definition lacks is a recognition that observation and theory are not so cleanly separable as the three conditions presuppose.

definition: A theory T1 is reduced to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every law of T1 is logically deducible from the laws of T2 together with appropriate bridge principles, (ii) every term of T1 is definable in terms of the vocabulary of T2 via those bridge principles, and (iii) T2 is more general than T1 — that is, the domain of T2 properly includes the domain of T1.
conditions: Every law of T1 is logically deducible from the laws of T2 together with bridge principles
Every term of T1 is definable in terms of the vocabulary of T2
T2 is more general than T1 — the domain of T2 properly includes the domain of T1
counterexample: Consider the relation between classical thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. It is commonly said that thermodynamics reduces to statistical mechanics, and indeed many of the laws of thermodynamics can be recovered from statistical-mechanical principles. Yet the concept of temperature in thermodynamics, when translated into statistical mechanics, corresponds not to a single definite mechanical quantity but to a statistical average over molecular motions — an average that admits of fluctuations absent from the thermodynamic concept. The bridge principle connecting the two is approximate, not exact. All three conditions appear to be met in the approximate sense customary in physics, yet the reduction is not strict: the deduction holds only in the limit of large numbers, and the definitional equivalences are idealisations.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that the bridge principles yield exact logical deductions and strict definitional equivalences, but actual reductions in science typically involve approximations, limiting conditions, and idealisations. A condition specifying the permissible degree of approximation is needed.
passage: One might propose that a theory T1 is reduced to a theory T2 if and only if every law of T1 is logically deducible from the laws of T2 together with appropriate bridge principles, every term of T1 is definable in terms of the vocabulary of T2, and T2 is more general than T1 in the sense that its domain properly includes that of T1. This definition embodies a tidy, logical picture of inter-theoretic reduction: the old theory is shown to be a special case of the new, its laws derived, its terms translated, its domain subsumed. The bridge principles serve as the connective tissue, linking the vocabularies of the two theories and enabling the deduction to go through. Yet actual cases of reduction in the sciences seldom conform to this picture with the precision the definition demands. Consider the reduction of classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics — perhaps the paradigm case of successful reduction. The second law of thermodynamics, when translated into statistical-mechanical terms, holds only with overwhelming probability, not with the strict universality that the thermodynamic formulation asserts. The concept of temperature, when given a statistical-mechanical definition, corresponds to a mean kinetic energy that admits of fluctuations which have no counterpart in the thermodynamic concept. The bridge principles, far from being exact definitional equivalences, are approximations that hold in the thermodynamic limit — that is, when the number of particles is astronomically large. It appears, therefore, that the three conditions are satisfied only in an approximate and idealised sense, and that genuine reductions in science involve a looseness of fit that the definition does not accommodate. What is wanted is a more nuanced account that allows for approximation and limiting conditions in the deductive and definitional relations.

definition: An investigation I constitutes a scientific experiment if and only if (i) I involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable by the investigator, (ii) I is designed to test a specific hypothesis by comparing the observed outcome with the outcome predicted by that hypothesis, and (iii) I is repeatable — that is, the procedure can in principle be carried out again under the same conditions with the expectation of the same result.
conditions: I involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable
I is designed to test a specific hypothesis by comparing observed and predicted outcomes
I is repeatable under the same conditions with the expectation of the same result
counterexample: Consider a geologist who observes the layers of sediment in a cliff face exposed by a landslide. She has not manipulated any variable; the landslide was a natural event. Yet she may use the exposed strata to test a specific hypothesis about the geological history of the region, comparing the sequence of layers with what her theory predicts. The observation is, in a sense, repeatable — other cliff faces in the region may be examined. The investigation satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) but not (i), yet it is commonly regarded as a legitimate piece of scientific investigation that functions, in practice, as an experiment does.
missing_condition: The definition excludes natural experiments and observational investigations that serve the same evidential function as manipulative experiments. A broader condition allowing nature to provide the 'manipulation' — or an alternative account of the evidential role of investigation — is required.
passage: It is commonly supposed that a scientific experiment is an investigation that involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable, that is designed to test a specific hypothesis by comparing observed and predicted outcomes, and that is repeatable under the same conditions. This definition reflects a picture of experimentation drawn from the controlled laboratory setting, where the investigator intervenes in the course of nature, holds all but one factor constant, and records what follows. The repeatability condition ensures that the result is not an artefact of chance. Yet one might observe that much of what passes for experimental science, and is rightly so called, does not conform to this picture. A geologist who examines the strata exposed by a landslide has not manipulated any variable; nature has, so to speak, performed the intervention for her. An astronomer who observes the deflection of starlight during a solar eclipse — as in the famous test of general relativity — has not arranged the eclipse or positioned the stars. Yet these investigations serve the same evidential function as laboratory experiments: they bring observation to bear upon hypothesis in a controlled and informative manner. The definition, it appears, is too narrow. By insisting upon deliberate manipulation by the investigator, it excludes a large class of investigations that are experimental in spirit and in evidential value, even though the manipulation is performed by nature rather than by the human hand. What is perhaps needed is a condition that captures the evidential structure of experimentation without requiring that the investigator be the agent of intervention.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M assigns a numerical value to Q by means of a well-defined operational procedure, (ii) the numerical value assigned by M is reproducible — that is, repeated applications of M to the same object under the same conditions yield the same value, and (iii) M is calibrated against a standard — that is, M has been checked against a reference quantity whose value is taken as given.
conditions: M assigns a numerical value to Q by means of a well-defined operational procedure
The numerical value is reproducible under repeated applications
M is calibrated against a standard reference quantity
counterexample: Consider a thermometer that has been improperly calibrated against a faulty standard. The thermometer assigns numerical values to temperature by a well-defined operational procedure — the expansion of mercury in a glass tube. Its readings are highly reproducible: the same thermometer, applied to the same body, yields the same reading each time. And it has been calibrated, though against a standard that is itself inaccurate. All three conditions are met, yet the thermometer does not measure temperature correctly. Its readings are systematically displaced from the true values, and it would be misleading to say that it measures temperature without qualification.
missing_condition: The definition requires calibration against a standard but does not require that the standard itself be accurate or traceable to a correct reference. A condition ensuring the reliability or correctness of the standard is needed.
passage: One might define measurement as a procedure that assigns a numerical value to a quantity by means of a well-defined operational procedure, that yields reproducible results, and that has been calibrated against a standard reference quantity. This definition has the merit of clarity: it specifies what the procedure does (assigns a number), how reliably it does it (reproducibly), and how its accuracy is assured (through calibration). The operationalist spirit of the definition — its insistence that measurement be tied to a concrete procedure — reflects a tradition in the philosophy of science that goes back at least to Bridgman. Yet one might observe that the three conditions, taken jointly, are not sufficient to guarantee that a procedure genuinely measures the quantity it purports to measure. Suppose a thermometer has been calibrated against a standard that is itself faulty — a reference thermometer, let us say, whose readings are systematically displaced from the true temperature by a fixed amount. The thermometer under consideration will assign numerical values by a well-defined procedure; its readings will be highly reproducible; and it will have been calibrated against a standard. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the thermometer does not measure temperature correctly. Its readings are systematically in error, and any conclusions drawn from them will be correspondingly mistaken. The difficulty is that the definition requires calibration but does not require that the standard against which calibration is performed be itself accurate or traceable to a reliable reference. What is needed, it appears, is a further condition to the effect that the standard be correct, or at least that the chain of calibration terminate in a standard whose reliability has been independently established.

definition: A statement S is a scientific prediction if and only if (i) S is derived from a scientific theory or hypothesis by logical or mathematical deduction, (ii) S describes a state of affairs that has not yet been observed at the time of derivation, and (iii) S is sufficiently precise that it could in principle be shown to be false by observation.
conditions: S is derived from a scientific theory or hypothesis by deduction
S describes a state of affairs not yet observed at the time of derivation
S is sufficiently precise that it could be shown false by observation
counterexample: Consider a theory that predicts the existence of a planet beyond the orbit of the outermost known planet, and suppose the prediction is derived deductively from the theory, describes a state of affairs not yet observed, and is sufficiently precise to be falsifiable. Now suppose the planet is in fact discovered, but by accident — an astronomer scanning the sky for comets stumbles upon it, and the discovery has nothing to do with the prediction. The prediction satisfies all three conditions, yet its confirmation is purely coincidental: the prediction played no role in guiding the observation. One would hesitate to say that the theory has been confirmed by its prediction in any methodologically significant sense.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the prediction play a guiding or motivating role in the observation that confirms it. Without such a condition, accidental confirmations count equally with those that are genuinely predicted and sought.
passage: It is natural to say that a scientific prediction is a statement derived from a theory, describing a state of affairs not yet observed, and sufficiently precise to be falsifiable. This definition captures the central features of what we take prediction to be in the sciences: the theory speaks in advance of experience, tells us what we shall find, and exposes itself to the risk of refutation. The deductive condition ensures that the prediction is genuinely entailed by the theory rather than being a loose guess; the novelty condition distinguishes prediction from mere accommodation of data already known; and the precision condition guards against the evasions of vagueness. Yet one might observe that these three conditions, though individually compelling, do not jointly ensure that a prediction plays the methodological role we expect of it. Suppose a theory entails the existence of a hitherto unobserved celestial body, specifying its mass, orbit, and position with admirable precision. And suppose that the body is in fact discovered — but not by anyone acting on the prediction. An astronomer, quite ignorant of the theoretical prediction, stumbles upon the body while searching for something else entirely. The prediction satisfies all three conditions: it was derived from the theory, it described an unobserved state of affairs, and it was precise enough to be falsifiable. Yet the confirmation is, in an important sense, accidental. The prediction did not guide the investigation that led to the discovery, and the methodological significance we attach to successful prediction — the sense that the theory has been put to the test and has survived — is correspondingly diminished. What appears to be missing is a condition requiring that the prediction actually direct or motivate the observational inquiry that confirms it.

definition: A system of categories C constitutes a scientific classification of a domain D if and only if (i) the categories in C are mutually exclusive — no member of D belongs to more than one category, (ii) the categories in C are jointly exhaustive — every member of D belongs to at least one category, and (iii) the assignment of members to categories is based upon observable, measurable properties.
conditions: The categories are mutually exclusive
The categories are jointly exhaustive
Assignment to categories is based on observable, measurable properties
counterexample: Consider a classification of chemical elements based solely on their observable colour at room temperature. The categories — silver, yellow, red, and so forth — might be made mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive by careful stipulation. The assignment is based on an observable, measurable property. Yet such a classification groups together elements with vastly different chemical behaviours — sodium and aluminium are both silvery — and separates elements with similar chemical properties. It satisfies all three conditions but fails to be a scientifically useful classification, for it does not track the natural groupings that matter for chemical theory.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the properties used for classification be scientifically significant — that is, that they track natural kinds or theoretically important groupings rather than superficial or arbitrary similarities.
passage: One might propose that a scientific classification of a domain is a system of mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive categories, assignment to which is based upon observable, measurable properties. The definition has a pleasing formal tidiness. Mutual exclusivity ensures that no object is classified in two ways at once, which would introduce ambiguity; joint exhaustivity ensures that nothing in the domain escapes classification; and the requirement of observable, measurable properties guards against the intrusion of subjective or metaphysical criteria. One might think that any system satisfying these three conditions would serve the purposes of science admirably. Yet one might observe that the conditions, taken together, are insufficient to distinguish a good scientific classification from a bad one. Consider a classification of the chemical elements based solely upon their colour at room temperature. The categories — silver, yellow, red, and so forth — can be defined so as to be mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive, and colour is certainly an observable, measurable property. But such a classification groups together sodium, aluminium, and iron under the heading 'silver,' while separating copper from gold, despite the important chemical similarities between them. The classification satisfies all three conditions, yet it is scientifically useless, for it fails to track the natural groupings — the families and periods — that reflect the underlying structure of chemical behaviour. What is wanted, it appears, is a further condition requiring that the properties used as the basis for classification be theoretically significant — that they carve nature at its joints, as the old metaphor has it.

definition: A representation M is a scientific model of a system S if and only if (i) M shares a structural correspondence with S — that is, there is a mapping between elements of M and elements of S that preserves certain relations, (ii) M is simpler than S in the sense that M omits or idealises certain features of S, and (iii) M is used by scientists to draw inferences about S.
conditions: M shares a structural correspondence with S via a relation-preserving mapping
M is simpler than S, omitting or idealising certain features
M is used by scientists to draw inferences about S
counterexample: Consider a toy globe sold in a shop for children. The globe shares a structural correspondence with the Earth — there is a mapping between points on the globe and points on the Earth that preserves certain spatial relations. The globe is simpler than the Earth, omitting the vast majority of geographical and geological detail. And a child — or indeed a scientist on holiday — might use the globe to draw some rough inference about the relative positions of the continents. All three conditions are met, yet one would hesitate to call a toy globe a scientific model. It was not constructed with the aim of facilitating scientific inquiry, and its simplifications are not guided by any theoretical purpose.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the simplifications and idealisations of the model be theoretically motivated — that is, that the features retained and the features omitted be selected on the basis of their relevance to a scientific question. Without this, any rough similarity counts as a model.
passage: It is natural to define a scientific model as a representation that shares a structural correspondence with the system it represents, that is simpler than the system by virtue of omitting or idealising certain features, and that is used by scientists to draw inferences about the system. This definition captures the central intuition that a model is a simplified stand-in for a more complicated reality, and that its value lies in the inferences it enables. The structural correspondence condition ensures that the model is not merely an arbitrary construction but bears a genuine relation to the system. The simplicity condition reflects the fact that a model that reproduced every feature of the system would not be a model at all but a duplicate. And the inferential condition ties the model to the practice of science. Yet one might observe that these conditions are met by objects that we should not wish to call scientific models. A toy globe, sold in a shop for children, shares a structural correspondence with the Earth, is vastly simpler than the Earth, and might be used by anyone — including a scientist — to draw rough inferences about the relative positions of the continents. But a toy globe is not a scientific model. Its simplifications are not guided by any theoretical concern; the features it retains and those it omits are chosen for commercial and aesthetic reasons, not for their scientific relevance. What the definition appears to lack is a condition specifying that the model's idealisations be theoretically motivated — that the selection of what to include and what to leave out be guided by the demands of a scientific question.

definition: A proposition E explains a phenomenon P if and only if (i) E cites a cause or set of causes that are temporally prior to P, (ii) E renders P intelligible by showing that P was to be expected given the cited causes, and (iii) E is itself well-confirmed by independent lines of evidence.
conditions: E cites a cause or set of causes temporally prior to P
E renders P intelligible by showing that P was to be expected given the cited causes
E is itself well-confirmed by independent lines of evidence
counterexample: Consider the fall of a barometer followed by a storm. The fall of the barometer is temporally prior to the storm. The fall of the barometer renders the storm intelligible in the sense that, having observed barometric drops before storms, one comes to expect storms after such drops. And the correlation between falling barometers and storms is well-confirmed by decades of meteorological observation. All three conditions are met, yet one would hesitate to say that the fall of the barometer explains the storm. The barometer does not cause the storm; both are effects of a common cause, namely a drop in atmospheric pressure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the cause cited in the explanation be a genuine cause of the phenomenon rather than a mere correlate or fellow-effect of a common cause. A condition distinguishing causal from merely correlational antecedence is needed.
passage: One might propose that a proposition explains a phenomenon if and only if it cites a cause or set of causes that are temporally prior to the phenomenon, renders the phenomenon intelligible by showing that it was to be expected, and is itself well-confirmed by independent evidence. This definition captures a good deal of what we expect of scientific explanation: temporal priority ensures that the explanation does not put the cart before the horse; intelligibility ensures that the explanation genuinely illuminates the phenomenon rather than merely restating it; and independent confirmation excludes mere conjecture. Yet one might observe that the definition is, in a certain respect, too permissive. Consider the case of a falling barometer and a subsequent storm. The barometric drop is temporally prior to the storm. The correlation between falling barometers and storms is so well-established that the storm, following the barometric drop, is entirely to be expected. And the correlation is supported by decades of independent meteorological evidence. All three conditions are satisfied. But it appears deeply unsatisfying to say that the barometer's fall explains the storm. The barometer does not bring about the storm; both are effects of the same atmospheric change. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the cited antecedent stand in a genuinely causal relation to the phenomenon, rather than being merely a correlated precursor. Without such a condition, any well-confirmed temporal antecedent counts as explanatory, however indirect or coincidental its relation to the phenomenon may be.

definition: A generalisation G is a scientific law if and only if (i) G is exceptionless within its specified domain — no observed instance falls outside the pattern it describes, (ii) G has been confirmed by a significant number of diverse observations, and (iii) G is expressible as a mathematical equation or formal relation between measurable quantities.
conditions: G is exceptionless within its specified domain
G has been confirmed by a significant number of diverse observations
G is expressible as a mathematical equation or formal relation
counterexample: Consider the generalisation that all swans observed in Europe are white. For many centuries this generalisation was exceptionless within its domain; it had been confirmed by a vast number of diverse observations across many countries and many years; and it could readily be expressed as a formal relation — for any x, if x is a swan observed in Europe, then x is white. Yet the generalisation was not a law but an accident of geographical sampling. When European explorers reached Australia, black swans were found in abundance. The generalisation satisfied all three conditions and yet lacked the necessity and universality characteristic of a genuine law.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that a scientific law hold necessarily across all possible instances of its domain, including unobserved ones. A condition requiring that the generalisation be nomologically necessary — that it would hold under all circumstances within its domain, not merely under those so far observed — is needed.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a generalisation qualifies as a scientific law if it is exceptionless within its domain, confirmed by a significant number of diverse observations, and expressible as a mathematical equation or formal relation between measurable quantities. The exceptionlessness condition captures the universality we expect of laws; the confirmational breadth ensures that the generalisation has not been established on the basis of a single favourable instance; and the mathematical expressibility requirement reflects the fact that the paradigmatic laws of physics — Newton's laws, Maxwell's equations, the gas laws — take the form of mathematical relationships. Yet one might observe that these conditions, though individually plausible, are jointly insufficient. Consider the proposition that all swans observed in Europe are white. For centuries this generalisation was exceptionless within its domain: not a single non-white swan had been observed in the whole of Europe. The observations were diverse — spanning different countries, climates, and seasons. And the generalisation was readily expressible in formal terms. All three conditions were met, and yet the generalisation was not a law. It was an accidental uniformity, contingent upon the fact that European observers had not yet encountered the black swans of Australia. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the generalisation hold not merely across observed instances but across all possible instances — that it possess the kind of necessity that distinguishes a law from a fortunate accident.

definition: A theory T is genuinely falsifiable if and only if (i) there exists at least one logically possible observation that would be inconsistent with T, (ii) T specifies the conditions under which such an observation would obtain, and (iii) the practitioners of T are willing to abandon or revise T in the face of such an observation.
conditions: There exists at least one logically possible observation inconsistent with T
T specifies the conditions under which such an observation would obtain
The practitioners of T are willing to abandon or revise T if such observation is made
counterexample: Consider a school of astrologers who formulate their theory in such a way that a logically possible observation — say, a specified conjunction of planets failing to produce the predicted effect — would be inconsistent with their theory. The theory specifies the conditions under which such an observation would arise. And the astrologers sincerely declare their willingness to revise their theory in the face of disconfirming evidence. Yet whenever such evidence arises, the astrologers introduce auxiliary hypotheses — an unrecorded planet, an interfering aspect, an error in the ephemeris — that preserve the theory from refutation. All three conditions are formally satisfied, yet the theory is not genuinely falsifiable in practice.
missing_condition: The definition requires willingness to abandon the theory but does not require that this willingness be exercised in practice — that is, it does not prohibit the systematic introduction of ad hoc auxiliary hypotheses that shield the theory from every disconfirming observation.
passage: One might propose that a theory is genuinely falsifiable if and only if there exists a logically possible observation inconsistent with it, the theory specifies the conditions under which such an observation would obtain, and the practitioners of the theory are willing to abandon or revise it in the face of such evidence. This definition attempts to capture not merely the logical possibility of refutation but also the psychological and sociological dimension — the community's readiness to take refutation seriously. The first condition ensures that the theory is not a tautology; the second ensures that it does not make itself immune to test by vagueness; and the third is meant to guard against the pretence of falsifiability in a community that would never actually acknowledge defeat. Yet one might observe that the third condition, though well-intentioned, is insufficient. Consider a school of astrologers whose theoretical framework formally entails certain observable consequences and who sincerely profess their willingness to revise their theory should those consequences fail to materialise. Whenever a predicted conjunction fails to produce the expected result, however, the astrologers introduce an auxiliary hypothesis — an unrecorded planet was exerting a countervailing influence, or an error in the ephemeris displaced the conjunction by a critical degree. The willingness to revise is professed but never exercised; the theory is shielded from refutation by an inexhaustible supply of ad hoc adjustments. All three conditions are met in the letter, yet the theory is not falsifiable in the spirit the definition was intended to capture. What is missing is a condition requiring that the practitioners refrain from systematic ad hoc immunisation — that their willingness to revise be genuine and not merely nominal.

definition: A framework F constitutes a paradigm for a scientific discipline if and only if (i) F defines the central problems of the discipline, (ii) F provides criteria by which proposed solutions are judged acceptable or unacceptable, and (iii) F has endured for at least one generation of practitioners.
conditions: F defines the central problems of the discipline
F provides criteria for judging proposed solutions
F has endured for at least one generation of practitioners
counterexample: Consider the phlogiston theory of combustion in eighteenth-century chemistry. Phlogiston theory defined the central problems of the discipline — what happens when substances burn, what distinguishes metals from their ores. It provided clear criteria for judging solutions — an adequate explanation must identify the phlogiston content of the substances involved. And it endured for well over a generation, dominating chemical thought for nearly a century. All three conditions are met, yet phlogiston theory was fundamentally mistaken, and the revolution brought about by Lavoisier's oxygen theory eventually displaced it entirely. The definition does not distinguish between paradigms that correctly identify the structure of the domain and those that lead inquiry systematically astray.
missing_condition: The definition omits a requirement that the paradigm correctly identify — or at least progressively approximate — the genuine structure of the domain it investigates. Without such a condition, paradigms that are systematically mistaken but historically durable qualify on equal footing with those that lead inquiry toward the truth.
passage: One might suppose that a framework constitutes a paradigm for a scientific discipline if and only if it defines the central problems of the discipline, provides criteria by which proposed solutions are judged, and has endured for at least one generation of practitioners. This definition captures the sociological and methodological role that a paradigm plays: it shapes the questions scientists ask, determines what counts as an adequate answer, and persists long enough to form a tradition. The durability condition is intended to exclude passing fashions and ensure that only established frameworks qualify. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken jointly, are compatible with a paradigm that is deeply and systematically wrong. Consider the phlogiston theory of combustion, which dominated chemistry for the better part of a century. Phlogiston theory defined the problems — what is combustion? what distinguishes a metal from its calx? — and provided criteria for judging solutions: an adequate account must identify the phlogiston released or absorbed. The theory endured for generations. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet phlogiston was a fiction, and the theory led chemists into a cul-de-sac from which only Lavoisier's revolutionary work eventually extracted them. The definition, it appears, mistakes durability and influence for adequacy. What is needed is a further condition requiring that the paradigm bear some genuine relation to the structure of the domain — that it not merely organise inquiry but organise it in a direction that is at least approximately correct.

definition: A phenomenon P is reduced to a lower-level description L if and only if (i) every occurrence of P can be fully described in terms of the entities and processes posited by L, (ii) the laws governing P can be derived from the laws governing the entities of L, and (iii) the vocabulary used to describe P can be translated without remainder into the vocabulary of L.
conditions: Every occurrence of P can be fully described in the terms of L
The laws governing P can be derived from the laws of L
The vocabulary of P can be translated without remainder into the vocabulary of L
counterexample: Consider the phenomenon of a traffic jam. One might attempt to reduce it to the physics of individual automobiles — their masses, velocities, accelerations, and frictional forces. Every occurrence of a traffic jam can, in principle, be described in terms of these physical quantities. The laws of motion governing individual cars can, in principle, account for the movements of every vehicle involved. And one might insist that the vocabulary of traffic — 'congestion,' 'flow,' 'bottleneck' — can in principle be translated into the language of physics. Yet the reduction misses something essential: the patterns of traffic congestion depend on the decisions, habits, and reactions of drivers, and these macroscopic regularities emerge from the interactions of agents in ways that are not usefully captured by the physics of individual vehicles.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the lower-level description preserve the explanatory and predictive power of the higher-level description. A reduction that translates vocabulary and derives laws but loses the capacity to identify and explain the macroscopic patterns that give the higher-level description its value is a reduction in name only.
passage: One might propose that a phenomenon is reduced to a lower-level description if and only if every occurrence of the phenomenon can be fully described in the lower-level terms, the laws governing the phenomenon can be derived from the lower-level laws, and the vocabulary of the higher-level description can be translated without remainder into the lower-level vocabulary. This definition embodies a vision of reductive unity in science: the higher-level sciences are, in the end, special cases of the lower-level ones, and their vocabularies are in principle eliminable. The three conditions together seem to guarantee that nothing is lost in the transition from the higher to the lower level. Yet one might observe that the conditions, even when satisfied, do not ensure that the reduction preserves what is most valuable about the higher-level description. Consider the phenomenon of a traffic jam. In principle, every traffic jam can be described in terms of the masses, velocities, and accelerations of individual motor cars. The laws of mechanics govern the motion of each vehicle. And the vocabulary of traffic — 'congestion,' 'bottleneck,' 'flow' — can, with sufficient ingenuity, be translated into the language of physics. All three conditions appear to be met. But the physical description of several hundred vehicles and their individual trajectories does not, in any useful sense, explain the traffic jam. The explanation lies in the macroscopic patterns — the wave-like propagation of congestion, the sensitivity to small perturbations, the role of driver behaviour — that emerge from the interactions but are invisible at the level of individual vehicles. The definition, it appears, requires only that the reduction be formally possible, not that it preserve the explanatory and predictive virtues that made the higher-level description valuable in the first place.

definition: An investigation I constitutes a controlled experiment if and only if (i) I compares an experimental group to a control group that is identical in all respects save the variable under investigation, (ii) the assignment of subjects to groups is randomised, and (iii) the investigation is conducted under conditions that can be replicated by other investigators.
conditions: I compares an experimental group to a control group identical save for the variable under investigation
The assignment of subjects to groups is randomised
The investigation can be replicated by other investigators
counterexample: Consider the investigation of whether a particular soil type produces better crop yields. A farmer notices that her northern field, which has clay soil, consistently produces better wheat than her southern field, which has sandy soil. The two fields differ in the crucial variable — soil type — and are otherwise similar in rainfall, sunlight, and cultivation practices. Yet the 'assignment' is not randomised — nature determined the soil type, not the investigator. And the conditions are, in a sense, unreplicable — another investigator cannot recreate the same two fields. The investigation satisfies condition (i) but fails (ii) and (iii), and yet it may constitute a perfectly valuable piece of agricultural inquiry, isolating the variable of soil type by exploiting a natural difference.
missing_condition: The definition requires randomisation and replicability in the laboratory sense but excludes quasi-experimental and natural-experimental designs that achieve the same evidential aim — the isolation of a single variable — through means other than randomisation and controlled replication.
passage: It is commonly supposed that a controlled experiment must compare an experimental group to a control group identical in all respects save the variable under investigation, that the assignment of subjects to groups must be randomised, and that the conditions must be replicable by other investigators. These requirements reflect the practice of the modern laboratory: randomisation guards against systematic bias in the assignment of subjects; the control group provides a baseline against which the effect of the variable can be measured; and replicability ensures that the result is not an artefact of the particular occasion. Yet one might observe that a great deal of valuable scientific investigation does not meet these conditions. Consider the case of a farmer who notices that her northern field, with clay soil, consistently yields better wheat than her southern field, with sandy soil, though the two fields are otherwise comparable in respect of rainfall, sunlight, and cultivation. The two fields serve, in effect, as experimental and control groups, differing in the single variable of soil type. But the assignment is not randomised — it was determined by geological processes, not by the investigator — and the particular arrangement of fields cannot be replicated by another investigator. The investigation satisfies the first condition but fails the second and third. Yet it may provide perfectly sound evidence that soil type affects crop yield, achieving the essential aim of the controlled experiment — the isolation of a single variable — by exploiting a natural difference rather than an artificial manipulation. The definition, it appears, has conflated a particular method of achieving the aim of experimentation with the aim itself.

definition: A procedure M measures a property Q of an object O if and only if (i) M assigns a unique numerical value to Q for each application to O, (ii) the value assigned is invariant under change of observer — that is, any competent observer applying M to O will obtain the same value, and (iii) the assignment of values is such that quantitative relations among the values reflect quantitative relations among the objects measured.
conditions: M assigns a unique numerical value to Q for each application
The value is invariant under change of observer
Quantitative relations among values reflect quantitative relations among objects
counterexample: Consider a panel of trained wine judges who assign numerical scores to wines on a scale from one to one hundred. Each judge assigns a unique numerical value. If the panel has been well-trained, different judges will, to a reasonable approximation, assign the same score to the same wine — the value is approximately invariant under change of observer. And the scores are such that quantitative relations among them reflect relations among the wines: a wine scoring 90 is better than one scoring 70, and the difference between 90 and 70 is the same as between 80 and 60. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one would hesitate to say that the wine-scoring procedure measures a property of the wine in the same sense that a thermometer measures temperature. The scores reflect a complex mixture of sensory responses, expectations, and conventions, and the property being 'measured' — quality — may not be a single, well-defined quantity at all.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property Q be a well-defined, unitary physical or natural quantity rather than a composite of subjective judgements. Without such a condition, any reliable rating procedure counts as measurement, even when the 'quantity' measured is not a genuine property of the object.
passage: One might define measurement as a procedure that assigns a unique numerical value to a property of an object, yields values invariant under change of observer, and ensures that quantitative relations among values reflect quantitative relations among the objects measured. This definition has the merit of generality: it is not tied to any particular instrument or physical property, and it captures the essential features of measurement — uniqueness, objectivity, and structural correspondence between the numerical representation and the empirical reality. The invariance condition, in particular, is intended to exclude subjective assessments from the domain of measurement. Yet one might observe that the definition, for all its apparent rigour, admits cases that we should hesitate to call measurement in the full scientific sense. Consider a panel of expert wine judges who have been trained to assign numerical scores on a standardised scale. Each judge assigns a unique score; well-trained judges achieve a high degree of inter-observer agreement; and the scoring scale is designed so that quantitative relations among scores reflect relations among wines. All three conditions appear to be met. But wine scoring is not measurement in the same sense as the measurement of temperature or mass. The property being assessed — quality — is not a single, well-defined natural quantity but a composite of sensory impressions, cultural expectations, and evaluative conventions. The definition, it appears, requires that the procedure be well-behaved in formal terms but does not require that the property measured be a genuine, unitary feature of the object. What is needed is a condition distinguishing natural properties from evaluative composites.

definition: A theory T makes a genuine prediction if and only if (i) T entails a statement describing a specific observable state of affairs, (ii) the statement was not among the data used in constructing T, and (iii) the statement is logically independent of T's auxiliary assumptions — that is, it follows from the core principles of T alone.
conditions: T entails a statement describing a specific observable state of affairs
The statement was not among the data used in constructing T
The statement follows from the core principles of T alone, independently of auxiliary assumptions
counterexample: Consider a theory of planetary motion whose core principles, taken alone, predict circular orbits but whose auxiliary assumptions — about the distribution of mass in the solar system, perturbative effects, and atmospheric refraction — are adjusted until the combined system predicts an observed planetary position with great accuracy. The theory entails a specific observable state of affairs. The particular position predicted was not among the original data. But the prediction depends crucially on the auxiliary assumptions, not on the core principles alone. The third condition is not met, and yet it is unclear whether any scientific theory ever makes predictions from its core principles alone, without the mediation of auxiliary assumptions.
passage: One might propose that a theory makes a genuine prediction if and only if it entails a statement describing a specific observable state of affairs, that statement was not among the data used in constructing the theory, and the statement follows from the core principles of the theory alone, independently of auxiliary assumptions. The first two conditions are widely accepted: a prediction must be specific and must concern something not already known. The third condition is more demanding — it requires that the prediction flow from the theory's fundamental principles rather than from an apparatus of auxiliary hypotheses that might be adjusted to yield any desired result. The intention behind this condition is admirable: it is meant to ensure that a successful prediction genuinely confirms the theory rather than merely confirming the skill of the theorist in adjusting auxiliary parameters. Yet one might observe that the condition, taken strictly, would disqualify virtually every prediction in the history of science. Newton's prediction of planetary positions required auxiliary assumptions about the masses and initial conditions of the planets. Einstein's prediction of the bending of starlight required assumptions about the mass of the sun and the geometry of the light path. No theory confronts the world in splendid isolation from auxiliary hypotheses; the core principles, taken alone, are almost always insufficient to entail any specific observable consequence. The definition, it appears, sets a standard so exacting that it cannot in practice be met, and in doing so it fails to distinguish the genuine predictive achievements of science from the ad hoc adjustments of pseudo-science. What is needed is not the elimination of auxiliary assumptions but a condition specifying when their role is legitimate and when it is not.

definition: A division of objects into kinds constitutes a natural classification if and only if (i) the kinds are defined by properties that the objects possess intrinsically rather than relationally, (ii) every object in a given kind shares all the defining properties with every other object in that kind, and (iii) the defining properties are discoverable by empirical investigation.
conditions: The kinds are defined by intrinsic rather than relational properties
Every object in a kind shares all the defining properties
The defining properties are discoverable by empirical investigation
counterexample: Consider the classification of organisms into species. On the biological species concept, a species is defined not by intrinsic morphological properties but by a relational property — the capacity to interbreed and produce fertile offspring. Individuals within a species may differ dramatically in their intrinsic properties — size, colour, shape — while sharing the relational property of interbreeding compatibility. The classification of organisms into biological species violates condition (i), since the defining property is relational rather than intrinsic, and arguably violates condition (ii), since individuals within a species need not share all intrinsic properties. Yet the biological species concept is widely regarded as the most natural classification of living organisms.
missing_condition: The definition excludes classifications based on relational properties, but some of the most scientifically important classifications — species, chemical bonds, social roles — are defined by relational rather than intrinsic properties. A condition permitting relational properties to serve as the basis of natural kinds is needed.
