passage: One might define a natural classification as a division of objects into kinds defined by intrinsic properties, where every member of a kind shares all the defining properties, and the properties are discoverable by empirical investigation. This definition reflects a picture of classification in which nature comes pre-sorted into kinds distinguished by their internal constitutions, and the task of the classifier is to discover the intrinsic features that mark the boundaries between kinds. The requirement that the properties be intrinsic rather than relational is intended to ensure that the classification reflects the nature of the objects themselves, not their accidental relations to other things. The universality condition — that every member of a kind share all the defining properties — is intended to ensure that the kinds are genuine and not arbitrary groupings. Yet one might observe that this picture sits uneasily with some of the most successful classifications in the sciences. Consider the classification of organisms into species according to the biological species concept. A species, on this account, is defined not by any intrinsic morphological property but by the relational property of reproductive compatibility: organisms belong to the same species if and only if they can interbreed and produce fertile offspring. Two organisms of the same species may differ dramatically in size, colour, and form — the male and female of many bird species are strikingly dissimilar — while sharing only the relational capacity for interbreeding. The classification violates the first condition, since the defining property is relational. It arguably violates the second, since members of a species need not share all intrinsic properties. Yet the biological species concept is widely regarded as the most natural and scientifically productive classification of living things. The definition, it seems, has imposed too restrictive a conception of the properties that may ground a natural classification.

definition: A construction M is an adequate scientific model of a target system S if and only if (i) M reproduces the observable behaviour of S within the domain for which M was designed, (ii) M is internally consistent — it does not generate contradictory predictions, and (iii) M employs only quantities and relations that have a clear physical interpretation.
conditions: M reproduces the observable behaviour of S within its designed domain
M is internally consistent
M employs only quantities and relations with clear physical interpretation
counterexample: Consider two distinct models of the same system — say, two models of the hydrogen atom, one treating the electron as a particle in orbit and another treating it as a standing wave. Within their respective domains, both reproduce the observable behaviour of the hydrogen atom (the emission spectrum). Both are internally consistent. Both employ quantities with clear physical interpretations. All three conditions are met by both models, and yet the two models offer incompatible pictures of the same system. The definition provides no means of choosing between them and indeed declares both adequate, even though at most one can correctly represent the underlying reality.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that an adequate model be unique, or at least uniquely best, in its representation of the target system. Without a condition addressing the problem of underdetermination — the possibility of multiple incompatible but empirically equivalent models — the definition cannot distinguish a model that captures the structure of reality from one that merely mimics its observable output.
passage: One might propose that a construction constitutes an adequate scientific model if and only if it reproduces the observable behaviour of its target system within the relevant domain, is internally consistent, and employs only quantities with a clear physical interpretation. The first condition ensures empirical adequacy — the model agrees with observation. The second guards against the logical defect of self-contradiction. And the third is meant to exclude models that achieve empirical success by means of quantities that have no discernible connection to the physical world — mere mathematical artefacts, as it were. These conditions jointly express the conviction that a good model must be faithful to observation, logically sound, and physically meaningful. Yet one might observe that the conditions admit a difficulty that is, in a certain sense, more troubling than the defects they are designed to exclude. Consider the case of the hydrogen atom. One model treats the electron as a classical particle in orbit around the nucleus; another treats it as a standing wave. Both models, within their respective domains, reproduce the observable emission spectrum. Both are internally consistent. Both employ quantities — position and momentum in the one case, wave functions and energy levels in the other — that possess clear physical interpretations. All three conditions are satisfied by both models. But the two models offer deeply incompatible pictures of the electron. The definition provides no resources for choosing between them and, indeed, declares both adequate. What is missing is a condition addressing the problem of underdetermination — the possibility that two or more incompatible models may satisfy all the stated criteria equally well, so that empirical adequacy, consistency, and physical interpretability do not suffice to identify the correct representation of reality.

definition: An event C constitutes an explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) C is a prior occurrence that stands in a regular temporal relation to P, (ii) the transition from C to P is subsumable under a general law or pattern, and (iii) the description of C renders P unsurprising to a competent enquirer.
conditions: C is a prior occurrence that stands in a regular temporal relation to P
The transition from C to P is subsumable under a general law or pattern
The description of C renders P unsurprising to a competent enquirer
counterexample: Consider the whistling of a kettle prior to its boiling over. The whistle is a prior occurrence regularly conjoined with the boiling; the transition is subsumable under the generalisation that kettles whistle before boiling over; and the whistle renders the boiling unsurprising to anyone familiar with kitchens. Yet we should hesitate to say the whistling explains the boiling, for the whistle is a by-product of the same thermal process that causes the boiling, not the operative cause itself.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that C be causally operative in bringing about P, rather than merely a concomitant or symptomatic antecedent of the same underlying process.
passage: One might propose that an event explains a phenomenon if and only if it is a prior occurrence standing in a regular temporal relation to the phenomenon, the transition is subsumable under a general law, and the description renders the phenomenon unsurprising to a competent enquirer. This proposal has much to recommend it: it excludes post hoc rationalisations by insisting on temporal priority, it demands nomological backing, and it captures the intuition that a good explanation should remove puzzlement. Yet the proposal admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. When a kettle is left upon a stove, one commonly hears a whistling from the spout before the water boils over. The whistle is temporally prior to the boiling over, the transition is subsumable under a commonplace generalisation, and the whistle renders the boiling entirely unsurprising. All three conditions are met. Yet the whistling does not explain the boiling; both are effects of the same underlying thermal process — the sustained application of heat to water. What is missing is a condition stipulating that the prior event be causally operative in the production of the phenomenon rather than a mere symptom of the genuine cause.

definition: A generalisation G counts as a scientific law if and only if (i) G is universally quantified over all instances in its domain, (ii) G has been confirmed by a substantial number of observed instances, and (iii) no falsifying instance of G has been observed.
conditions: G is universally quantified over all instances in its domain
G has been confirmed by a substantial number of observed instances
No falsifying instance of G has been observed
counterexample: Consider the generalisation that all the coins in a particular philosopher's pocket are silver. This generalisation is universally quantified over its domain — the coins in the pocket. It has been confirmed by examining every coin therein. And no falsifying instance has been observed. Yet no one would regard this as a scientific law; it is a merely accidental truth, lacking the modal force we associate with genuine lawfulness.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that G support counterfactual conditionals or possess a kind of nomological necessity that distinguishes laws from accidentally true generalisations.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a scientific law is simply a universal generalisation that has been confirmed and never falsified. One might therefore define a scientific law as a generalisation that is universally quantified over its domain, confirmed by a substantial number of observed instances, and free of any falsifying instance. This definition appears to capture the salient features of paradigmatic laws: the law of gravitation, for example, is universally quantified, abundantly confirmed, and unfalsified. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is near at hand. Suppose a philosopher empties his pockets and finds that every coin therein happens to be silver. The generalisation that all coins in his pocket are silver is universally quantified over its domain. It has been confirmed by the examination of each instance. And no falsifying instance has been observed. Nevertheless, it would be absurd to call this a scientific law. The generalisation is merely accidentally true — it could easily have been otherwise — and it lacks the modal force that enables genuine laws to support counterfactual reasoning. A further condition is required: that the generalisation sustain counterfactual conditionals and possess the character of nomological necessity.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is internally consistent, (ii) T entails at least some observational consequences that have been verified, and (iii) T is expressible in a precise and systematic form.
conditions: T is internally consistent
T entails at least some observational consequences that have been verified
T is expressible in a precise and systematic form
counterexample: Consider a logician who constructs a consistent axiomatic system of pure arithmetic, expressed with maximal precision. Suppose she then adds the empirically verified proposition that the boiling point of water at standard pressure is 100 degrees Celsius. The resulting body of propositions is internally consistent, entails a verified observational consequence, and is expressed in a precise and systematic form. Yet it appears absurd to call this a scientific theory, for the components bear no organic relation to one another and the empirical content is a foreign graft.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the propositions of the theory be conceptually unified, such that the observational consequences flow from the theoretical principles rather than being merely appended.
passage: One might analyse the concept of a scientific theory by requiring internal consistency, the entailment of verified observational consequences, and precise systematic expression. Such a definition captures several important desiderata: consistency ensures that the theory does not generate contradictions; observational consequences connect it to the world; and precision facilitates communication and scrutiny. However, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be readily constructed. Imagine a logician who has developed an elegant axiomatic system of pure arithmetic. The system is internally consistent and expressed with the utmost precision. Suppose she then appends to this system the single empirical proposition that water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure, a claim that has been verified innumerable times. The enlarged body of propositions satisfies all three conditions: it is consistent, it entails a verified observational consequence, and it is systematic. Yet one would hesitate to call this a scientific theory. The empirical proposition and the arithmetical axioms bear no conceptual connection to one another. What is wanted is a further condition requiring the propositions to form an integrated whole, such that the observational consequences issue from the theoretical principles themselves.

definition: A report R counts as an observation if and only if (i) R describes a state of affairs that is directly perceived by a human observer, (ii) R is expressed in a vocabulary that does not presuppose any theoretical commitments, and (iii) R is intersubjectively verifiable under specified conditions.
conditions: R describes a state of affairs directly perceived by a human observer
R is expressed in a vocabulary free of theoretical presuppositions
R is intersubjectively verifiable under specified conditions
counterexample: Consider a trained physicist who reports that the needle of a galvanometer has deflected to the right. She directly perceives the needle's position. The report appears couched in purely observational language — 'needle,' 'deflection,' 'right.' And any colleague standing beside her may verify the same. Yet the very identification of the instrument as a galvanometer, and the significance of the deflection, are saturated with theoretical commitments concerning electric current. The second condition is only apparently satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that no vocabulary is wholly free of theoretical presuppositions, and thus requires a criterion for distinguishing acceptable from unacceptable degrees of theoretical loading.
passage: A common philosophical analysis holds that an observation is a report describing a directly perceived state of affairs, expressed without theoretical presuppositions, and intersubjectively verifiable. One might observe that this definition has considerable intuitive appeal: it excludes inference and speculation, it insists on plain descriptive language, and it demands public accessibility. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in ordinary scientific practice. When a physicist reports that a galvanometer needle has deflected to the right, she satisfies, on the face of it, every condition. She directly perceives the needle; her report employs terms that seem innocent of theory — 'needle,' 'deflection,' 'right'; and any colleague may verify the deflection. But the matter is less straightforward than it appears. The identification of the device as a galvanometer already presupposes a body of theory about the relationship between electric current and magnetic needles. The recognition of the deflection as significant depends on a theoretical understanding of what the instrument is designed to measure. If one presses the demand for a wholly theory-free vocabulary, it becomes exceedingly difficult to find any report in mature science that satisfies it. What is needed is not the elimination of all theoretical presupposition, but rather some principled distinction between permissible and impermissible degrees of theoretical commitment in observational reports.

definition: A theory T1 reduces to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every term of T1 is definable in the vocabulary of T2, (ii) every law of T1 is derivable from the laws of T2 together with the bridge definitions, and (iii) T2 is more fundamental than T1 in the sense that T2 applies to a wider domain.
conditions: Every term of T1 is definable in the vocabulary of T2
Every law of T1 is derivable from the laws of T2 together with bridge definitions
T2 is more fundamental than T1 in applying to a wider domain
counterexample: Consider the relationship between thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. The term 'temperature' in thermodynamics is commonly said to be definable as mean molecular kinetic energy. The laws of thermodynamics are said to be derivable from statistical mechanics. And statistical mechanics applies to a wider domain. Yet the derivation of the second law of thermodynamics requires a statistical assumption — that the system tends toward the most probable macrostate — which is not itself a law of mechanics. The 'derivation' is not strictly deductive but involves an additional probabilistic premise.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the derivation of the laws of the reduced theory from the reducing theory be strictly deductive, without the introduction of additional substantive assumptions beyond the bridge definitions.
passage: It is frequently maintained that one theory reduces to another when the vocabulary of the former is translatable into that of the latter, the laws of the former are derivable from those of the latter, and the latter is more fundamental. This analysis captures the standard textbook picture of theoretical reduction — the absorption of a less fundamental science into a more fundamental one. One might illustrate this with the much-discussed case of thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. The temperature of a gas, we are told, is definable as mean molecular kinetic energy. The gas laws are derivable from Newtonian mechanics applied to molecular populations. And statistical mechanics governs a wider domain. Yet closer inspection reveals a difficulty which the definition does not anticipate. The derivation of the second law of thermodynamics — that entropy tends to increase in closed systems — does not follow from the mechanical laws alone, even supplemented by bridge definitions. An additional assumption is required: that the system evolves toward the most probable macrostate. This is a substantive probabilistic postulate, not a bridge definition. The derivation thus contains an extra premise that the definition does not sanction, and it appears that the reduction is not as clean as the analysis suggests.

definition: A procedure E constitutes a scientific experiment if and only if (i) E involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable by the experimenter, (ii) E is designed to test a specific hypothesis, and (iii) E employs controls sufficient to isolate the effect of the manipulated variable.
conditions: E involves deliberate manipulation of at least one variable
E is designed to test a specific hypothesis
E employs controls sufficient to isolate the effect of the manipulated variable
counterexample: Consider the astronomical observations carried out during a solar eclipse to test general relativity's prediction of the bending of starlight. These observations were designed to test a specific hypothesis. They employed comparison photographs of the star field taken when the sun was absent as a control. Yet no variable was deliberately manipulated — the eclipse, the starlight, and the gravitational field were entirely beyond human control. All that was manipulated was the direction of the telescope.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate observational tests in which the experimenter does not manipulate the variables of interest but instead exploits naturally occurring variations, a mode of enquiry common in astronomy, geology, and parts of biology.
passage: One might define a scientific experiment as a procedure involving the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable, designed to test a specific hypothesis, and employing adequate controls to isolate the effect of the manipulation. This definition captures the paradigmatic laboratory experiment admirably: the chemist who varies the concentration of a reagent, the biologist who withholds a nutrient from a treatment group. Yet there are cases in the history of science which reveal the insufficiency of this analysis. The celebrated observations made during the solar eclipse of 1919, undertaken to test the prediction of general relativity that starlight is bent by the sun's gravitational field, satisfied two of the three conditions: they were designed to test a specific hypothesis, and they employed comparison photographs of the same star field taken in the absence of the sun's interference. But no variable was deliberately manipulated by the observers. The eclipse was not produced by them; the gravitational field was not of their making; the starlight was not subject to their control. They positioned their telescopes and waited upon nature. If we insist that deliberate manipulation is essential to experiment, we must exclude this episode from the category of experiment — a conclusion that few philosophers of science would find palatable.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M produces a numerical value that purports to represent the magnitude of Q, (ii) M is repeatable in the sense that the same procedure applied to the same object under the same conditions yields the same value, and (iii) M employs an instrument that has been calibrated against a recognised standard.
conditions: M produces a numerical value purporting to represent the magnitude of Q
M is repeatable under the same conditions
M employs an instrument calibrated against a recognised standard
counterexample: Consider the measurement of the half-life of a single radioactive atom by observing when it decays. The observation produces a numerical value — the time elapsed before decay. The instrument (a Geiger counter or scintillation detector) has been calibrated against recognised standards. But the procedure is not repeatable: once the atom has decayed, it cannot be made to decay again, and a second atom of the same species will in general decay at a different time. Yet physicists would not hesitate to call this a measurement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for measurements of intrinsically stochastic quantities, where repeatability can only be understood in terms of the statistical distribution of outcomes rather than the reproduction of a single value.
passage: A natural analysis of measurement might require that the procedure produce a numerical value representing the quantity in question, that it be repeatable, and that the instrument be calibrated against a recognised standard. This captures much of what we ordinarily mean by measurement: the chemist's use of a calibrated balance to weigh a sample, the surveyor's use of a theodolite to measure an angle. Repeatability, in particular, seems essential — we distrust a measurement that cannot be reproduced. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in nuclear physics. When a physicist observes the decay of a single radioactive atom by means of a calibrated detector, she obtains a numerical value — the time at which the atom decayed. The instrument is properly calibrated. But the measurement is not repeatable in the required sense: the atom, once decayed, cannot be restored to its original state, and a different atom of the same isotope will in general yield a different decay time. It would be most peculiar to deny that the physicist has measured anything. What the definition overlooks is that some quantities are intrinsically stochastic, and for such quantities repeatability must be understood statistically — as convergence upon a distribution rather than upon a single value.

definition: A statement S counts as a prediction of a theory T if and only if (i) S describes a state of affairs that has not yet been observed, (ii) S is deducible from T together with a set of auxiliary assumptions, and (iii) the truth or falsity of S can in principle be ascertained by observation.
conditions: S describes a state of affairs not yet observed
S is deducible from T together with auxiliary assumptions
The truth or falsity of S is in principle ascertainable by observation
counterexample: Consider the case of Mendeleev's periodic table and the element gallium. Mendeleev described the properties of an as-yet-undiscovered element he called eka-aluminium. This description was deducible from the pattern of the periodic table together with auxiliary chemical principles. The properties could in principle be ascertained by observation. When gallium was discovered in 1875, its properties matched Mendeleev's description. Yet Mendeleev's statement was deducible equally from the observed periodic pattern before the theory was well articulated — it was arguably a sophisticated interpolation rather than a prediction in the full theoretical sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the prediction be novel with respect to the data that motivated the theory, as opposed to being implicit in the very regularities from which the theory was constructed.
passage: One might propose that a prediction of a theory is any statement describing an unobserved state of affairs that is deducible from the theory and whose truth is empirically ascertainable. This definition captures the standard cases tolerably well. When Newton's theory predicts the return of a comet at a specified date, the prediction describes an unobserved future event, is deducible from the theory, and is in principle verifiable by looking up at the sky. Yet there are cases which reveal the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider Mendeleev's famous description of the properties of eka-aluminium prior to the discovery of gallium. The description was deducible from the periodic arrangement of the elements and could be verified by the eventual isolation and examination of the element. All three conditions are satisfied. But the description was in a certain sense already implicit in the data from which the periodic table was constructed. Mendeleev interpolated from a known pattern rather than venturing beyond the evidence in any striking way. If we wish the notion of prediction to carry confirmatory weight — if successful predictions are to count in favour of a theory — we should arguably require that the prediction be genuinely novel, not merely an extraction of what was already contained in the evidence that gave rise to the theoretical framework.

definition: A system of categories C constitutes a natural classification if and only if (i) C is exhaustive, assigning every member of the relevant domain to at least one category, (ii) C is mutually exclusive, assigning no member to more than one category, and (iii) C groups members by properties that are observable and stable over time.
conditions: C is exhaustive over its domain
C is mutually exclusive
C groups members by observable and stable properties
counterexample: Consider the biological classification of organisms by observable morphological features — colour of plumage, shape of beak, and the like. Such a classification may be exhaustive and mutually exclusive, and it groups organisms by observable, temporally stable features. Yet modern systematists have shown that morphologically similar organisms may be only distantly related, while morphologically dissimilar organisms may be close kin. The duck and the grebe, for instance, share many superficial features but are now understood to belong to widely separated lineages.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the properties by which members are grouped reflect genuine causal or phylogenetic relationships rather than superficial resemblances, thereby omitting the distinction between natural and artificial classification.
passage: It is tempting to define a natural classification as one that is exhaustive, mutually exclusive, and based on observable, temporally stable properties. Such a definition appears to capture what we want: every specimen is classified, no specimen falls into two categories at once, and the basis of classification is plain to see and does not shift with the passage of time. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a classification of birds based solely upon observable morphological features — the shape of the beak, the colour and pattern of the plumage, the structure of the feet. Such a classification can be made exhaustive and exclusive, and morphological features are observable and relatively stable over the lifetime of an organism. Yet modern phylogenetic research has repeatedly shown that morphological similarity is an unreliable guide to natural affinity. The duck and the grebe, which share numerous superficial features, turn out upon molecular analysis to belong to widely separated lineages, while organisms of strikingly different appearance may prove to be close relatives. What the definition overlooks is the requirement that the properties grounding a natural classification should reflect genuine underlying causal or genealogical relationships, not merely surface resemblances.

definition: A representation M constitutes a scientific model of a system S if and only if (i) M shares structural features with S such that there is a mapping from elements of M to elements of S, (ii) M simplifies S by omitting certain details, and (iii) M is used by scientists for the purpose of understanding or predicting the behaviour of S.
conditions: M shares structural features with S via a mapping from elements of M to elements of S
M simplifies S by omitting certain details
M is used by scientists for understanding or predicting S
counterexample: Consider a map of the London Underground. The map shares structural features with the actual railway network — there is a mapping from coloured lines to rail lines and from dots to stations. It simplifies the network by omitting geographical distances and curvature. And it is used for the practical purpose of navigating the system. All three conditions are satisfied, yet one would hesitate to call the Tube map a scientific model. It is a navigational aid, not a representation intended for scientific understanding.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the structural correspondence between M and S be invoked to generate or test claims about the causal or nomological features of S, rather than serving merely practical or navigational ends.
passage: One might analyse the notion of a scientific model as any representation that shares structural features with a target system, simplifies that system by omitting details, and is employed by scientists for understanding or prediction. This analysis accommodates many central cases: the billiard-ball model of an ideal gas, the double-helix model of DNA, the Lotka-Volterra model of predator-prey dynamics. In each, there is a structural mapping, a deliberate simplification, and a scientific purpose. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. The well-known map of the London Underground satisfies all three conditions. It shares structural features with the railway network — there is a systematic mapping from coloured lines on the map to rail lines underground and from station dots to physical stations. It simplifies the actual geography drastically, omitting true distances and bends. And it is used — one might even say relied upon — for the practical purpose of understanding and predicting how to get from one station to another. Yet the Tube map is not a scientific model. It serves navigational purposes; it is not deployed to illuminate the causal structure of the transport system or to test hypotheses about railway dynamics. A further condition is wanted, requiring that the model be invoked in the service of causal or nomological enquiry.

definition: A set of propositions E constitutes a scientific explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) E contains at least one general law under which P may be subsumed, (ii) E includes statements of initial conditions from which, together with the law, P can be deduced, and (iii) the statements in E are empirically well-confirmed.
conditions: E contains at least one general law under which P may be subsumed
E includes statements of initial conditions from which, together with the law, P can be deduced
The statements in E are empirically well-confirmed
counterexample: Consider an astronomer who notes that a particular star is receding from us and explains this by subsuming it under the well-confirmed Hubble law — that distant galaxies recede at velocities proportional to their distance. The law is genuine, the initial conditions (the star's distance) are stated, and the deduction is valid. Yet the astronomer has explained why the star appears to recede by citing a regularity about recession, which is not to identify the mechanism — the expansion of the metric of space itself — that produces the recession. The explanation is formally correct by the definition's lights but fails to illuminate the operative process.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the explanation identify the mechanism or process by which the initial conditions and the law bring about P, rather than merely subsuming P under a covering regularity.
passage: One might propose that a scientific explanation of a phenomenon consists in a set of propositions that includes at least one general law under which the phenomenon may be subsumed, statements of initial conditions from which the phenomenon can be deduced in conjunction with the law, and the requirement that these propositions be empirically well-confirmed. This proposal has considerable merit: it insists upon nomological backing, it demands deductive rigour, and it excludes speculations that lack empirical support. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider explanations that are formally correct but mechanistically vacuous. An astronomer who explains the recession of a distant star by citing the Hubble law and the star's measured distance has satisfied all three conditions. The law is well-confirmed, the initial conditions are stated, and the deduction is valid. But the explanation does not tell us why the star recedes — it does not identify the expansion of the spatial metric as the operative process. The covering-law structure has been provided, but no mechanism has been disclosed. One might observe that genuine scientific explanations characteristically identify the process by which the phenomenon is produced, and that the definition, by requiring only subsumption under a law, permits explanations that are formally adequate but causally uninformative.

definition: A generalisation G qualifies as a scientific law if and only if (i) G is universally true without exception, (ii) G has been confirmed by repeated observation under controlled conditions, and (iii) G is expressible in precise mathematical form.
conditions: G is universally true without exception
G has been confirmed by repeated observation under controlled conditions
G is expressible in precise mathematical form
counterexample: Consider the biological generalisation that all organisms are composed of cells. This generalisation is universally true and has been confirmed by vast numbers of observations. Yet it is not expressible in precise mathematical form — it is a structural principle, not a quantitative relation. Biologists would not hesitate to call it a fundamental law of biology, yet condition (iii) excludes it from the category of scientific law.
passage: It appears natural to propose that a scientific law is a generalisation that is universally true, confirmed by repeated controlled observation, and expressible in precise mathematical form. This definition accommodates the paradigmatic laws of physics — Newton's laws of motion, the gas laws, Maxwell's equations — with admirable tidiness. Each is universally true (within its domain), confirmed by experiment, and stated in the language of mathematics. Yet the definition encounters difficulty when we turn from physics to the biological sciences. The generalisation that all organisms are composed of cells — the cell theory — is universally true in the biological domain. It has been confirmed by innumerable observations under every variety of condition. But it resists mathematical formulation; it is a structural and compositional principle rather than a quantitative relationship. The requirement of mathematical expressibility, which seemed so natural when the paradigmatic cases were drawn from physics, proves too restrictive when applied to the sciences at large. One might observe that scientific laws in biology, ecology, and geology are often qualitative or structural in character, and that a definition which excludes them on grounds of mathematical form confuses a frequent feature of physical laws with a necessary condition of lawhood itself.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T provides a unified account of a range of phenomena within a specified domain, (ii) T generates testable predictions that extend beyond the phenomena already observed, and (iii) T is internally consistent.
conditions: T provides a unified account of a range of phenomena within a specified domain
T generates testable predictions extending beyond phenomena already observed
T is internally consistent
counterexample: Consider Bohr's 1913 model of the hydrogen atom, which combined classical mechanics with the ad hoc postulate that electrons occupy only certain quantised orbits. The model unified a range of spectral phenomena, generated predictions about previously unobserved spectral lines, and was internally consistent. Yet the model was known to rest upon an incoherent foundation — it applied classical electrodynamics selectively, invoking it when convenient and suspending it when not. Physicists treated it as an indispensable heuristic rather than a genuine theory, precisely because the conceptual foundations were recognised as provisional and unsatisfactory.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the propositions composing T rest upon a coherent conceptual foundation, rather than upon ad hoc postulates adopted solely to accommodate the data.
passage: One might define a scientific theory as a body of propositions that provides a unified account of phenomena within a domain, generates testable predictions beyond what has been observed, and is internally consistent. This is a plausible and economical proposal. It captures the predictive ambition of genuine theories, their unifying power, and their logical coherence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by Bohr's early model of the hydrogen atom. The model unified an impressive range of spectral data — the Balmer series, the Lyman series, and others — under a single framework. It generated predictions about spectral lines that had not yet been observed, and these predictions were subsequently confirmed. It was internally consistent in the narrow logical sense that no proposition within it contradicted any other. All three conditions are met. Yet physicists of the period were acutely aware that the model rested upon an unsatisfactory foundation. It applied classical electrodynamics to the electron's orbital motion but arbitrarily suspended the classical requirement that accelerating charges radiate energy. This suspension was not derived from any deeper principle but was simply postulated to save the phenomena. The model functioned as an indispensable heuristic, but it was widely recognised as something less than a genuine theory, precisely because its conceptual foundations were ad hoc. The definition, by requiring only consistency, unity, and predictive reach, omits the requirement that a theory's principles be grounded in a coherent conceptual framework rather than adopted as convenient expedients.

definition: A report R constitutes a scientific observation if and only if (i) R describes a perceptible state of affairs, (ii) R is produced under conditions that allow independent verification, and (iii) R is expressed in terms that are free from theoretical presuppositions.
conditions: R describes a perceptible state of affairs
R is produced under conditions that allow independent verification
R is expressed in terms free from theoretical presuppositions
counterexample: Consider the observation, routinely recorded by particle physicists, that a muon has been detected in a cloud chamber. The report describes a perceptible state of affairs — a visible track in the chamber — and the conditions allow independent verification. Yet the report is saturated with theoretical presuppositions: the identification of the track as a muon depends upon theories of particle decay, electromagnetic interaction, and detector response. The third condition is violated, yet particle physicists would insist that this is a perfectly legitimate scientific observation.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to recognise that scientific observations may be legitimately theory-laden, provided that the theoretical presuppositions involved are well-confirmed and do not beg the question under investigation.
passage: It appears reasonable to propose that a scientific observation is a report that describes a perceptible state of affairs, is produced under conditions allowing independent verification, and is expressed in terms free from theoretical presuppositions. The aspiration underlying this definition is clear: it seeks to identify a stratum of empirical report that is neutral between competing theories and can therefore serve as an impartial arbiter of theoretical disputes. Yet the definition encounters a serious difficulty when applied to actual scientific practice. When a particle physicist records that a muon has been detected in a cloud chamber, the report describes something perceptible — there is a visible track in the chamber. The conditions are such that another physicist could independently verify the observation. But the identification of that track as a muon is dependent upon a considerable body of theory: the physicist must presuppose theories of particle decay, of electromagnetic interaction with the chamber medium, and of detector response. To express the observation in terms entirely free from theoretical presuppositions would be to say only that a track of a certain shape and length has appeared — which would strip the observation of its scientific content. One might observe that the demand for theory-free observation, however appealing in the abstract, does not correspond to the way in which observation actually functions in the sciences, where theory-laden reports are not only common but indispensable.

definition: A scientific theory T1 is reducible to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every term in T1 can be connected to terms in T2 by means of bridge laws, (ii) every law of T1 is derivable from T2 together with the bridge laws, and (iii) T2 is a more fundamental theory than T1 in the sense that T2 concerns a lower level of organisation.
conditions: Every term in T1 can be connected to terms in T2 by bridge laws
Every law of T1 is derivable from T2 together with the bridge laws
T2 concerns a more fundamental level of organisation
counterexample: Consider the relationship between thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. The temperature of a gas can be connected, via a bridge law, to the mean kinetic energy of its molecules, and the laws of thermodynamics can be approximately derived from statistical mechanics. Yet the second law of thermodynamics — that entropy does not decrease in an isolated system — is not strictly derivable from the time-reversible laws of molecular mechanics; it holds only statistically and admits of fluctuations at the microscopic level. Condition (ii) is not fully satisfied, yet the reduction of thermodynamics to statistical mechanics is one of the most celebrated achievements of theoretical physics.
missing_condition: The definition requires strict derivability of every law, but in practice the most important reductions are approximate, holding in certain limits or with high probability rather than as strict logical consequences.
passage: One might propose that a theory is reducible to a more fundamental theory if every term in the first can be connected to terms in the second by bridge laws, every law of the first is derivable from the second together with the bridge laws, and the second concerns a lower level of organisation. This proposal captures what many philosophers have taken to be the ideal of inter-theoretic reduction: the higher-level theory is shown to be a logical consequence of the lower-level theory, once the appropriate term-by-term translations are in place. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this ideal is provided by the relationship between thermodynamics and statistical mechanics — arguably the most successful reduction in the history of physics. Temperature can indeed be connected to mean molecular kinetic energy, and most thermodynamic laws can be derived, at least approximately, from statistical mechanics. But the second law of thermodynamics presents a difficulty. The underlying molecular dynamics are time-reversible, and statistical mechanics can derive only that entropy increase is overwhelmingly probable, not that it is strictly necessary. Fluctuations — brief decreases in entropy — are permitted by the molecular theory, though they are vanishingly rare for macroscopic systems. The law of entropy increase is not derivable as a strict logical consequence of statistical mechanics; it holds approximately, in the thermodynamic limit, and with extremely high probability. The definition demands strict derivability and thereby excludes the very reduction that should stand as its paradigm. One might observe that the most important reductions in science are characteristically approximate, and that a definition requiring strict derivation mistakes the idealised textbook case for a necessary condition.

definition: A procedure P constitutes a scientific experiment if and only if (i) P involves the deliberate manipulation of one or more variables, (ii) P is conducted under conditions that control for confounding factors, and (iii) the results of P are reproducible by other investigators following the same procedure.
conditions: P involves the deliberate manipulation of one or more variables
P is conducted under conditions that control for confounding factors
The results of P are reproducible by other investigators
counterexample: Consider the astronomical observations made during a total solar eclipse to test general relativity's prediction of light bending. The astronomer does not manipulate any variable — the sun, the stars, and the eclipse are beyond human control. The conditions are not controlled in the usual sense; the astronomer must accept whatever atmospheric conditions prevail. Yet such observations are universally regarded as among the most important experiments in the history of physics.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate natural experiments and observational tests in which nature provides the relevant conditions without human manipulation, yet which are nevertheless treated by scientists as genuine experiments.
passage: It seems natural to define a scientific experiment as a procedure involving the deliberate manipulation of variables, conducted under controlled conditions, with reproducible results. This definition captures the logic of the controlled laboratory experiment admirably — one varies a single factor while holding all others constant, and one repeats the procedure to ensure reliability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the eclipse observations that tested Einstein's general theory of relativity. In 1919, Eddington and his collaborators observed the positions of stars near the sun during a total solar eclipse, comparing them with the positions of the same stars when the sun was absent from the field. No variable was deliberately manipulated: the sun's gravitational field, the positions of the stars, and the occurrence of the eclipse were entirely beyond human control. The conditions were not controlled in the laboratory sense; the astronomers were at the mercy of the weather and the brevity of totality. Yet these observations are universally regarded as constituting one of the decisive experiments of modern physics. One might observe that the definition, by insisting upon deliberate manipulation and controlled conditions, excludes a class of observational tests — natural experiments, as they are sometimes called — that play an indispensable role in those sciences where the phenomena of interest are not subject to human control. The definition confuses a feature of laboratory science with a necessary condition of experimental inquiry as such.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M assigns a numerical value to Q, (ii) the assignment is produced by a causal interaction between the measuring instrument and the system possessing Q, and (iii) the numerical value is expressed in a standard unit that allows comparison with other measurements of Q.
conditions: M assigns a numerical value to Q
The assignment is produced by a causal interaction between instrument and system
The numerical value is expressed in a standard unit
counterexample: Consider the measurement of the distance to a remote galaxy by means of the redshift of its spectral lines. A numerical value is assigned — the distance in megaparsecs. The value is expressed in a standard unit. Yet the 'causal interaction' between the measuring instrument (the telescope and spectrograph) and the quantity being measured (the distance) is indirect in the extreme; the instrument interacts with light, not with distance, and the distance is inferred via a chain of theoretical reasoning involving the Hubble relation and the cosmological model. Condition (ii) is at best tenuously satisfied, yet astrophysicists regard such measurements as perfectly legitimate.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to accommodate measurements in which the quantity of interest is not directly causally engaged by the instrument but is instead inferred from quantities that are so engaged, provided the inference rests upon well-confirmed theoretical relationships.
passage: One might propose that a measurement consists in assigning a numerical value to a quantity through a causal interaction between the measuring instrument and the system, with the value expressed in a standard unit. This seems a straightforward and uncontroversial analysis. When one places a thermometer in a liquid, the mercury column responds causally to the temperature; when one places an object on a balance, the arm responds causally to the mass. The causal interaction ensures that the measurement is of the quantity in question, not of some other quantity, and the standard unit ensures comparability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the measurement of the distance to a remote galaxy. The astrophysicist obtains a spectrum, identifies the redshift of known spectral lines, and applies the Hubble relation to infer a distance in megaparsecs. The instrument — the telescope and spectrograph — interacts causally with the light from the galaxy, but it does not interact causally with the distance. The distance is not a property that can reach out and deflect a needle or raise a column of mercury; it is inferred from the redshift via a chain of theoretical reasoning that involves the Hubble constant, the cosmological model, and assumptions about the source of the spectral lines. The definition requires a direct causal interaction between instrument and quantity, but modern science abounds in measurements where the quantity of interest is reached only indirectly, through theory-mediated inference from quantities that are directly measured. One should hesitate to deny that these are genuine measurements merely because the causal chain is indirect and theoretically mediated.

definition: A statement S is a scientific prediction if and only if (i) S is derived from a theory or hypothesis by deductive or mathematical reasoning, (ii) S concerns an observable state of affairs that has not yet been observed, and (iii) S specifies the conditions under which the predicted state of affairs will obtain.
conditions: S is derived from a theory by deductive or mathematical reasoning
S concerns an observable state of affairs not yet observed
S specifies the conditions under which the predicted state of affairs will obtain
counterexample: Consider Darwin's prediction that transitional fossils linking major groups of organisms would be discovered as the geological record was more fully explored. This prediction was derived from the theory of evolution by natural selection, and it concerned states of affairs not yet observed. Yet Darwin could not specify the conditions under which such fossils would be found — he could not say in which stratum, in which geographical region, or at what date the discoveries would occur. The third condition is not met, yet the prediction was of immense scientific significance and its subsequent fulfilment was widely regarded as confirming the theory.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the conditions of fulfilment be specified in advance, but many important scientific predictions are existential rather than conditional — they predict that something exists or will be found without specifying when, where, or under what precise conditions.
passage: One might define a scientific prediction as a statement derived from a theory by deductive reasoning, concerning an observable state of affairs not yet observed, that specifies the conditions under which the predicted state of affairs will obtain. This definition captures a great deal of what we expect from predictions in the physical sciences, where the value of a prediction often lies precisely in its specificity — it tells us not merely that something will happen but when and under what circumstances. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is Darwin's prediction of transitional fossils. The theory of evolution by natural selection entails that the major groups of organisms are connected by ancestral forms, and Darwin predicted that the geological record would eventually yield fossils exhibiting transitional characteristics. This prediction was derived from the theory, and it concerned states of affairs that had not been observed. But Darwin was quite unable to specify the conditions under which the transitional fossils would be found: he could not name the stratum, the continent, or the century. The prediction was existential in character — it asserted that something existed, without specifying the precise conditions of its discovery. Yet when Archaeopteryx was found in the Solnhofen limestone in 1861, and when many further transitional forms were subsequently unearthed, the predictions were widely regarded as having been fulfilled. The definition, by demanding that the conditions of fulfilment be specified, excludes existential predictions that are of the greatest scientific importance.

definition: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C groups entities by shared properties that are causally significant, (ii) the categories in C are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive, and (iii) C is stable in the sense that new discoveries do not require wholesale revision of the categories.
conditions: C groups entities by causally significant shared properties
Categories are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive
C is stable under new discoveries
counterexample: Consider the Linnaean system of biological classification, which groups organisms into hierarchical taxa — species, genera, families, and so forth. The system groups entities by shared morphological properties that are, in many cases, causally significant. It is reasonably stable. Yet the categories are not mutually exclusive in the way the definition requires: the phenomenon of hybridisation means that certain organisms possess the characteristics of two distinct species and cannot be unambiguously assigned to either. Ring species present a further difficulty, in which populations at the ends of a geographic chain cannot interbreed though each can breed with its neighbour. The second condition is violated, yet the Linnaean system is the foundation of biological classification.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that scientifically adequate classification systems may tolerate borderline cases and overlapping categories, particularly in the biological sciences where variation is continuous and boundaries are intrinsically fuzzy.
passage: It appears reasonable to require that a scientifically adequate classification group entities by causally significant shared properties, that its categories be mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive, and that it be stable under new discoveries. This is a tidy and appealing set of requirements. Mutual exclusivity ensures that every entity belongs to exactly one category; exhaustiveness ensures that nothing is left out; causal significance ensures that the classification tracks something real in nature; and stability ensures that the system is not rendered useless by every new finding. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this set of requirements is provided by the Linnaean system of biological classification. The Linnaean hierarchy groups organisms into species, genera, families, and so forth, largely on the basis of morphological resemblance, which in many cases reflects genuine causal and phylogenetic relationships. The system has proved remarkably stable. But the requirement of mutual exclusivity runs into difficulty. Hybridisation between species is not an anomaly but a widespread phenomenon, especially among plants: certain organisms share the characteristics of two species and resist unambiguous assignment to either. Ring species present a further complication, in which a chain of populations encircles a geographic barrier, each population able to interbreed with its neighbours, yet the populations at the two ends of the chain unable to interbreed with each other. The neat boundaries demanded by mutual exclusivity dissolve. One might observe that biological nature is characterised by continuous variation and fuzzy boundaries, and that a definition which insists upon mutually exclusive categories mistakes the idealisations of logic for the realities of nature.

definition: A representation M is a scientific model of a target system S if and only if (i) M preserves the structural relations among the relevant elements of S, (ii) M enables the derivation of predictions about S that can be empirically tested, and (iii) M is constructed with the intention of representing S.
conditions: M preserves the structural relations among the relevant elements of S
M enables the derivation of testable predictions about S
M is constructed with the intention of representing S
counterexample: Consider a model of population growth in ecology that assumes unlimited resources and no predation — the simple exponential growth model. This model does not preserve the structural relations among the relevant elements of an actual ecosystem, which include resource limitation, predation, disease, and competition. Yet it enables the derivation of predictions about short-term growth that can be empirically tested, and it is certainly constructed with the intention of representing population dynamics. Scientists regard it as a useful model precisely because it deliberately distorts the structural relations in order to isolate a single factor.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to allow for models that are scientifically valuable precisely because they deliberately idealise or distort the structural relations of the target system in order to isolate the effect of a single variable or mechanism.
passage: One might propose that a scientific model is a representation that preserves the structural relations among the relevant elements of a target system, enables the derivation of testable predictions, and is constructed with the intention of representing that system. This proposal captures a natural expectation: a good model should mirror the structure of the thing it represents, generate predictions, and be aimed at the right target. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the exponential growth model in ecology. This model assumes that a population grows without limit at a rate proportional to its current size. It ignores resource limitation, predation, disease, migration, and every other factor that constrains real populations. The structural relations among the relevant elements of an actual ecosystem — the interactions between predators and prey, the dependence of growth on resource availability — are not preserved but deliberately suppressed. Yet the model enables testable predictions about short-term growth rates, and it is constructed with the clear intention of representing population dynamics. Ecologists regard it as a valuable and important model. Its value lies precisely in its distortion of the structural relations: by stripping away every complicating factor, it isolates the effect of reproductive rate alone and thereby reveals something that the full complexity of the ecosystem obscures. The definition, by requiring structural preservation, cannot accommodate models whose scientific utility derives from deliberate idealisation and simplification.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites a general law or regularity under which E can be subsumed, (ii) A specifies the antecedent conditions from which, conjoined with the law, E follows deductively, and (iii) the general law cited in A is itself empirically well-confirmed.
conditions: A cites a general law or regularity under which E can be subsumed
A specifies the antecedent conditions from which, conjoined with the law, E follows deductively
The general law cited in A is itself empirically well-confirmed
counterexample: Consider a physicist who explains why a certain pendulum has a period of two seconds by citing the well-confirmed law relating period to length and gravitational acceleration, together with the measured length of the pendulum. The deduction is valid and the law well-confirmed. Yet precisely the same law and the same pendulum length could be used to deduce the gravitational acceleration from the period, reversing the direction of explanation without altering the deductive structure. The definition cannot distinguish the genuine explanation from the mere re-derivation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the explanatory account reflect the direction of causal or productive dependence, so that one can distinguish explanations from symmetric deductions that run counter to the causal order.
passage: One might propose that an explanation of an event consists in an account that cites a general law under which the event can be subsumed, specifies the antecedent conditions from which the event follows deductively in conjunction with the law, and requires that the law be empirically well-confirmed. This proposal has evident virtues: it ties explanation to the nomological structure of science and it excludes speculative accounts unsupported by evidence. Yet one should hesitate to regard it as fully adequate. A case which reveals the insufficiency of the proposal arises when we consider the explanation of a pendulum's period. A physicist who cites the law relating period to the square root of length divided by gravitational acceleration, together with the measured length, can deduce the observed period. All three conditions are met. But the very same law permits one to deduce the gravitational acceleration from the period and the length, and in this reverse derivation the conditions are equally satisfied. We should not wish to say that the period explains the gravitational acceleration, for it appears that the causal dependence runs in the opposite direction. The difficulty is that the definition, by attending only to deductive structure and empirical confirmation, takes no account of the asymmetry inherent in causal or productive relations.

definition: A statement L is a law of nature if and only if (i) L is a true universal generalisation, (ii) L contains no reference to particular individuals, places, or times, and (iii) L supports counterfactual conditionals.
conditions: L is a true universal generalisation
L contains no reference to particular individuals, places, or times
L supports counterfactual conditionals
counterexample: Consider the statement that all rivers on Earth flow toward the sea. This is a true universal generalisation about rivers, it makes no reference to particular individuals or times, and it appears to support the counterfactual that if a new river were to form, it would flow toward the sea. Yet we should hesitate to call it a law of nature, for it is a consequence of the contingent topography of the Earth's surface rather than a fundamental regularity of nature. It is, as it were, a cosmic coincidence elevated by its generality to the appearance of lawfulness.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the generalisation hold not merely as a matter of contingent fact but in virtue of some underlying nomic necessity or physical structure that makes its truth non-accidental.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a law of nature is simply a true universal generalisation that contains no reference to particular individuals, places, or times and that supports counterfactual reasoning. This account captures much of what we ordinarily mean by the notion: the law of gravitation, for instance, is a universal generalisation, free of particular reference, and it supports such conditionals as that if a body were released near the Earth's surface it would accelerate downward. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed as follows. The statement that all rivers on Earth flow toward the sea satisfies all three conditions. It is a true universal generalisation about rivers. It makes no essential reference to particular named rivers or to particular dates. And it appears to sustain the counterfactual conditional that if a new river were to form upon the Earth's surface, it too would flow toward the sea. Nevertheless, one might observe that this statement lacks the character of a genuine law. It owes its truth to the contingent topography of the planet's surface, not to any deep physical necessity. Were the topography different, rivers might flow into enclosed basins and never reach the sea. The definition, by requiring only universality, generality, and counterfactual support, cannot distinguish the genuinely nomic from the merely accidentally universal.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is internally consistent, (ii) T entails observable consequences that have been confirmed by experiment, and (iii) T is expressible in precise, preferably mathematical, language.
conditions: T is internally consistent
T entails observable consequences that have been confirmed by experiment
T is expressible in precise, preferably mathematical, language
counterexample: Consider Ptolemaic astronomy as refined by the addition of epicycles. The system is internally consistent; it entails observable consequences regarding the positions of planets that were, for centuries, confirmed with tolerable accuracy by astronomical observation; and it is expressed in precise mathematical language. Yet one should hesitate to regard the fully elaborated Ptolemaic system as a genuine scientific theory, for its epicyclic machinery is devised solely to save the phenomena and does not purport to describe the actual structure of the heavens.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the propositions of the theory purport to describe or represent the actual structure of the domain in question, rather than merely serving as a computational device for predicting observations.
passage: One might define a scientific theory as a body of propositions that is internally consistent, that entails observable consequences confirmed by experiment, and that is expressed in precise, preferably mathematical, language. Such a definition has the merit of insisting upon rigour and empirical adequacy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the Ptolemaic astronomical system in its fully elaborated form, with its deferents, epicycles, and equant points. This system satisfies all three conditions admirably. It is internally consistent, for its geometrical constructions do not contradict one another. It entails observable consequences — the positions of the planets at specified times — that were confirmed by centuries of careful observation. And it is expressed in the most precise mathematical language available to its era. Yet it appears doubtful whether we should call it a genuine theory in the fullest sense. The epicyclic machinery is devised precisely so as to yield the observed positions; it does not purport to describe the actual arrangement of the heavens. One might observe that a genuine scientific theory characteristically aims to represent the real structure of the phenomena it addresses, and that a definition which omits this aspiration toward representational fidelity will fail to distinguish theories from mere calculating instruments.

definition: A report R is an observation statement if and only if (i) R describes a state of affairs directly accessible to the senses, (ii) R can be verified or refuted by any competent observer placed in the appropriate circumstances, and (iii) the truth or falsity of R does not depend upon the acceptance of any scientific theory.
conditions: R describes a state of affairs directly accessible to the senses
R can be verified or refuted by any competent observer placed in the appropriate circumstances
The truth or falsity of R does not depend upon the acceptance of any scientific theory
counterexample: Consider the statement that a certain liquid in a cloud chamber contains a trail produced by a positron. A trained physicist, looking at the chamber, sees the characteristic curved trail and reports what she observes. The trail is directly accessible to the senses, and any competent physicist placed before the chamber would concur. Yet whether what one sees is a positron trail depends upon one's acceptance of the theory of subatomic particles and electromagnetic interactions. Without that theoretical framework, one sees merely a thin white line in a fluid.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the identification of many observable states of affairs is itself theory-laden, so that the truth of the observation statement may depend upon theoretical commitments even when the sensory experience is shared.
passage: It is tempting to characterise an observation statement as a report that describes a state of affairs directly accessible to the senses, that can be verified by any competent observer in the appropriate circumstances, and whose truth does not depend upon the acceptance of any scientific theory. This characterisation is intended to provide science with a secure, theory-independent foundation of empirical fact. Yet one should hesitate to accept it without reservation. A case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the cloud-chamber observations familiar to particle physics. A physicist who peers into a cloud chamber and reports that she observes the trail of a positron describes something that is, in one sense, directly accessible to the senses — she sees a thin, curved white line in a supersaturated vapour. Any competent physicist placed before the same chamber would concur in the report. But whether what is observed is correctly described as a positron trail depends upon one's acceptance of a substantial body of physical theory concerning subatomic particles, their charges, and their interactions with magnetic fields. Without that theory, one sees merely a white line. The observation statement, it appears, is not independent of theory after all, and the definition, by requiring such independence, fails to accommodate the theory-ladenness that pervades actual scientific observation.

definition: A procedure P constitutes a scientific experiment if and only if (i) P involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable, (ii) P is designed to test a specific hypothesis, and (iii) P is reproducible by other investigators following the same procedure.
conditions: P involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable
P is designed to test a specific hypothesis
P is reproducible by other investigators following the same procedure
counterexample: Consider the astronomical observations conducted during a total solar eclipse to test the prediction of general relativity that light is deflected by the sun's gravitational field. The procedure is designed to test a specific hypothesis, and it is reproducible in the sense that other astronomers can make the same observations during subsequent eclipses. Yet no variable is deliberately manipulated: the astronomer merely waits for the eclipse and measures the apparent positions of stars. The procedure satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) but not (i), and yet it would be perverse to deny it the title of experiment.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring deliberate manipulation, thereby excluding natural experiments and observational tests that exploit naturally occurring variations to test hypotheses with the same rigour as interventionist experiments.
passage: A common account of scientific experimentation holds that a procedure constitutes an experiment if and only if it involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable, is designed to test a specific hypothesis, and is reproducible by other investigators. This account captures the essential features of the controlled laboratory experiment, which is rightly regarded as the paradigm of scientific method. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises in the domain of astronomy. The observations conducted during total solar eclipses to test the general-relativistic prediction of light deflection are designed with great care to test a specific hypothesis — that starlight passing near the sun will be deflected by a calculable amount. The observations are reproducible, in the sense that other astronomers can repeat them during subsequent eclipses using the same instruments and methods. Yet no variable is deliberately manipulated. The astronomer does not move the sun, alter the path of the starlight, or adjust the gravitational field. She merely positions her instruments and waits for the natural event. One might observe that such procedures are among the most important experiments in the history of physics, and that a definition which excludes them by insisting upon deliberate manipulation is too narrow to serve as an adequate analysis of scientific experimentation.

definition: A statement S is a genuine scientific prediction if and only if (i) S is derived from a theory T prior to the observation of the predicted phenomenon, (ii) S specifies observable conditions under which the predicted phenomenon will occur, and (iii) the derivation of S from T is deductively valid.
conditions: S is derived from a theory T prior to the observation of the predicted phenomenon
S specifies observable conditions under which the predicted phenomenon will occur
The derivation of S from T is deductively valid
counterexample: Consider Mendeleev's prediction of the existence and properties of gallium, derived from his periodic table before the element was discovered. The prediction was derived from the theory prior to observation, it specified observable properties such as density and melting point, and the derivation was, in the relevant sense, valid. Yet Mendeleev's periodic table was an empirical classification rather than a deductive system; the derivation of gallium's properties proceeded not by strict deduction but by analogical reasoning from the properties of known elements in adjacent positions. The prediction was genuine and strikingly successful, yet it fails condition (iii) on a strict reading.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring deductive validity, thereby excluding predictions that are derived by well-grounded analogical or inductive reasoning from theoretical frameworks that are not themselves axiomatised deductive systems.
passage: One might propose that a statement counts as a genuine scientific prediction if and only if it is derived from a theory prior to the observation of the predicted phenomenon, it specifies the observable conditions under which the phenomenon will occur, and its derivation from the theory is deductively valid. This proposal has the merit of tying prediction closely to the logical structure of theory and of distinguishing genuine predictions from post hoc accommodations. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by Mendeleev's celebrated prediction of the element gallium. Mendeleev, working from his periodic classification of the elements, predicted the existence of an as-yet-undiscovered element with specific physical and chemical properties — a certain density, a certain melting point, a certain valence. The element was subsequently discovered and found to possess precisely the predicted properties. The prediction was derived from the theory prior to observation and it specified observable conditions with remarkable precision. Yet the periodic table is not an axiomatised deductive system, and the derivation of gallium's properties proceeded not by strict deduction but by analogical reasoning from the properties of neighbouring elements. It appears that the definition, by requiring deductive validity, excludes some of the most impressive predictions in the history of science.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M assigns a numerical value to Q, (ii) the assignment is reproducible under the same conditions, and (iii) the numerical value assigned bears a determinate relation to a standard unit.
conditions: M assigns a numerical value to Q
The assignment is reproducible under the same conditions
The numerical value assigned bears a determinate relation to a standard unit
counterexample: Consider the Mohs hardness scale, by which minerals are ranked according to which can scratch which. Talc is assigned the value 1, diamond the value 10, and other minerals receive intermediate values. The assignment is reproducible, and each value bears a determinate relation to the standard minerals that define the scale. Yet the intervals between the values are not equal — the difference in hardness between 9 and 10 is vastly greater than the difference between 1 and 2.
