The procedure assigns numbers, but the numbers do not represent the quantity in the manner one expects of genuine measurement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the numerical values assigned preserve the quantitative structure of the property being measured — specifically, that equal differences in the assigned values correspond to equal differences in the property.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a measurement of a quantity consists in a procedure that assigns a numerical value to the quantity, that the assignment is reproducible under the same conditions, and that the value bears a determinate relation to a standard unit. This account seems to capture the essential features of paradigmatic measurements — one measures a length by comparing it with a standard rod, assigns a number, and can reproduce the result. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers the Mohs hardness scale. By this procedure, minerals are ranked according to their ability to scratch one another, and each mineral is assigned a numerical value from 1 to 10. The assignment is reproducible — any competent mineralogist will obtain the same ranking — and each value is defined by reference to a standard mineral. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the Mohs scale does not measure hardness in the way that a ruler measures length. The intervals between successive values are wildly unequal: the difference in actual hardness between corundum (9) and diamond (10) is greater than the difference between talc (1) and corundum (9). The numerical values rank the minerals without representing the quantitative structure of the property. It appears that genuine measurement requires something more than the assignment of reproducible numbers relative to a standard.

definition: A representation M is a scientific model of a system S if and only if (i) M shares structural features with S, (ii) M is simpler than S in respects relevant to the inquiry, and (iii) M permits the derivation of predictions about the behaviour of S.
conditions: M shares structural features with S
M is simpler than S in respects relevant to the inquiry
M permits the derivation of predictions about the behaviour of S
counterexample: Consider a toy model in condensed-matter physics — the Ising model of ferromagnetism — in which magnetic spins are arranged on a lattice and interact only with their nearest neighbours. The model shares certain structural features with real ferromagnets, is far simpler than any actual magnetic material, and permits the derivation of predictions about phase transitions. Yet the Ising model deliberately misrepresents the system in important respects: it reduces continuous spin orientations to a binary choice and ignores long-range interactions. The model's scientific utility depends precisely upon these deliberate distortions, which are not mere simplifications but purposeful falsehoods.
missing_condition: The definition fails to acknowledge that a scientific model may derive its utility not merely from simplification but from deliberate and strategic misrepresentation of the target system, so that the model's departures from the system are not defects but constitutive features of its explanatory role.
passage: One might define a scientific model as a representation that shares structural features with its target system, is simpler than the system in respects relevant to the inquiry, and permits the derivation of predictions about the system's behaviour. This definition captures much of what is ordinarily meant by the term. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the Ising model of ferromagnetism. In this model, magnetic spins are arranged upon a regular lattice and each spin is constrained to point either up or down, interacting only with its nearest neighbours. The model shares certain structural features with real ferromagnets, is vastly simpler than any actual magnetic material, and yields striking predictions about phase transitions. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the Ising model's scientific value depends not merely upon its simplicity but upon its deliberate misrepresentation of the target. Real spins are not binary but continuous; real interactions are not confined to nearest neighbours. These are not merely simplifications — the omission of irrelevant detail — but purposeful distortions that enable the model to isolate the features responsible for the phenomenon of interest. It appears that the definition, by speaking only of shared structure and simplification, fails to recognise the constitutive role that strategic misrepresentation plays in scientific modelling.

definition: A system C constitutes a natural classification of objects in a domain D if and only if (i) C partitions D into mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive classes, (ii) the members of each class share a set of properties that distinguish them from members of other classes, and (iii) the classification is stable under the addition of newly discovered objects.
conditions: C partitions D into mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive classes
Members of each class share a set of distinguishing properties
The classification is stable under the addition of newly discovered objects
counterexample: Consider the classification of chemical elements into metals and non-metals. This classification partitions the elements into two mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive classes. Members of each class share distinguishing properties — metals are typically lustrous, conductive, and malleable; non-metals are not. The classification has proven stable as new elements have been discovered. Yet the existence of metalloids — elements such as silicon and arsenic that exhibit properties of both metals and non-metals — reveals that the boundary between the classes is not sharp but admits of borderline cases that resist unambiguous assignment.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the boundaries between classes be determinate, so that every object in the domain can be unambiguously assigned to exactly one class without resort to stipulation or convention.
passage: A natural classification of a domain of objects might be defined as a system that partitions the domain into mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive classes, whose members share distinguishing properties, and that remains stable as new objects are discovered. Such a definition captures the desiderata that guide taxonomic practice in the sciences. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the classification of chemical elements into metals and non-metals. This familiar division partitions the elements exhaustively — every element is either a metal or a non-metal — and the members of each class share recognisable properties: metals are lustrous, conductive, and ductile, whilst non-metals are dull, insulating, and brittle. The classification has proven stable as the periodic table has grown. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the existence of the metalloids reveals a difficulty. Silicon, arsenic, and germanium exhibit some properties characteristic of metals and some characteristic of non-metals. They resist unambiguous assignment to either class. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only mutual exclusivity and shared properties, presupposes that the boundaries between natural classes are sharp, when in fact natural domains frequently exhibit continuous gradation. A satisfactory account of natural classification, it appears, must address the possibility of indeterminate boundaries.

definition: A theory T1 is reducible to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every term of T1 can be defined by means of terms of T2, (ii) every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2, and (iii) T2 is more fundamental than T1 in the sense that it applies to a wider domain of phenomena.
conditions: Every term of T1 can be defined by means of terms of T2
Every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2
T2 is more fundamental than T1 in the sense of applying to a wider domain
counterexample: Consider the putative reduction of classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. Temperature, a term of thermodynamics, is defined in statistical mechanics as mean molecular kinetic energy. The ideal gas law can be derived from statistical-mechanical principles. Statistical mechanics applies to a wider domain. Yet the second law of thermodynamics, which states that entropy never decreases in an isolated system, cannot be strictly derived from statistical mechanics; it can only be recovered as an overwhelmingly probable generalisation. The reduction satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but falters on condition (ii).
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate approximate or statistical reduction, in which the laws of the reduced theory are not strictly derivable from the reducing theory but are recoverable as very good approximations holding with overwhelming probability.
passage: One might propose that one theory is reducible to another if and only if every term of the first can be defined in terms of the second, every law of the first can be derived from the laws of the second, and the second applies to a wider domain. This proposal captures the paradigmatic cases of theoretical reduction in their idealised form. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the relation between classical thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. The terms of thermodynamics can indeed be defined in statistical-mechanical terms — temperature as mean kinetic energy, pressure as mean momentum transfer, and so forth. Statistical mechanics does apply to a wider domain, encompassing both equilibrium and non-equilibrium phenomena. Yet when one attempts to derive the second law of thermodynamics — the law that the entropy of an isolated system never decreases — from the principles of statistical mechanics, one discovers that strict derivation is impossible. Statistical mechanics yields only the result that entropy decrease is overwhelmingly improbable, not that it is impossible. The law is recovered as an approximation of extraordinary accuracy but not as a deductive consequence. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon strict derivation, is too demanding to accommodate the actual relations that obtain between theories in the sciences.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) A identifies a set of antecedent conditions and a general law from which P can be deduced, (ii) the antecedent conditions cited in A are true, and (iii) the general law invoked in A has been independently confirmed by empirical investigation.
conditions: A identifies a set of antecedent conditions and a general law from which P can be deduced
The antecedent conditions cited in A are true
The general law invoked in A has been independently confirmed by empirical investigation
counterexample: Consider a physician who explains a patient's recovery from an infection by citing the true antecedent condition that the patient ingested a sugar pill and the independently confirmed law that infections often resolve spontaneously within a fortnight. The recovery can be deduced from these premises, and all three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call this a satisfactory explanation, for the sugar pill played no role whatever in bringing about the recovery; the true cause was the patient's immune response.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the antecedent conditions cited in A be causally relevant to the occurrence of P, so that explanatorily idle factors may be included without penalty.
passage: One might attempt to characterise satisfactory explanation as follows: an account explains a phenomenon if it identifies true antecedent conditions and an independently confirmed general law from which the phenomenon can be deduced. This proposal captures the deductive-nomological structure that many philosophers have regarded as central to explanation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed without great difficulty. Suppose a physician explains a patient's recovery from a mild infection by citing the true antecedent condition that the patient ingested a sugar pill and the well-confirmed generalisation that such infections resolve spontaneously within a fortnight. The recovery can be validly deduced. The antecedent conditions are true. The law is well-confirmed. Nevertheless, one might observe that the sugar pill is entirely irrelevant to the recovery. The genuine cause is the patient's immune response, and the inclusion of the pharmacologically inert pill renders the account defective as an explanation, even though it satisfies every condition in the definition. It appears that the definition, by attending only to deductive structure and empirical adequacy, permits the inclusion of causally idle factors and thereby fails to distinguish genuine explanatory relevance from accidental co-occurrence.

definition: A statement L is a law of nature if and only if (i) L is a universally quantified conditional that admits of no exceptions, (ii) L is not logically true, and (iii) L has been confirmed by observation of its instances.
conditions: L is a universally quantified conditional that admits of no exceptions
L is not logically true
L has been confirmed by observation of its instances
counterexample: Consider the statement that all spheres of enriched uranium have a mass less than one hundred thousand kilograms. This is a universally quantified conditional without exceptions, it is not logically true, and it has been confirmed by every observed instance of enriched uranium. Yet one might observe that its truth arises not from any fundamental regularity but from the practical impossibility of assembling so large a sphere before a chain reaction intervenes. It is an accidental generalisation sustained by technological constraint rather than a genuine law of nature.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that L hold in virtue of some underlying nomic or physical necessity, rather than merely as a consequence of contingent practical limitations upon the assembly of its instances.
passage: A natural proposal for demarcating laws of nature holds that a law is a universally quantified conditional, not logically true, which has been confirmed by observation of its instances. The proposal has intuitive appeal: Newton's laws, the laws of thermodynamics, and Maxwell's equations all conform to this pattern. Yet one should hesitate to regard it as fully satisfactory. Consider the statement that all spheres of enriched uranium have a mass less than one hundred thousand kilograms. This statement is universally quantified and admits of no observed exceptions. It is not logically true — there is no contradiction in the supposition of a very massive sphere of uranium. And it has been confirmed by every instance we have examined. Nevertheless, we should not wish to count it as a law of nature. Its truth depends upon the practical impossibility of assembling so large a sphere before a critical chain reaction intervenes — a contingent feature of nuclear physics and engineering constraints, not a fundamental regularity of nature. The definition, by requiring only universal truth, non-logical status, and observational confirmation, cannot distinguish the genuinely nomic from the accidentally universal.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a genuine scientific theory if and only if (i) T yields precise quantitative predictions that can be compared with experimental data, (ii) T is internally consistent, and (iii) T has survived at least one serious attempt at experimental refutation.
conditions: T yields precise quantitative predictions that can be compared with experimental data
T is internally consistent
T has survived at least one serious attempt at experimental refutation
counterexample: Consider a purely curve-fitting formula that expresses the relationship between the pressure and volume of a gas at a given temperature. It yields precise quantitative predictions, is internally consistent, and has survived every experimental test. Yet we should hesitate to regard it as a genuine scientific theory, for it merely describes the observed regularity without postulating any underlying mechanism or unifying principle. It is an empirical formula, not a theory in the sense that the kinetic theory of gases is a theory.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that T postulate underlying mechanisms or unifying principles that render its predictions intelligible, rather than merely fitting mathematical functions to observed data.
passage: One might propose that a genuine scientific theory is a body of internally consistent propositions that yields precise quantitative predictions and has survived serious attempts at experimental refutation. This characterisation captures much of what we expect from our best theories: the general theory of relativity, quantum mechanics, and the theory of evolution by natural selection all satisfy these conditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be readily constructed. Consider a purely empirical formula — say, an interpolation of observed data relating the pressure and volume of a gas at a constant temperature. Such a formula yields quantitative predictions of great precision. It is internally consistent, for it is merely a mathematical expression. And it has survived every attempt at experimental refutation, for it was derived from the data themselves. Nevertheless, one might observe that it lacks the character of a genuine scientific theory. It does not postulate any mechanism, it does not explain why pressure and volume are so related, and it does not unify the thermal behaviour of gases with other physical phenomena. It appears that the definition, by attending only to predictive accuracy and empirical survival, conflates the descriptive with the explanatory.

definition: A report R constitutes a pure observation if and only if (i) R describes what is directly perceived by the senses without inference, (ii) R employs only terms whose meaning is fixed by ostensive definition, and (iii) any competent observer placed in the same circumstances would produce the same report.
conditions: R describes what is directly perceived by the senses without inference
R employs only terms whose meaning is fixed by ostensive definition
Any competent observer placed in the same circumstances would produce the same report
counterexample: Consider a trained radiologist who reports seeing a tumour on an X-ray film. The radiologist perceives the tumour directly, without conscious inference. The term 'tumour' could in principle be fixed by ostensive definition pointing to characteristic shapes on radiographic images. And any competent radiologist placed in the same circumstances would produce the same report. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the radiologist's perception is thoroughly shaped by years of medical training; an untrained observer looking at the same film would see only a pattern of light and shadow.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the observation be independent of the observer's theoretical training and background expectations, so that theoretically saturated perceptions may count as pure observations.
passage: It has sometimes been supposed that a pure observation is a report which describes what is directly perceived by the senses without inference, employs only ostensively defined terms, and would be produced identically by any competent observer in the same circumstances. This account promises to secure an uncontaminated empirical foundation for the sciences. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from medical practice. A trained radiologist, examining an X-ray film, reports seeing a tumour. One might argue that the radiologist perceives the tumour directly, without conscious inference — the shape is apprehended immediately, much as one apprehends the colour of a ripe apple. The term 'tumour' might be fixed ostensively by pointing to characteristic radiographic appearances. And any competent radiologist would produce the same report. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to call this a pure observation in the intended sense. The radiologist's capacity to perceive the tumour is the product of years of theoretical training; an untrained observer, looking at the same film, would perceive only an unintelligible pattern of light and shadow. The definition, by requiring only directness, ostensive terminology, and inter-observer agreement among competent observers, cannot exclude observations that are deeply informed by theoretical presuppositions.

definition: A procedure E constitutes a genuine scientific experiment if and only if (i) E involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable by the experimenter, (ii) E is designed to test a specific hypothesis, and (iii) the outcome of E is observed and recorded systematically.
conditions: E involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable by the experimenter
E is designed to test a specific hypothesis
The outcome of E is observed and recorded systematically
counterexample: Consider an astronomer who systematically observes and records a transit of Venus across the face of the Sun in order to test a specific hypothesis about the distance between the Earth and the Sun. All three conditions would appear to be satisfied if we treat the natural event as providing the relevant variation. Yet the astronomer manipulates no variable whatever — the transit is an entirely natural occurrence. It seems wrong to deny that the astronomer is conducting a genuine experiment, and yet it seems equally wrong to say that a variable has been deliberately manipulated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for natural experiments and observational tests in which the variation arises from nature rather than from deliberate intervention, yet which possess the same evidential force as manipulative experiments.
passage: One might define a genuine scientific experiment as a procedure in which the experimenter deliberately manipulates at least one variable, designs the procedure to test a specific hypothesis, and observes and records the outcome systematically. This characterisation accords well with paradigmatic experiments in physics and chemistry, where the investigator controls the conditions under which a phenomenon is produced. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the observational sciences. Consider an astronomer who observes a transit of Venus to test a hypothesis about the Earth-Sun distance. The observation is designed to test a specific hypothesis. The outcome is recorded with great systematic care. But the astronomer manipulates no variable whatever; the transit is a natural event beyond human control. We should not wish to say that the astronomer's meticulous investigation is not a genuine experiment, for it possesses the same evidential structure as a laboratory test. It appears that the definition, by requiring deliberate manipulation, is too restrictive, for it excludes natural experiments in which nature itself provides the relevant variation and the scientist's role is confined to careful observation and measurement.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a valid measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M produces a numerical value that can be assigned to Q, (ii) M is reproducible under similar conditions, and (iii) the instrument employed in M has been calibrated against a recognised standard.
conditions: M produces a numerical value that can be assigned to Q
M is reproducible under similar conditions
The instrument employed in M has been calibrated against a recognised standard
counterexample: Consider a chemist who measures the temperature of a solution by inserting a mercury thermometer that has been properly calibrated. The procedure produces a reproducible numerical value. However, the act of inserting the thermometer alters the temperature of the solution by transferring heat between the thermometer and the solution. The measured value is reproducible, the instrument is calibrated, yet the number obtained does not correspond to the temperature the solution had before the measurement was performed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the measurement procedure does not systematically disturb the quantity being measured, so that the numerical value obtained may differ from the true value of Q prior to the intervention.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a valid measurement of a quantity consists in a procedure that produces a reproducible numerical value by means of an instrument calibrated against a recognised standard. This characterisation captures the essential features of routine measurement in the laboratory: the balance is calibrated, the readings are reproducible, and a number is assigned. Yet one should hesitate to regard it as fully adequate. A case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from thermometry. A chemist wishes to measure the temperature of a small volume of solution and inserts a mercury thermometer that has been properly calibrated against a standard. The procedure is reproducible — repeated insertions yield the same reading. A numerical value is produced and assigned to the temperature. Yet the insertion of the thermometer into the solution transfers heat from the solution to the thermometer, thereby altering the very quantity the chemist seeks to measure. The temperature recorded is not the temperature the solution possessed before the measurement was performed. It appears that the definition, by requiring only reproducibility, numerical output, and calibration, takes no account of the possibility that the measurement procedure may systematically perturb the quantity under investigation.

definition: A statement S constitutes a genuine scientific prediction if and only if (i) S is derived from a scientific theory prior to the observation of the event it describes, (ii) S specifies observable conditions under which the predicted event will occur, and (iii) S is in principle capable of being shown false by observation.
conditions: S is derived from a scientific theory prior to the observation of the event it describes
S specifies observable conditions under which the predicted event will occur
S is in principle capable of being shown false by observation
counterexample: Consider an evolutionary biologist who derives from the theory of natural selection the prediction that organisms well-adapted to their environments will tend to survive and reproduce. This statement is derived from the theory prior to any particular observation, it specifies observable conditions (adaptation, survival, reproduction), and it appears capable of falsification. Yet one might observe that the terms 'well-adapted' and 'tend to survive' are so intimately linked that the prediction reduces to a near-tautology: to call an organism well-adapted is, in large part, to say that it survives and reproduces.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the predicted outcome be logically independent of the terms used to specify the conditions under which it is expected, so that the prediction has genuine empirical content rather than quasi-tautological character.
passage: One might propose that a genuine scientific prediction is a statement derived from a theory prior to observation, specifying the observable conditions under which the predicted event will occur, and capable in principle of falsification. This proposal is attractive, for it ties prediction to both theoretical derivation and empirical vulnerability. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in evolutionary biology. From the theory of natural selection, one may derive the prediction that organisms well-adapted to their environments will tend to survive and reproduce. This statement is derived prior to particular observation, it specifies observable conditions — namely, the degree of adaptation and the rates of survival and reproduction — and it appears falsifiable in principle. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard it as a genuine prediction with substantive empirical content. The concept of being well-adapted is so closely bound to that of surviving and reproducing that the prediction threatens to become a tautology. It appears that the definition, by requiring only temporal priority, observational specification, and falsifiability, cannot distinguish substantive predictions from those that are empirically vacuous in virtue of the logical interdependence of their terms.

definition: A representation M constitutes a scientific model of a system S if and only if (i) M shares the same formal structure as S with respect to the relevant relations, (ii) M simplifies S by omitting features deemed irrelevant to the investigation, and (iii) M permits the derivation of testable consequences about S.
conditions: M shares the same formal structure as S with respect to the relevant relations
M simplifies S by omitting features deemed irrelevant to the investigation
M permits the derivation of testable consequences about S
counterexample: Consider a map of the London Underground. It shares the formal structure of the network with respect to the topological relations among stations and lines, it simplifies by omitting geographical distances and surface features, and it permits one to derive testable consequences — for example, that one must change trains at certain interchange stations. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call the Underground map a scientific model, for it is a practical navigational device rather than a tool for investigating the physical properties of the railway system.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that M be employed in the service of theoretical inquiry or the advancement of understanding of S, so that purely practical representations may be counted as scientific models.
passage: It is tempting to define a scientific model as a representation that shares the formal structure of a system, simplifies it by omitting irrelevant features, and permits the derivation of testable consequences. Such a characterisation accords well with the practice of modelling in physics and biology: the ideal gas model, the Lotka-Volterra equations, and the Bohr model of the atom all satisfy these conditions. Yet one might observe that the characterisation is too permissive. Consider a map of the London Underground. It shares the formal, topological structure of the network: the order of stations along each line and the interchange connections are faithfully preserved. It simplifies the system by omitting geographical distances, the curvature of tunnels, and the surface topography of London. And it permits the derivation of testable consequences — one can predict, for example, that a journey from King's Cross to Waterloo requires a change at the Northern line. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to call the Underground map a scientific model. It is a practical navigational tool, not an instrument of theoretical inquiry. The definition, by attending only to structural correspondence, simplification, and testability, cannot distinguish scientific models from merely practical representations.

definition: A theory T1 is reduced to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2 together with appropriate bridge principles, (ii) every term of T1 can be defined in terms of the vocabulary of T2, and (iii) T2 is more fundamental than T1 in the sense that it describes a more basic level of organisation.
conditions: Every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2 together with appropriate bridge principles
Every term of T1 can be defined in terms of the vocabulary of T2
T2 is more fundamental than T1 in the sense that it describes a more basic level of organisation
counterexample: Consider the reduction of classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. The laws of thermodynamics can in many cases be derived from statistical mechanics with appropriate bridge principles, and the fundamental vocabulary of thermodynamics — temperature, entropy, pressure — can be given statistical-mechanical definitions. Yet the second law of thermodynamics, as stated in thermodynamics, admits of no exceptions, whereas in statistical mechanics it holds only with overwhelming probability. The statistical-mechanical derivation yields a law that is almost but not quite identical to the thermodynamic original.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the derived laws be strictly identical to the laws of T1, rather than merely approximate to them, so that cases of approximate or inexact derivation cannot be distinguished from genuine reduction.
passage: A standard account of intertheoretic reduction holds that a theory T1 is reduced to a more fundamental theory T2 when every law of T1 can be derived from T2 with bridge principles, every term of T1 can be defined using the vocabulary of T2, and T2 describes a more basic level of organisation. This account has been widely discussed in the philosophy of science, and it captures the aspirations of many reductive programmes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with the supposed reduction of classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. The bridge principles identifying temperature with mean kinetic energy and entropy with a logarithmic measure of microstates are reasonably well-defined. The laws of thermodynamics can, in favourable cases, be derived from statistical-mechanical premises. Yet the second law of thermodynamics, as stated within thermodynamics, is an exceptionless generalisation: entropy never decreases in an isolated system. In statistical mechanics, however, the corresponding proposition holds only with overwhelming probability. There exist exceedingly rare but physically possible fluctuations in which entropy decreases. The derived law is not strictly identical to the original. It appears that the definition, by requiring only derivability without specifying exactness, cannot distinguish strict reduction from approximate correspondence.

definition: A system of classification C is a natural classification if and only if (i) C groups objects according to properties that are shared by all and only the members of each class, (ii) the properties used in C are intrinsic to the objects classified, and (iii) membership in the classes of C is determined by observable features.
conditions: C groups objects according to properties that are shared by all and only the members of each class
The properties used in C are intrinsic to the objects classified
Membership in the classes of C is determined by observable features
counterexample: Consider the classification of biological species. It would appear that species are grouped according to shared intrinsic properties — morphological features, physiological characteristics — and that membership is determined by observable features. Yet one might observe that the boundaries of species are determined not solely by intrinsic, observable properties but by historical and genealogical relations: two organisms may be morphologically indistinguishable and yet belong to different species if their populations are reproductively isolated by separate evolutionary histories.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the classes reflect the historical, causal, or genealogical relations among the objects, so that historically constituted groupings cannot be distinguished from those based solely on intrinsic resemblance.
passage: One might propose that a natural classification groups objects according to intrinsic properties shared by all and only members of each class, where membership is determined by observable features. This proposal has an attractive simplicity: it grounds classification in the discernible characters of the objects themselves, free from arbitrary convention. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the biological sciences. The classification of organisms into species appears at first glance to conform to the definition. Organisms are grouped by shared morphological and physiological characteristics — features that are intrinsic and observable. Yet the boundaries of biological species are not fixed by intrinsic resemblance alone. Two populations of insects may be morphologically indistinguishable in every observable respect and yet belong to distinct species because they are reproductively isolated by separate evolutionary lineages. It appears that what makes a species a natural kind is not merely the sharing of observable intrinsic properties but the historical and genealogical relationships that bind its members. The definition, by attending only to intrinsic and observable features, cannot accommodate classifications whose naturalness derives from causal or historical relations rather than from resemblance.

definition: An account A constitutes a genuine explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) A cites conditions and regularities from which P follows deductively, (ii) the cited conditions actually obtain, and (iii) A renders P intelligible by showing why P rather than some alternative outcome was to be expected.
conditions: A cites conditions and regularities from which P follows deductively
The cited conditions actually obtain
A renders P intelligible by showing why P rather than some alternative outcome was to be expected
counterexample: Suppose one explains the length of a flagpole's shadow by citing the height of the pole, the angle of the sun, and the laws of optics. All three conditions are met: the shadow's length follows deductively, the conditions obtain, and the account renders the shadow intelligible. Yet one could equally deduce the height of the pole from the shadow's length, the angle of the sun, and the same optical laws — satisfying the same three conditions. It would be odd to say that the shadow explains the height of the pole, for the pole is the cause, not the effect, of the shadow.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the explanatory relation be asymmetric — that the cited conditions stand in a causal or ontological priority relation to the phenomenon, so as to exclude cases in which the explanandum could equally well serve as premise for the explanans.
passage: One might characterise genuine explanation as an account that cites actually obtaining conditions and regularities from which the phenomenon follows deductively and which renders the phenomenon intelligible by showing why it, rather than some alternative, was to be expected. This characterisation captures the logical structure of explanation and adds the requirement of intelligibility. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed from elementary optics. Suppose one explains the length of a flagpole's shadow by citing the height of the pole, the angle of elevation of the sun, and the rectilinear propagation of light. The shadow's length follows deductively. The conditions obtain. The account renders the shadow's length intelligible. Yet one could equally well deduce the height of the pole from the length of the shadow and the same optical law. This reverse derivation satisfies every condition in the definition: the conditions obtain, the pole's height follows deductively, and one might even claim it renders the height intelligible. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the shadow explains the height. It appears that the definition, by attending to deductive structure and intelligibility alone, cannot secure the asymmetry that explanation requires. The pole causes the shadow, not the reverse, and no purely logical characterisation captures this directional dependence.

definition: A generalisation L qualifies as a law of nature if and only if (i) L is a universally quantified statement admitting no actual exceptions, (ii) L supports counterfactual conditionals, and (iii) L is empirically testable.
conditions: L is a universally quantified statement admitting no actual exceptions
L supports counterfactual conditionals
L is empirically testable
counterexample: Consider the generalisation that all rivers flow to the sea. It is universally quantified and admits of no actual exception among the rivers one has surveyed. It supports counterfactuals — if a new river were to form, it would flow to the sea. And it is empirically testable. Yet several rivers, such as those terminating in inland basins, do not reach the sea at all. The statement appeared to support counterfactuals only because the speaker's experience was confined to rivers draining seaward. The generalisation is parochial rather than genuinely nomic.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that L hold across all physically possible circumstances rather than merely across the circumstances that have been sampled, so that generalisations whose counterfactual support derives from limited experience may masquerade as laws.
passage: A natural suggestion for characterising laws of nature holds that a law is a universally quantified statement without exceptions, supporting counterfactual conditionals, and empirically testable. The proposal is attractive because it moves beyond mere universality by requiring counterfactual robustness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from physical geography. Consider the claim that all rivers flow to the sea. An observer confined to the coastal regions of Western Europe might find this generalisation universally true, counterfactually robust — if a new river were to form here, it would reach the sea — and empirically testable. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the generalisation fails in the interior of Central Asia, where rivers terminate in landlocked basins and never reach any sea. The apparent counterfactual support was an artefact of parochial experience. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that the generalisation support counterfactuals without specifying the range of circumstances over which such support must hold, permits locally robust but globally false generalisations to qualify as laws. A satisfactory account would need to require that L hold across all physically accessible circumstances, not merely those that happen to have been encountered.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is a deductively closed set of statements, (ii) T contains at least one general law, and (iii) T entails observational consequences that have been confirmed.
conditions: T is a deductively closed set of statements
T contains at least one general law
T entails observational consequences that have been confirmed
counterexample: Consider a deductively closed set containing Newton's law of gravitation conjoined with the proposition that all emeralds are green. This set contains a general law, is deductively closed, and entails confirmed observational consequences about both planetary motion and emerald colour. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call this conjunction a single scientific theory, for the two components bear no systematic relation to one another. The theory of gravitation and the generalisation about emeralds are merely bundled together by logical conjunction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the propositions in T be systematically unified — that the laws and auxiliary assumptions bear upon a common domain and explain its phenomena by means of shared principles, rather than being arbitrary conjunctions of unrelated claims.
passage: One might propose that a scientific theory is a deductively closed set of statements containing at least one general law and entailing confirmed observational consequences. This proposal captures the formal and empirical features that theories are commonly expected to possess. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by a simple logical device. Take Newton's law of universal gravitation and conjoin it with the proposition that all emeralds are green. The resulting set is deductively closed, for any consequence of the conjunction is included. It contains a general law — indeed, one of the most powerful laws in physics. And it entails observational consequences that have been abundantly confirmed: the motions of the planets and the colour of emeralds. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this conjunction is not a scientific theory in any substantive sense. The gravitational law and the emerald generalisation bear no systematic relation to one another; they address different domains, invoke different concepts, and explain different phenomena. The definition, by requiring only deductive closure, the presence of a law, and confirmed consequences, cannot distinguish a genuinely unified theory from an arbitrary conjunction of unrelated truths.

definition: A theory T1 is reducible to a more fundamental theory T2 if and only if (i) every term of T1 can be defined using the vocabulary of T2, (ii) every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2 together with these definitions, and (iii) T2 explains all the phenomena that T1 explains.
conditions: Every term of T1 can be defined using the vocabulary of T2
Every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2 together with these definitions
T2 explains all the phenomena that T1 explains
counterexample: Consider the reduction of classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. The term 'temperature' in thermodynamics is identified with mean molecular kinetic energy. The gas laws can be derived from statistical mechanics. And statistical mechanics explains all the phenomena of thermodynamics. Yet the second law of thermodynamics, in its classical formulation, is an exceptionless law, whereas its statistical-mechanical counterpart permits extremely improbable violations — fluctuations in which entropy spontaneously decreases. The derived law is not identical with the original, for it holds only statistically.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the derived laws of T1 preserve the modal strength of the original laws, so that a derivation which yields merely statistical approximations of originally exceptionless laws may count as a complete reduction.
passage: The classical account of inter-theoretic reduction holds that one theory is reducible to another if every term of the first can be defined in the vocabulary of the second, every law of the first can be derived from the second together with these definitions, and the second explains all the phenomena that the first explains. This characterisation captures the logical structure of paradigmatic reductions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the relationship between thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. The term 'temperature' can be identified with mean molecular kinetic energy. The ideal gas law can be derived from statistical-mechanical principles. Statistical mechanics explains all thermodynamic phenomena. All three conditions appear satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the second law of thermodynamics, classically understood as an exceptionless generalisation, becomes in statistical mechanics a statement that admits of fantastically improbable but physically possible violations. The derived law is weaker than the original. It appears that the definition, by requiring only derivability without attention to the modal status of the derived laws, permits reductions in which the laws of the reduced theory undergo subtle but significant alteration. A more adequate account would need to specify that the derived laws preserve the strength and generality of the originals.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a valid measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M yields a numerical value that corresponds to the magnitude of Q, (ii) the numerical value is reproducible under the same conditions, and (iii) M is calibrated against a recognised standard.
conditions: M yields a numerical value that corresponds to the magnitude of Q
The numerical value is reproducible under the same conditions
M is calibrated against a recognised standard
counterexample: Consider a thermometer that has been calibrated against the international temperature standard and yields reproducible readings. When placed in a beaker of water, it reads 85 degrees Celsius. All three conditions are met. Yet the thermometer is positioned near the flame heating the beaker, so that the reading reflects the combined influence of the water temperature and radiant heat from the flame. The thermometer measures something, and does so reproducibly, but it does not validly measure the temperature of the water.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the procedure isolate the quantity Q from extraneous influences, so that a measurement which is reproducible and calibrated but systematically contaminated by confounding factors may still count as valid.
passage: One might define a valid measurement as a procedure that yields a numerical value corresponding to the magnitude of a quantity, is reproducible under the same conditions, and is calibrated against a recognised standard. This characterisation captures the essential features of scientific measurement: numerical precision, reproducibility, and calibration. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from laboratory practice. Suppose a thermometer, properly calibrated against the international temperature standard, is placed in a beaker of water that is being heated by an open flame. The thermometer yields a reproducible reading of 85 degrees Celsius. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the thermometer is positioned in such a way that it receives radiant heat from the flame in addition to the thermal energy of the water. The reading is reproducible because the thermometer is always placed in the same position relative to the flame. It is calibrated. It yields a numerical value. Nevertheless, it does not validly measure the temperature of the water, for it is systematically contaminated by an extraneous influence. It appears that the definition, by requiring only reproducibility and calibration, cannot distinguish a measurement that isolates the target quantity from one that conflates it with confounding factors.

definition: A statement S constitutes a genuine scientific prediction if and only if (i) S is derived from a theory T prior to the observation of the predicted phenomenon, (ii) S specifies observable conditions under which the phenomenon will occur, and (iii) S is precise enough to be confirmed or disconfirmed by observation.
conditions: S is derived from a theory T prior to the observation of the predicted phenomenon
S specifies observable conditions under which the phenomenon will occur
S is precise enough to be confirmed or disconfirmed by observation
counterexample: Consider a numerologist who, by manipulating the digits of certain physical constants according to an arbitrary rule, derives, prior to any observation, the precise prediction that a solar eclipse will occur on a specific date. The prediction specifies observable conditions and is precise enough to be confirmed. When the eclipse occurs as predicted, all three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to regard this as a genuine scientific prediction, for the theory from which it is derived has no systematic connection to the phenomena of celestial mechanics.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the theory T from which S is derived bear a systematic and principled connection to the domain of phenomena being predicted, so that a prediction derived by accident or arbitrary manipulation may count as genuinely scientific.
passage: One might characterise a genuine scientific prediction as a statement derived from a theory prior to the observation of the predicted phenomenon, specifying observable conditions under which the phenomenon will occur, and precise enough to be confirmed or disconfirmed. This characterisation captures the temporal and empirical features that distinguish prediction from mere postdiction. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed. Suppose a numerologist, by applying an arbitrary arithmetic rule to the digits of certain physical constants, derives the prediction that a total solar eclipse will be visible from a particular location on a specific date. The prediction is derived prior to observation. It specifies observable conditions with great precision. And it is readily confirmed or disconfirmed. When the eclipse occurs as predicted, all three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the numerological theory bears no systematic relation to the phenomena of celestial mechanics. The successful prediction is a coincidence — the theory could not reliably generate further accurate predictions about eclipses or any other astronomical events. It appears that the definition, by requiring only temporal priority, observational specificity, and precision, cannot distinguish a prediction that issues from genuine understanding of the relevant phenomena from one that succeeds by happenstance.

definition: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C assigns every object in the domain to exactly one class, (ii) the classes are defined by observable similarities among their members, and (iii) membership in a class enables reliable inferences about further properties of the member.
conditions: C assigns every object in the domain to exactly one class
The classes are defined by observable similarities among their members
Membership in a class enables reliable inferences about further properties of the member
counterexample: Consider a classification of chemical substances into those that are white and those that are not white. Every substance is assigned to exactly one class. The classes are defined by an observable property — colour. And membership enables certain reliable inferences: white substances tend to be powdery, to dissolve in water more readily than dark minerals, and so forth. All three conditions appear met. Yet the classification groups table salt with chalk and cocaine, and separates iron oxide from manganese dioxide despite their chemical kinship. The classification is superficial and fails to track the chemical structure that determines the most important properties of substances.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the observable similarities on which the classes are based reflect underlying structural or causal properties that generate the broadest and most informative range of inferences, rather than superficial resemblances.
passage: A natural proposal holds that a scientifically adequate classification assigns every object in a domain to exactly one class, defines classes by observable similarities, and enables reliable inferences about further properties of classified objects. This proposal captures the practical virtues of classification: exhaustiveness, exclusivity, and predictive utility. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from chemistry. Consider a classification of chemical substances into white substances and non-white substances. Every substance is assigned to exactly one class. The classes are defined by a directly observable property. And membership enables certain reliable inferences — white substances tend to be powdery solids, to have higher solubility in water, and so forth. All three conditions are met. Yet the classification groups table salt with chalk, with sugar, and with cocaine, substances whose chemical natures are profoundly different. It separates iron oxide from manganese dioxide despite their structural kinship as metal oxides. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only observable similarity and inferential utility, permits classifications that track superficial resemblances rather than the deep structural properties that determine the most important chemical behaviours.

definition: A representation M constitutes a scientific model of a system S if and only if (i) M reproduces the observed behaviour of S under specified conditions, (ii) M simplifies S by omitting features judged irrelevant, and (iii) M is expressed in a precise mathematical or formal language.
conditions: M reproduces the observed behaviour of S under specified conditions
M simplifies S by omitting features judged irrelevant
M is expressed in a precise mathematical or formal language
counterexample: Consider Ptolemy's geocentric model of planetary motion. It reproduces the observed behaviour of the planets — their positions in the sky — with remarkable accuracy. It simplifies the heavens by omitting features such as cometary orbits. And it is expressed in precise mathematical language, employing epicycles and deferents. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call it a satisfactory scientific model, for its underlying structure — the earth at the centre, with planets moving on circles upon circles — does not correspond even approximately to the actual arrangement of the solar system.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the structural features of M correspond, at least approximately, to the structural features of S, so that a model which reproduces behaviour by means of a fundamentally misleading mechanism may count as scientifically adequate.
passage: One might define a scientific model as a representation that reproduces the observed behaviour of a system, simplifies it by omitting irrelevant features, and is expressed in precise mathematical language. This characterisation captures the pragmatic virtues that make models useful: empirical adequacy, tractability, and formal precision. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the history of astronomy. Ptolemy's geocentric model of planetary motion satisfies all three conditions. It reproduces the observed positions of the planets with accuracy sufficient for centuries of practical use. It simplifies the heavens by confining attention to the major celestial bodies. And it is expressed in a sophisticated mathematical formalism of epicycles, deferents, and equants. Nevertheless, one might observe that the model's underlying structure is fundamentally misleading. The earth is not at the centre of the solar system, and the planets do not move on circles upon circles. The model achieves empirical adequacy by a mechanism that bears no resemblance to the actual workings of the system it represents. It appears that the definition, by attending only to behavioural reproduction, simplification, and formal precision, cannot distinguish a model that captures the genuine structure of a system from one that merely mimics its observable output.

definition: A theory T is genuinely scientific if and only if (i) T makes claims that could in principle be shown false by observation, (ii) the observational conditions under which T would be falsified are specifiable in advance, and (iii) T has not yet been falsified by any observation.
conditions: T makes claims that could in principle be shown false by observation
The observational conditions under which T would be falsified are specifiable in advance
T has not yet been falsified by any observation
counterexample: Consider Newtonian mechanics in the year 1900. It had been falsified by the anomalous precession of Mercury's perihelion — an observation inconsistent with its predictions. By condition (iii), it would no longer count as genuinely scientific. Yet in 1900 Newtonian mechanics remained one of the most powerful and productive scientific theories ever devised. It would be absurd to classify it as unscientific merely because it had encountered a single anomalous observation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that a theory may remain genuinely scientific even after encountering falsifying observations, provided it retains substantial empirical support and explanatory power in other domains; the requirement of non-falsification is too stringent.
passage: One might propose that a theory is genuinely scientific if and only if it makes claims falsifiable by observation, the conditions of falsification are specifiable in advance, and the theory has not yet been falsified. This proposal aims to demarcate science from pseudoscience by requiring both the possibility and the absence of refutation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of physics. Consider Newtonian mechanics in the final decade of the nineteenth century. The anomalous precession of Mercury's perihelion had been observed since the 1850s, and no Newtonian account could fully accommodate it. By the third condition, Newtonian mechanics had been falsified and should therefore be classified as not genuinely scientific. Yet this conclusion is plainly absurd. In 1900, Newtonian mechanics was among the most successful and productive scientific theories ever formulated, accurately predicting the motions of countless celestial and terrestrial bodies. One might observe that the definition, by requiring that a genuinely scientific theory remain unfalsified, conflates the demarcation of science from pseudoscience with the quite different question of whether a particular theory has encountered difficulties. A theory may be genuinely scientific and yet face anomalies that require revision rather than wholesale abandonment.

definition: A framework F constitutes a dominant scientific paradigm if and only if (i) F provides the fundamental concepts and methods that guide research in a discipline, (ii) F is accepted by the overwhelming majority of practitioners in the discipline, and (iii) F has produced a substantial body of successful problem-solutions.
conditions: F provides the fundamental concepts and methods that guide research in a discipline
F is accepted by the overwhelming majority of practitioners in the discipline
F has produced a substantial body of successful problem-solutions
counterexample: Consider the phlogiston theory in the 1770s. It provided the fundamental concepts — phlogiston, dephlogisticated air — that guided chemical research. It was accepted by the overwhelming majority of chemists. And it had produced a substantial body of successful problem-solutions, accounting for combustion, calcination, and respiration. All three conditions were met. Yet the phlogiston theory was profoundly mistaken about the nature of combustion, and within two decades it would be replaced by Lavoisier's oxygen theory. Satisfying the definition did not prevent the framework from being fundamentally in error.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the paradigm's fundamental concepts correspond to real features of the natural world, so that a framework which is universally accepted and practically successful but ontologically mistaken may count as a satisfactory paradigm without qualification.
passage: One might define a dominant scientific paradigm as a framework that provides the fundamental concepts and methods guiding research in a discipline, is accepted by the overwhelming majority of its practitioners, and has generated a substantial body of successful problem-solutions. This characterisation captures the sociological and pragmatic features of paradigms. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the history of chemistry. In the 1770s, the phlogiston theory satisfied all three conditions. It provided the guiding concepts for chemical research — phlogiston was the substance supposedly released during combustion. It was accepted by nearly every practising chemist. And it had produced an impressive body of successful problem-solutions, accounting for the phenomena of combustion, calcination, and the reduction of metalite ores. Nevertheless, the theory was fundamentally mistaken. Phlogiston does not exist; combustion involves the combination of substances with oxygen, not the release of a substance. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to conceptual guidance, communal acceptance, and problem-solving success, cannot distinguish a paradigm whose success reflects genuine insight into natural processes from one whose success is achieved despite fundamental ontological error. A more adequate characterisation would require some connection between the paradigm's concepts and the actual structure of the phenomena under investigation.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) A subsumes P under a general law or regularity, (ii) the law or regularity cited in A is empirically well-confirmed, and (iii) the conditions specified in A are sufficient for the occurrence of P.
conditions: A subsumes P under a general law or regularity
The law or regularity cited in A is empirically well-confirmed
The conditions specified in A are sufficient for the occurrence of P
counterexample: Consider a physician who explains why a patient fell asleep by citing the well-confirmed regularity that anyone who ingests a sleeping draught falls asleep, together with the condition that the patient ingested a sleeping draught. The account subsumes the phenomenon under a confirmed regularity and specifies sufficient conditions. Yet the explanation is circular in a subtle way: calling the substance a sleeping draught simply means that it induces sleep. The explanation has not advanced our understanding of why the patient fell asleep; it has merely redescribed the phenomenon in general terms.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the law or regularity cited be independently characterisable — that it explain the phenomenon by appeal to features that are not simply restatements of the explanandum in different vocabulary.
passage: One might characterise a satisfactory explanation as an account that subsumes a phenomenon under a well-confirmed general regularity and specifies conditions sufficient for the phenomenon's occurrence. This proposal captures the deductive and empirical features we commonly associate with explanatory success. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a physician is asked why a particular patient fell asleep and replies that the patient ingested a sleeping draught, and that anyone who ingests a sleeping draught falls asleep. The account subsumes the particular occurrence under a general regularity. The regularity is empirically well-confirmed — sleeping draughts do invariably induce sleep. The conditions specified are sufficient for the occurrence. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this as a satisfactory explanation. The term 'sleeping draught' simply designates a substance that induces sleep; to say that the patient fell asleep because she took a substance that induces sleep is to move in a circle. It appears that the definition, by requiring only subsumption, confirmation, and sufficiency, cannot distinguish an explanation that genuinely advances our understanding from one that merely redescribes the phenomenon under a different guise.

definition: A generalisation L qualifies as a law of nature if and only if (i) L is universally quantified over all instances of a specified kind, (ii) L has no known exceptions, and (iii) L plays a central role in an established scientific theory.
conditions: L is universally quantified over all instances of a specified kind
L has no known exceptions
L plays a central role in an established scientific theory
counterexample: Consider the generalisation that all the coins in the fountain at the Piazza Navona are copper coins. Suppose this generalisation is universally quantified, has no known exceptions, and — by some administrative convention — plays a central role in the municipality's official theory of fountain maintenance. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the generalisation is plainly accidental: it reflects nothing more than the habits of tourists and is not a law of nature in any recognisable sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that L hold by virtue of the nature or constitution of the kinds involved, rather than by the contingent arrangement of particular circumstances, so that merely accidental generalisations satisfying the three conditions are not excluded.
passage: One might propose that a generalisation qualifies as a law of nature if it is universally quantified over all instances of a specified kind, has no known exceptions, and plays a central role in an established scientific theory. This characterisation aims to separate laws from mere generalisations by adding the requirement of theoretical embeddedness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed. Suppose the municipality of Rome maintains an official theory of fountain maintenance according to which all coins deposited in the fountain at the Piazza Navona are copper coins — a claim that, let us suppose, happens to be true and has no known exceptions. The generalisation is universally quantified. It has no exceptions. And it plays a central role in an established (if bureaucratic) theory. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the generalisation is entirely accidental. It reflects the contingent habits of tourists rather than any feature of natural necessity. It appears that the definition, by requiring theoretical centrality without specifying that the theory must concern the natures or constitutions of the kinds in question, cannot distinguish genuine laws from accidentally true generalisations embedded in theories of the wrong sort.

definition: A set of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is internally consistent, (ii) T entails predictions about observable phenomena, and (iii) the predictions entailed by T have been confirmed by experiment or observation.
conditions: T is internally consistent
T entails predictions about observable phenomena
The predictions entailed by T have been confirmed by experiment or observation
counterexample: Consider the conjunction of Euclidean geometry with the claim that the number of stars is even. This set is internally consistent. It entails predictions about observable phenomena — for instance, that the angles of any triangle drawn on a flat surface sum to one hundred and eighty degrees. These predictions have been confirmed. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to regard this conjunction as a scientific theory, for the claim about the parity of the number of stars bears no systematic relationship to Euclidean geometry.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the propositions in T be systematically related to one another — that they address a common domain and derive their predictive power from shared principles rather than being an arbitrary conjunction of individually adequate claims.
passage: A familiar proposal holds that a scientific theory is an internally consistent set of propositions that entails predictions about observable phenomena which have been confirmed. This characterisation captures the logical and empirical virtues we expect of theories. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed by a simple logical device. Take Euclidean geometry, a well-confirmed body of propositions, and conjoin it with the claim that the number of stars in the observable universe is even. The resulting set is internally consistent. It entails predictions about observable phenomena — the angular sums of triangles, the properties of parallel lines — that have been amply confirmed. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the resulting conjunction is not a theory in any substantive sense. The geometrical propositions and the cosmological claim share no principles, address different domains, and derive their confirmation from entirely unrelated observations. The definition, by requiring only consistency, observational consequences, and confirmation, cannot distinguish a genuinely unified body of scientific thought from a mere aggregate of individually satisfactory claims.

definition: A theory T1 has been successfully reduced to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every theoretical term of T1 can be mapped onto expressions in the vocabulary of T2, (ii) every law of T1 is derivable from T2 together with the mapping, and (iii) T2 is more general than T1 in the sense that T2 applies to a wider range of phenomena.
conditions: Every theoretical term of T1 can be mapped onto expressions in the vocabulary of T2
Every law of T1 is derivable from T2 together with the mapping
T2 is more general than T1 in the sense that T2 applies to a wider range of phenomena
counterexample: Consider the attempt to reduce Mendelian genetics to molecular biology. The Mendelian term 'gene' can be mapped onto sequences of DNA. Many Mendelian laws — for instance, the law of segregation — can be derived from molecular-biological principles. And molecular biology is more general, applying to phenomena far beyond classical genetics. Yet the Mendelian concept of dominance does not map cleanly onto any single molecular mechanism: dominance may arise from haplosufficiency, from regulatory effects, or from protein interactions, depending on the case. The mapping is one-to-many, and the reduction is at best partial.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the mapping between the terms of T1 and the expressions of T2 be unique and uniform — that each theoretical term of T1 correspond to a single, well-defined expression in T2, rather than to a disjunction of heterogeneous mechanisms.
passage: One might characterise a successful inter-theoretic reduction as obtaining when every theoretical term of the reduced theory can be mapped onto the vocabulary of the reducing theory, every law of the reduced theory is derivable from the reducing theory together with this mapping, and the reducing theory is more general. This account captures the logical structure of paradigmatic reductions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in biology. Consider the reduction of Mendelian genetics to molecular biology. The Mendelian term 'gene' can be associated with stretches of DNA. Many Mendelian laws are derivable from molecular principles. Molecular biology is unquestionably more general. All three conditions appear met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the concept of dominance in Mendelian genetics resists a clean mapping. In some cases dominance arises because a single copy of a gene produces sufficient protein; in others it arises from complex regulatory interactions; in still others from the physical properties of competing protein products. The Mendelian term 'dominant' corresponds not to a single molecular expression but to a heterogeneous disjunction of mechanisms. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that a mapping exist without insisting that it be uniform, permits reductions in which the terms of the reduced theory are splintered into a multiplicity of unrelated molecular realisations.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a valid measurement of a property Q in a system S if and only if (i) M assigns a numerical value to Q, (ii) repeated applications of M to the same system under the same conditions yield the same value, and (iii) the assigned value is independent of the particular instrument used to carry out M.
conditions: M assigns a numerical value to Q
Repeated applications of M to the same system under the same conditions yield the same value
The assigned value is independent of the particular instrument used to carry out M
counterexample: Consider the measurement of the length of a metal rod using a metal ruler in a room whose temperature is steadily rising. Repeated measurements at the same moment yield the same value, and different metal rulers of the same composition agree. All three conditions are met. Yet both the rod and the ruler are expanding at the same rate, so the measurement consistently reports the same length even as the rod is genuinely growing longer. The measurement is reproducible and instrument-independent but systematically blind to the very change it ought to detect.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the measurement procedure be sensitive to genuine changes in Q — that it be capable of registering variations in the property when they occur, rather than merely yielding consistent values that may reflect a systematic artefact.
passage: One might define a valid measurement as a procedure that assigns a numerical value to a property, yields the same value upon repetition under identical conditions, and produces results independent of the particular instrument employed. This characterisation captures the desiderata of precision, reproducibility, and objectivity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from thermal physics. Consider measuring the length of a metal rod with a metal ruler in a room whose temperature is steadily increasing. At any given moment, repeated measurements agree; different rulers of the same metallic composition also agree. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet both the rod and the ruler are expanding at the same rate due to thermal expansion. The measured length remains constant even as the rod is genuinely growing longer. The measurement is perfectly reproducible and instrument-independent, but it is systematically insensitive to the very change it purports to detect. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only consistency and instrument-independence, cannot distinguish a measurement that faithfully tracks the property from one that is blind to real variations because the instrument undergoes the same distortion as the measured object.

definition: A classification scheme C is scientifically natural if and only if (i) C partitions the domain into mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive classes, (ii) members of each class share a cluster of co-occurring properties, and (iii) the classes support reliable inductive inferences about unobserved properties of their members.
conditions: C partitions the domain into mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive classes
Members of each class share a cluster of co-occurring properties
The classes support reliable inductive inferences about unobserved properties of their members
counterexample: Consider a classification of animals into those that are domesticated and those that are wild. This scheme partitions the animal kingdom exhaustively and exclusively. Domesticated animals share a cluster of co-occurring properties — docility, tolerance of human proximity, modified reproductive cycles. And membership supports reliable inductive inferences: domesticated animals are likely to accept confinement, to breed in captivity, and to respond to training. All three conditions are met.
