Yet the classification groups dogs with silkworms and separates wolves from dogs, despite wolves and dogs being nearly identical genetically. The classification tracks a relation to human activity rather than biological nature.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the properties on which the classes are founded be intrinsic to the members themselves rather than relational to external agents, so that a classification grounded in extrinsic features such as domestication status may count as scientifically natural.
passage: One might propose that a scientifically natural classification partitions a domain into mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive classes whose members share co-occurring properties and which support reliable inductive inferences. This proposal captures the formal and inferential virtues we expect of natural classifications. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from zoology. Consider a classification of animals into domesticated and wild. The partition is exhaustive and exclusive. Domesticated animals share a cluster of properties — docility, tolerance of human contact, altered reproductive patterns, modified morphology. Membership supports reliable inferences: one may predict that a domesticated species will accept confinement and breed in captivity. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the classification groups the dog with the silkworm and separates the dog from the wolf, despite the near-identity of their genomes. One might observe that domestication is a relational property — it describes an animal's history of interaction with human beings rather than any feature intrinsic to the animal itself. The definition, by requiring only shared properties and inferential utility without specifying that the properties be intrinsic, permits classifications founded upon extrinsic and relational features to qualify as scientifically natural.

definition: A hypothesis H is genuinely falsifiable if and only if (i) there exists a logically possible observation O that is inconsistent with H, (ii) O is physically realisable — that is, it could in principle be carried out with available or foreseeable technology, and (iii) the proponents of H would accept O as a refutation.
conditions: There exists a logically possible observation O that is inconsistent with H
O is physically realisable with available or foreseeable technology
The proponents of H would accept O as a refutation
counterexample: Consider Lavoisier proposing the oxygen theory of combustion in the 1770s. The phlogiston theorists — the proponents of the rival hypothesis — would not accept Lavoisier's experiments as refutations of their view. They reinterpreted the results within the phlogiston framework. By condition (iii), the phlogiston theory would count as unfalsifiable simply because its proponents refused to accept the relevant observations as refutations. Yet the phlogiston theory was in fact falsifiable; what was lacking was not logical falsifiability but the willingness of its adherents to concede defeat.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the logical property of falsifiability with the sociological disposition of a theory's proponents; it fails to separate the question of whether an observation logically conflicts with a hypothesis from the question of whether anyone would in practice accept it as a refutation.
passage: One might define a genuinely falsifiable hypothesis as one for which there exists a logically possible and physically realisable observation inconsistent with it and which the proponents of the hypothesis would accept as a refutation. The third condition is intended to ensure that falsifiability is not merely a logical nicety but has practical force. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the history of chemistry. When Lavoisier demonstrated that combustion involves the combination of substances with oxygen, the phlogiston theorists did not accept his observations as refutations. They reinterpreted the experimental results — arguing, for instance, that oxygen was dephlogisticated air and that the experiments were compatible with phlogiston after all. By the third condition, the phlogiston theory would fail to count as falsifiable, since its proponents would not accept the relevant observations as refutations. Yet one might observe that the phlogiston theory was in fact logically falsifiable; the observations were inconsistent with it. The definition, by including a sociological condition among the criteria for falsifiability, confuses the logical structure of a hypothesis with the psychological rigidity of its advocates.

definition: A representation M constitutes an adequate scientific model of a target system T if and only if (i) M shares relevant structural features with T, (ii) M enables the derivation of correct predictions about T's behaviour, and (iii) M is simpler than T in the sense that M contains fewer variables or parameters.
conditions: M shares relevant structural features with T
M enables the derivation of correct predictions about T's behaviour
M is simpler than T in the sense that M contains fewer variables or parameters
counterexample: Consider a model of oceanic tidal patterns that includes the gravitational influence of the moon and the sun, the rotation of the earth, and the depth profile of the ocean floor. This model shares relevant structural features with the actual tidal system, yields correct predictions, and is simpler than the real ocean. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the model entirely omits the effect of coastal geometry, which in many harbours and estuaries produces tidal patterns radically different from those the model predicts. The model is adequate for open-ocean tides but profoundly misleading for the very locations where tidal prediction is most practically important.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the model specify the domain of conditions under which its structural correspondence and predictive accuracy hold, so that a model which is adequate only within an unspecified range may be mistaken for one that is adequate without qualification.
passage: A natural proposal holds that an adequate scientific model shares relevant structural features with its target, enables correct predictions, and is simpler than the system it represents. This captures the desiderata of structural fidelity, predictive utility, and tractability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from oceanography. Consider a tidal model incorporating the gravitational influence of the moon and sun, the earth's rotation, and the bathymetric profile of the ocean floor. This model shares structural features with the real tidal system, yields correct predictions for open-ocean tides, and is far simpler than the actual ocean. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the model omits coastal geometry entirely. In many harbours and estuaries, the interaction of tidal waves with complex coastlines produces patterns — standing waves, bore tides, resonance effects — that differ radically from the model's predictions. One might observe that the model is adequate only within a restricted domain that the definition does not require it to specify. The definition, by requiring only structural correspondence, predictive success, and simplicity, cannot distinguish a model that is adequate within a well-defined range from one that is silently inadequate outside an unstated range of conditions.

definition: A statement S constitutes a successful scientific prediction if and only if (i) S is derived from the principles of a theory T before the relevant observation is made, (ii) S is sufficiently precise to admit of confirmation or disconfirmation, and (iii) the observation subsequently confirms S.
conditions: S is derived from the principles of a theory T before the relevant observation is made
S is sufficiently precise to admit of confirmation or disconfirmation
The observation subsequently confirms S
counterexample: Consider a theory of economic cycles that yields, among its thousands of consequences, the prediction that a recession will begin in the third quarter of a particular year. Suppose the recession does indeed begin in that quarter. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the theory also predicted, with equal precision, that inflation would fall below two per cent — and inflation in fact rose. The theory generated many precise predictions, most of which failed; the one that succeeded was a matter of chance rather than theoretical insight.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the theory T have a general track record of predictive success, so that a theory which generates a single correct prediction among many false ones may receive credit for a successful prediction indistinguishable from a lucky guess.
passage: One might characterise a successful scientific prediction as a statement derived from a theory before the relevant observation, sufficiently precise to admit of confirmation, and subsequently confirmed. This characterisation captures the temporal and logical features of prediction. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from economics. Consider a theory of economic cycles that, among its many consequences, yields the precise prediction that a recession will commence in the third quarter of a given year. Suppose the recession does begin then. All three conditions are met. Yet the same theory also predicted, with equal precision, that inflation would drop below two per cent during the same period; in fact inflation rose sharply. The theory generated dozens of precise predictions, the great majority of which proved false. One might observe that the single correct prediction, taken in isolation, satisfies every condition in the definition. Yet it appears indistinguishable from a fortunate guess. The definition, by focusing on the derivation and confirmation of individual predictions without attending to the broader predictive record of the theory, cannot distinguish a prediction that issues from genuine theoretical understanding from one that succeeds by chance amid a background of failure.

definition: A framework F constitutes a scientific paradigm if and only if (i) F provides a set of fundamental assumptions that define the legitimate questions and methods of a discipline, (ii) F has generated a body of exemplary problem-solutions that practitioners emulate, and (iii) F is transmitted through the standard educational practices of the discipline.
conditions: F provides a set of fundamental assumptions that define the legitimate questions and methods of a discipline
F has generated a body of exemplary problem-solutions that practitioners emulate
F is transmitted through the standard educational practices of the discipline
counterexample: Consider astrology in the Hellenistic world. It provided a set of fundamental assumptions — the influence of celestial bodies upon terrestrial affairs — that defined what questions were legitimate and what methods were appropriate. It generated a body of exemplary problem-solutions: the casting of horoscopes, the interpretation of planetary conjunctions, the prediction of harvests. And it was transmitted through the standard educational practices of the period, taught alongside astronomy in the great schools. All three conditions were satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to grant astrology the status of a genuine scientific paradigm.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the framework's fundamental assumptions be amenable to systematic empirical scrutiny, so that a framework which meets all three conditions but whose core assumptions resist testing may qualify as a scientific paradigm.
passage: One might propose that a scientific paradigm is a framework providing fundamental assumptions that define the legitimate questions and methods of a discipline, generating exemplary problem-solutions, and transmitted through the discipline's standard educational practices. This characterisation captures the normative and sociological dimensions of paradigms. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from ancient intellectual history. Consider astrology as practised in the Hellenistic world. Astrology provided fundamental assumptions — the systematic influence of planetary positions upon terrestrial events — that defined the legitimate questions for its practitioners. It generated a rich body of exemplary problem-solutions: the casting of natal horoscopes, the interpretation of transits, the prediction of agricultural outcomes. And it was transmitted through the established educational institutions of the day, taught in the same schools that transmitted astronomical and mathematical learning. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should hesitate to regard Hellenistic astrology as a scientific paradigm. Its fundamental assumptions were not amenable to the kind of systematic empirical scrutiny that characterises genuine scientific practice. The definition, by requiring only assumption-provision, problem-solving success, and educational transmission, cannot distinguish a framework whose assumptions are empirically accountable from one whose assumptions are empirically insulated.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) A appeals to a covering law under which P is subsumed, (ii) the initial conditions cited in A are true, and (iii) the deduction from law and initial conditions to P is logically valid.
conditions: A appeals to a covering law under which P is subsumed
The initial conditions cited in A are true
The deduction from law and initial conditions to P is logically valid
counterexample: Consider a physician who explains why Mr Smith has not become pregnant by citing the covering law that all persons who take oral contraceptive pills regularly and are male do not become pregnant, together with the true initial conditions that Mr Smith is male and takes oral contraceptive pills regularly. The deduction is logically valid. Yet we should not regard this as explaining why Mr Smith has not become pregnant; his sex alone suffices, and the contraceptive pills are plainly irrelevant.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the conditions and law cited be explanatorily relevant to the phenomenon — that each element of the explanans make a genuine difference to the occurrence of P, rather than being a logically dispensable addendum.
passage: One might propose that a satisfactory explanation consists in a logically valid deduction of a phenomenon from a covering law together with true initial conditions. This proposal captures the deductive and empirical features that many philosophers have associated with explanatory adequacy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from medical reasoning. Suppose a physician is asked why Mr Smith has not become pregnant. The physician cites the law that all persons who take oral contraceptive pills regularly and are male do not become pregnant, together with the true conditions that Mr Smith is male and does take such pills. The deduction is logically valid. The law is true. The initial conditions obtain. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this as a satisfactory explanation. The contraceptive pills are explanatorily idle; Mr Smith's sex alone accounts for his not being pregnant. It appears that the definition, by requiring only subsumption under a true law with true conditions and a valid deduction, cannot distinguish explanations whose elements are all genuinely relevant from those that incorporate irrelevant but logically harmless additions. One might observe that explanatory adequacy requires something more than deductive correctness — it demands that each component of the explanans make a real difference to the phenomenon explained.

definition: A proposition H is scientifically meaningful if and only if (i) H makes a definite empirical claim about the observable world, (ii) there exists at least one logically possible observation that would refute H, and (iii) H has been formulated with sufficient precision to distinguish it from competing hypotheses.
conditions: H makes a definite empirical claim about the observable world
There exists at least one logically possible observation that would refute H
H has been formulated with sufficient precision to distinguish it from competing hypotheses
counterexample: Consider the hypothesis that all ravens are black. It makes a definite empirical claim, it is refutable by the observation of a non-black raven, and it is precise enough to be distinguished from the hypothesis that all ravens are grey. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the hypothesis is logically equivalent to the claim that all non-black things are non-ravens, which is confirmed by the observation of a white shoe. If scientific meaningfulness is determined by these conditions alone, one is committed to the view that observing footwear is relevant to ornithological claims, which seems absurd.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the observations relevant to H bear a substantive connection to the subject matter of H — that the confirming and disconfirming instances be drawn from the domain to which H pertains, rather than from logically equivalent reformulations whose instances may be entirely unrelated.
passage: One might characterise scientific meaningfulness as the property of a proposition that makes a definite empirical claim, is refutable by at least one logically possible observation, and is precise enough to be distinguished from its competitors. This formulation aims to separate genuine empirical hypotheses from unfalsifiable metaphysical pronouncements. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises from the logic of confirmation. Consider the hypothesis that all ravens are black. It plainly makes a definite empirical claim about observable birds. It is refutable: a single non-black raven would suffice. It is precise enough to be distinguished from the hypothesis that all ravens are grey or brown. All three conditions are met. However, the hypothesis is logically equivalent to the claim that all non-black things are non-ravens. By the lights of standard logic, any observation that confirms the latter must confirm the former. Thus the observation of a white shoe — a non-black thing that is a non-raven — confirms the hypothesis that all ravens are black. If one accepts the three conditions as sufficient, one must accept that observing footwear is relevant to claims about ornithology. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the logical structure of the hypothesis, cannot discriminate between observations that are substantively relevant to the hypothesis and those that are merely formally related through logical equivalence. One might observe that scientific meaningfulness requires some constraint upon the domain from which confirming and disconfirming instances are to be drawn.

definition: A representation M constitutes an adequate scientific model of a target system S if and only if (i) M shares structural features with S, (ii) M enables the derivation of correct predictions about the behaviour of S, and (iii) M is simpler than S in at least one significant respect.
conditions: M shares structural features with S
M enables the derivation of correct predictions about the behaviour of S
M is simpler than S in at least one significant respect
counterexample: Consider a map of London drawn at a scale of one-to-one. It shares all structural features with the city. It enables the derivation of correct predictions about distances, routes, and relative positions. It is not simpler in any respect — indeed, it is identical in scale to the city itself. But now suppose we produce a slightly simplified version by omitting a single lamp-post. The resulting map shares structural features with London, yields correct predictions about virtually everything, and is simpler than the city in at least one respect. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the map is not a scientific model of London in any useful sense; it is merely a near-replica that omits a trivial detail.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the simplification effected by M be theoretically motivated — that the features omitted or idealised be precisely those whose omission illuminates the principles governing the behaviour of S, rather than an arbitrary reduction that leaves M as complex and uninstructive as the original.
passage: One might define an adequate scientific model as a representation that shares structural features with its target, enables the derivation of correct predictions, and is simpler than the target in at least one respect. This characterisation captures the representational, predictive, and simplifying functions we associate with modelling. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed by considering maps. Imagine a map of London produced at a one-to-one scale — a full-size replica of the city in every detail. Such a map shares all structural features with its target and enables perfectly correct predictions but is not simpler than London at all. Now suppose we modify this map by omitting a single lamp-post from a minor street. The modified map shares structural features with London. It enables the derivation of correct predictions about nearly every feature of the city. And it is simpler than London in at least one respect: it has one fewer lamp-post. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet one might observe that this near-replica is not a scientific model in any recognisable sense. It does not illuminate the principles governing urban geography or transport; it merely reproduces the city with a trivial omission. It appears that the definition, by requiring only some simplification without specifying that the simplification be theoretically motivated, cannot distinguish a genuine model — one whose idealisations reveal the structure of phenomena — from a near-copy that omits an arbitrary detail.

definition: A system of classification C is natural if and only if (i) C groups entities according to properties they actually possess, (ii) the groups in C are mutually exclusive, and (iii) the groups in C are jointly exhaustive of the domain under consideration.
conditions: C groups entities according to properties they actually possess
The groups in C are mutually exclusive
The groups in C are jointly exhaustive of the domain under consideration
counterexample: Consider a classification of all animals into two groups: those whose names in English begin with a vowel and those whose names begin with a consonant. This classification groups entities according to a property they actually possess — the initial letter of their English name. The groups are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the classification is plainly artificial; it reflects the accidents of English orthography rather than anything about the animals themselves.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the properties upon which the classification is based be intrinsic to the entities classified and theoretically significant — that the grouping reflect genuine similarities in nature rather than extrinsic or conventional features.
passage: One might propose that a natural classification is one that groups entities according to properties they actually possess, with groups that are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive of the domain. This characterisation appears to capture the objectivity and completeness we expect of a well-formed taxonomy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be readily constructed. Consider a classification of all animals into two groups: those whose English names begin with a vowel — ape, elk, ibis, octopus, urchin — and those whose English names begin with a consonant. This classification groups animals according to a property they actually possess: the initial letter of their English name is a perfectly definite attribute. The groups are mutually exclusive; no animal's name begins with both a vowel and a consonant. The groups are jointly exhaustive; every name begins with some letter. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to call this classification natural. It reflects nothing about the biology, anatomy, or evolutionary relationships of the organisms. The property in question is extrinsic — it pertains to the conventions of a particular human language, not to the animals themselves. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that entities be grouped according to properties they actually possess, cannot distinguish properties that are intrinsic and theoretically illuminating from those that are merely conventional or accidental.

definition: A body of scientific practice P constitutes a paradigm if and only if (i) P provides a set of standard problems and methods accepted by a community of practitioners, (ii) P has generated solutions to a significant number of those problems, and (iii) no rival body of practice has solved more of the problems addressed by P.
conditions: P provides a set of standard problems and methods accepted by a community of practitioners
P has generated solutions to a significant number of those problems
No rival body of practice has solved more of the problems addressed by P
counterexample: Consider a medieval guild of astrologers whose practice provides a set of standard problems — predicting the fate of kings, the outcomes of harvests, the fortunes of marriages — together with accepted methods involving planetary charts and horoscopes. The practice has generated solutions to a significant number of these problems, in the sense that predictions have been issued for each. No rival body of practice has solved more of these astrological problems. All three conditions are met. Yet the astrological practice is not a scientific paradigm; its solutions are not genuinely adequate, for the predictions are no more accurate than chance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the solutions generated by P be genuinely adequate — that they satisfy independent standards of accuracy, rigour, or empirical success, rather than merely counting as solutions by the internal criteria of the community itself.
passage: One might characterise a paradigm as a body of scientific practice that provides standard problems and methods accepted by a community, has generated solutions to many of those problems, and faces no rival that has solved more of them. This formulation aims to capture the way paradigms organise scientific activity and accumulate achievements. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of astrology. Consider a medieval guild of astrologers whose practice provides a well-defined set of problems: predicting the fortunes of monarchs, the outcomes of agricultural seasons, the prospects of marriages. The guild has accepted methods — the casting of horoscopes, the interpretation of planetary conjunctions — and has applied them to a significant number of these problems, producing predictions for each. No rival practice addresses these same problems with greater success, for the simple reason that no rival practice takes them up at all. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that astrological practice is not a scientific paradigm in any defensible sense. Its predictions are no more accurate than those generated by chance. The solutions it has produced are solutions only by its own internal standards. It appears that the definition, by measuring success against the community's own problems without requiring that the solutions meet independent standards of empirical adequacy, cannot distinguish a genuine paradigm from an entrenched pseudoscientific tradition.

definition: A statement S counts as a successful prediction of a theory T if and only if (i) S is derived from T before the observation that confirms it, (ii) S specifies an observable outcome with sufficient precision to be tested, and (iii) the specified outcome is subsequently observed.
conditions: S is derived from T before the observation that confirms it
S specifies an observable outcome with sufficient precision to be tested
The specified outcome is subsequently observed
counterexample: Consider an astrologer who derives, from the principles of astrology, the prediction that a person born under Aries will experience a significant change in the coming month. The prediction is derived before the observation. It is precise enough to be tested — one can observe whether a significant change occurs. A significant change is subsequently observed: the person moves house. All three conditions are met. Yet the prediction succeeds only because the category of a significant change is so broad that virtually any month will contain something that qualifies. The prediction could hardly have failed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the prediction carry a genuine risk of failure — that the class of outcomes consistent with the prediction be narrow enough that the prediction would have been disconfirmed by a substantial range of possible observations.
passage: One might define a successful prediction as a statement derived from a theory before the confirming observation, sufficiently precise to be tested, and subsequently observed. This formulation is intended to capture the temporal priority and empirical specificity that distinguish genuine predictions from post hoc rationalisations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from astrological practice. Suppose an astrologer derives from the principles of her discipline the prediction that a person born under Aries will experience a significant change during the coming month. The prediction is derived before any observation of the month's events. It specifies an observable outcome — a significant change — with apparent precision. And the outcome is observed: the person changes employment, or moves house, or falls ill. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the prediction succeeds only because its terms are so accommodating. The category of a significant change encompasses so vast a range of occurrences that virtually any month in any person's life would furnish a confirming instance. The prediction carries no genuine risk of failure. It appears that the definition, by requiring only temporal priority, testability, and subsequent confirmation, cannot distinguish predictions that are genuinely informative from those whose apparent success is guaranteed by the breadth of the outcome class they specify.

definition: A generalisation L qualifies as a scientific law if and only if (i) L is exceptionless within its specified domain, (ii) L supports counterfactual conditionals, and (iii) L has been confirmed by a substantial body of observational or experimental evidence.
conditions: L is exceptionless within its specified domain
L supports counterfactual conditionals
L has been confirmed by a substantial body of observational or experimental evidence
counterexample: Consider the generalisation that all screws in a particular antique clock are made of brass. Suppose this is exceptionless — every screw has been examined and found to be brass. The generalisation supports counterfactuals: if a screw were added to this clock (by the original craftsman, under the original workshop conditions), it would be brass, given her consistent practice. And the generalisation has been confirmed by thorough inspection of every screw. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this is plainly not a law of nature; it is an accidental regularity reflecting the craftsman's preferences.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that L hold by virtue of the natures or physical constitutions of the entities involved, rather than by virtue of contingent circumstances such as the habits or preferences of particular agents.
passage: One might define a scientific law as an exceptionless generalisation that supports counterfactual conditionals and has been confirmed by substantial evidence. This tripartite characterisation aims to exclude accidental generalisations, which are traditionally held to fail the counterfactual test. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed. Consider an antique clock whose every screw has been fashioned from brass by a single master craftsman. The generalisation that all screws in the clock are brass is exceptionless within its domain. It supports the relevant counterfactuals: had an additional screw been required, the craftsman — given her unwavering habits and the brass stock in her workshop — would have made it from brass. And the generalisation has been confirmed by the thorough examination of each screw. All three conditions appear satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this is not a law of nature. It reflects the contingent preferences and circumstances of a particular artisan, not anything about the physical constitution of screws or clocks as such. It appears that the definition, by requiring only exceptionlessness, counterfactual support, and confirmation, cannot distinguish genuine natural laws from regularities that owe their exceptionlessness and counterfactual robustness to the fixed habits of individual agents rather than to the natures of the kinds involved.

definition: A theory T1 is reducible to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every term of T1 can be defined using the vocabulary of T2, (ii) every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2 together with these definitions, and (iii) T2 explains everything that T1 explains.
conditions: Every term of T1 can be defined using the vocabulary of T2
Every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2 together with these definitions
T2 explains everything that T1 explains
counterexample: Consider the reduction of thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. The thermodynamic term 'temperature' can be defined as mean kinetic energy. The gas laws can be derived from statistical mechanics. And statistical mechanics explains everything thermodynamics explains. All three conditions appear met. Yet the second law of thermodynamics, formulated thermodynamically as an exceptionless law, can only be derived statistically — as overwhelmingly probable but not strictly exceptionless. The derivation alters the modal status of the law, replacing certainty with near-certainty. The reduction is approximate, not exact.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the derivation preserve the modal status of the laws — that laws which are exceptionless in T1 be derivable as exceptionless in T2, rather than as merely overwhelmingly probable approximations.
passage: One might propose that a theory is reducible to another when all terms of the first can be defined in the vocabulary of the second, all laws of the first derived from the second, and all explanations of the first preserved. This is a familiar and attractive account. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the relationship between thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. The thermodynamic concept of temperature can be defined as mean molecular kinetic energy. The ideal gas law and other thermodynamic regularities can be derived from statistical-mechanical principles. Statistical mechanics provides explanations of all the phenomena thermodynamics addresses. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe a difficulty with the second law. In thermodynamics, the second law states that entropy in an isolated system never decreases — it is exceptionless. In statistical mechanics, the corresponding result is that entropy decrease is overwhelmingly improbable but not strictly impossible. The derivation replaces an exceptionless law with a statistical approximation. The modal character of the law has been altered in transit. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that laws be derivable without specifying that the derivation preserve their modal status, permits reductions in which exact laws are replaced by their statistical shadows.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a genuine measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M produces a numerical output, (ii) the output of M covaries systematically with Q, and (iii) M can be calibrated against a known standard for Q.
conditions: M produces a numerical output
The output of M covaries systematically with Q
M can be calibrated against a known standard for Q
counterexample: Consider measuring atmospheric pressure by counting the number of seagulls visible from a coastal observation post. Suppose it is discovered that, in a particular location, the number of visible seagulls covaries systematically with atmospheric pressure — more gulls appear in high pressure, fewer in low. The procedure produces a numerical output (the count), covaries with pressure, and can be calibrated against a barometer. All three conditions are met. Yet the gull-count is not a genuine measurement of atmospheric pressure; it exploits a merely accidental correlation that may break down when conditions change.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the covariation between M and Q be grounded in a lawful or causal connection, rather than in a merely accidental or local correlation that is not projectable to new circumstances.
passage: One might define a genuine measurement as a procedure that produces a numerical output, covaries systematically with the quantity of interest, and can be calibrated against a known standard. This characterisation captures the quantitative, responsive, and standardisable features we associate with measurement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from an unusual observational practice. Suppose a coastal naturalist discovers that, at her particular observation post, the number of seagulls visible from the cliff covaries systematically with atmospheric pressure. When pressure is high, the gulls congregate near the shore; when it falls, they disperse inland. The gull-count produces a numerical output. It covaries with atmospheric pressure. It can be calibrated against a mercury barometer. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that counting seagulls is not a genuine measurement of atmospheric pressure. The correlation is contingent upon a host of local factors — the particular geography, the feeding habits of this colony, the direction of prevailing winds — and may vanish entirely at a different location or in a different season. It appears that the definition, by requiring only systematic covariation and calibrability, cannot distinguish a measurement grounded in a lawful or causal connection from one that exploits a merely accidental and non-projectable correlation.

definition: A set of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T provides a unified account of a range of phenomena, (ii) T is logically consistent, and (iii) every proposition in T is individually well-confirmed by empirical evidence.
conditions: T provides a unified account of a range of phenomena
T is logically consistent
Every proposition in T is individually well-confirmed by empirical evidence
counterexample: Consider a set of propositions consisting of the well-confirmed claim that water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at standard pressure, the well-confirmed claim that copper conducts electricity, and the well-confirmed claim that whales are mammals. Each proposition is individually well-confirmed. The set is logically consistent. And one might claim it provides a unified account of diverse phenomena. All three conditions are met. Yet this collection is not a scientific theory; it is a miscellany of individually adequate claims that share no explanatory principles.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the propositions of T be integrated by shared explanatory principles or mechanisms — that their unification be substantive rather than merely aggregative.
passage: One might characterise a scientific theory as a logically consistent set of individually well-confirmed propositions that provides a unified account of a range of phenomena. This seems to combine the desiderata of coherence, empirical support, and explanatory breadth. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed by simple aggregation. Consider a set comprising three propositions: that water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at standard pressure, that copper conducts electricity, and that whales are mammals. Each proposition is individually well-confirmed by extensive empirical evidence. The set is logically consistent — no proposition contradicts another. And one might claim, with some ingenuity, that the set provides a unified account of diverse natural phenomena, ranging from thermodynamics to biology to materials science. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet one might observe that this collection is not a scientific theory in any substantive sense. The propositions share no explanatory principles, invoke no common mechanisms, and illuminate no connections among their respective domains. It appears that the definition, by requiring only individual confirmation and logical consistency, cannot distinguish a genuine theory — whose propositions are woven together by shared principles — from a mere catalogue of independently adequate empirical claims.

definition: A procedure E constitutes a genuine scientific experiment if and only if (i) E involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable, (ii) E is designed to test a specific hypothesis, and (iii) E yields a determinate observational outcome that can be recorded and communicated.
conditions: E involves the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable
E is designed to test a specific hypothesis
E yields a determinate observational outcome that can be recorded and communicated
counterexample: Consider a child who, out of curiosity, mixes together several household liquids — vinegar, food colouring, and washing-up soap — in a bowl. The child deliberately manipulates several variables. The child has the hypothesis that the mixture will fizz. The procedure yields a determinate outcome: the mixture does indeed fizz, and the child records this in a notebook. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this a genuine scientific experiment, for the child has no control group, no means of isolating which variable produced the fizzing, and no systematic method for ruling out alternative explanations.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that E include provisions for isolating the effect of the manipulated variable from confounding factors — that the design incorporate controls or comparison conditions sufficient to warrant the attribution of the observed outcome to the hypothesised cause.
passage: One might propose that a genuine scientific experiment is a procedure involving the deliberate manipulation of at least one variable, designed to test a specific hypothesis, and yielding a determinate observational outcome. This characterisation captures the active, hypothesis-directed nature of experimental inquiry, and it excludes mere passive observation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from ordinary domestic life. Suppose a child, having formed the hypothesis that mixing certain household liquids will produce a fizzing reaction, deliberately combines vinegar, food colouring, and washing-up soap in a bowl. The child manipulates several variables — the quantities and types of liquids — with the specific aim of testing the fizzing hypothesis. The result is determinate: the mixture fizzes, and the child dutifully records the outcome in a notebook. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this as a genuine scientific experiment. The child has introduced multiple substances simultaneously and possesses no means of determining which substance or combination of substances is responsible for the fizzing. There is no control condition against which the outcome might be compared. The procedure cannot isolate the causal contribution of any single variable. It appears that the definition, by requiring only deliberate manipulation, a testable hypothesis, and a recordable outcome, fails to demand what is essential to experimentation properly so called — namely, the systematic isolation of the variable under investigation from confounding factors through the use of controls and comparison conditions.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a valid measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M assigns a numerical value to Q, (ii) the value assigned by M is reproducible under the same conditions, and (iii) M has been calibrated against a recognised standard for Q.
conditions: M assigns a numerical value to Q
The value assigned by M is reproducible under the same conditions
M has been calibrated against a recognised standard for Q
counterexample: Suppose a physician measures a patient's health by counting the number of letters in the patient's surname, and suppose the clinic's protocol calibrates this procedure against a standard table that assigns numerical health scores to different name-lengths. The procedure assigns a numerical value to health. The value is perfectly reproducible — the same name always yields the same count. The procedure has been calibrated against a recognised standard within the clinic. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not regard this as a valid measurement of health, for the number of letters in a surname bears no systematic connection to the patient's physiological condition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the procedure M be sensitive to actual variations in Q — that changes in the quantity being measured produce corresponding changes in the numerical output, so that the assignment reflects a genuine empirical relationship between the procedure and the quantity rather than a merely conventional or arbitrary correlation.
passage: One might define a valid measurement as a procedure that assigns a numerical value to a quantity, is reproducible under the same conditions, and has been calibrated against a recognised standard. This formulation captures the quantitative, reliable, and standardised character that we associate with measurement in the sciences. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from medical practice. Suppose a physician adopts the procedure of measuring a patient's health by counting the number of letters in the patient's surname. The clinic has established a standard table that assigns health scores to various name-lengths, so the procedure is calibrated. The numerical assignment is perfectly reproducible: the same patient will always receive the same score, since surnames do not ordinarily change. And the procedure assigns a definite numerical value to health. All three conditions are fulfilled. Nevertheless, one might observe that this is not a valid measurement of health in any recognisable sense. The number of letters in a surname bears no systematic relation to physiological condition. A change in the patient's actual health — an improvement or deterioration — would produce no corresponding change in the assigned value. It appears that the definition, by requiring only numerical assignment, reproducibility, and calibration, fails to require that the procedure be sensitive to genuine variations in the quantity it purports to measure. Without this sensitivity, the numerical output reflects nothing more than an arbitrary convention.

definition: A statement S constitutes a successful scientific prediction if and only if (i) S was derived from a theory T prior to the observation of the predicted event, (ii) the event described by S subsequently occurred, and (iii) S specifies the event with sufficient precision to be distinguished from vague or trivial forecasts.
conditions: S was derived from a theory T prior to the observation of the predicted event
The event described by S subsequently occurred
S specifies the event with sufficient precision to be distinguished from vague or trivial forecasts
counterexample: Suppose an astrologer derives from astrological theory the precise prediction that a volcano in Iceland will erupt on the fourteenth of March, and suppose that by pure coincidence a volcano in Iceland does erupt on that date. The prediction was derived from a theory prior to the event. The event occurred. The prediction was precise. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this a successful scientific prediction, for the theory from which it was derived has no genuine connection to volcanic activity, and the correspondence between prediction and event is entirely accidental.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the theory T from which S is derived have a genuine explanatory or causal connection to the domain of phenomena to which the prediction pertains — that the success of the prediction be non-accidental and traceable to the adequacy of the theory rather than to coincidence.
passage: One might characterise a successful scientific prediction as a statement derived from a theory in advance of the predicted event, where the event subsequently occurs and the statement specifies it with sufficient precision. This formulation is designed to exclude vague prophecies and post hoc rationalisations, and it captures the temporal and specificity requirements that distinguish scientific forecasting from guesswork. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the practice of astrology. Suppose an astrologer, working from the principles of astrological theory, derives the precise prediction that a volcano in Iceland will erupt on the fourteenth of March of the coming year. As it happens, by sheer coincidence, a volcano in Iceland does erupt on exactly that date. The prediction was derived from a theory — astrological theory — prior to the observation of the eruption. The event occurred precisely as predicted. The prediction was specific: a particular country, a particular date, a particular type of geological event. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should not regard this as a successful scientific prediction. The astrological theory from which the prediction was derived bears no genuine causal or explanatory relation to volcanic activity. The correspondence between forecast and outcome is entirely fortuitous. It appears that the definition, by attending only to temporal priority, occurrence, and precision, cannot distinguish predictions whose success reflects the adequacy of the underlying theory from those whose success is a matter of pure coincidence.

definition: A theory T1 is reducible to a more fundamental theory T2 if and only if (i) every term of T1 can be translated into the vocabulary of T2, (ii) every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2 together with appropriate bridge principles, and (iii) T2 explains all the phenomena that T1 explains.
conditions: Every term of T1 can be translated into the vocabulary of T2
Every law of T1 can be derived from the laws of T2 together with appropriate bridge principles
T2 explains all the phenomena that T1 explains
counterexample: Suppose one claims that folk psychology is reducible to a fictional neuroscience whose vocabulary includes terms for every folk-psychological state — each folk term is translated by stipulative definition into a neuro-term, the laws of folk psychology are derived trivially from the stipulated bridge principles, and the fictional neuroscience thereby explains whatever folk psychology explains. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet no genuine reduction has been achieved, for the bridge principles are mere stipulations rather than empirical discoveries, and the supposed explanatory gain is illusory.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the bridge principles connecting T1 and T2 be empirically grounded — that the translations and derivations reflect genuine discoveries about the relationship between the domains of the two theories, rather than stipulative or trivially constructed correspondences.
passage: One might propose that a theory is reducible to a more fundamental theory when every term of the former can be translated into the vocabulary of the latter, every law of the former can be derived from the laws of the latter with bridge principles, and the latter explains all the phenomena explained by the former. This characterisation aims to capture the sense in which reduction achieves genuine unification by showing that one domain of phenomena is really a special case of another. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed by considering a fictional neuroscience. Suppose one wishes to reduce folk psychology to a neuroscientific theory. One proceeds by inventing, for each folk-psychological term — desire, intention, fear — a corresponding neuroscientific term, defined stipulatively as whatever brain state happens to correlate with the folk state in question. One then formulates bridge principles connecting each folk term to its stipulated neuroscientific counterpart. From these bridge principles, every law of folk psychology is trivially derivable. The resulting neuroscience explains all the phenomena that folk psychology explains, since it simply redescribes them in a new vocabulary. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that no genuine reduction has been achieved. The bridge principles are not empirical discoveries about the relationship between psychological and neural phenomena; they are mere stipulations. The derivations are trivial consequences of these stipulations. The supposed explanatory gain is illusory, for no deeper understanding of psychological phenomena has been attained. It appears that the definition cannot distinguish genuine reductions — in which the bridge principles embody substantive empirical findings — from trivially manufactured correspondences.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a well-confirmed scientific theory if and only if (i) T entails a range of observational predictions, (ii) a significant number of those predictions have been verified, and (iii) no verified prediction has been inconsistent with T.
conditions: T entails a range of observational predictions
A significant number of those predictions have been verified
No verified prediction has been inconsistent with T
counterexample: Consider a theory that asserts only that the sun will rise each morning and that the tides will ebb and flow. This body of propositions entails a range of observational predictions. A very significant number of those predictions have been verified over many centuries. No verified prediction has been inconsistent with the theory. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not regard this as a well-confirmed scientific theory, for the propositions are merely a restatement of observed regularities and entail no predictions beyond what is already known from direct observation; the theory has never been tested against a novel or surprising prediction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that T entail novel predictions — predictions concerning phenomena not already known at the time T was formulated — so that verification amounts to a genuine test of the theory rather than a mere recapitulation of the observations from which the theory was originally derived.
passage: One might define a well-confirmed scientific theory as a body of propositions that entails a range of observational predictions, a significant number of which have been verified, with none found inconsistent. This characterisation captures the empirical orientation and track record of success that we associate with confirmed theories. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the most ordinary observations. Consider a body of propositions asserting only that the sun rises each morning and that the tides ebb and flow at regular intervals. These propositions entail observational predictions: that the sun will rise tomorrow, that the tide will come in this afternoon. A very great number of such predictions have been verified over the course of centuries. No observation has contradicted them. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to call this a well-confirmed scientific theory. The propositions are nothing more than a restatement of observed regularities; they entail no predictions beyond what has already been directly witnessed. Their so-called confirmation amounts to no more than a repeated observation of the facts from which the propositions were originally derived. One might observe that what distinguishes a genuinely confirmed theory from a mere summary of past observations is that the theory entails novel predictions — predictions concerning phenomena not already known — whose subsequent verification provides evidence that the theory captures something beyond the data that prompted its formulation.

definition: A report R constitutes an objective scientific observation if and only if (i) R describes a state of affairs directly accessible to the senses, (ii) R is expressed in language free from theoretical interpretation, and (iii) R can be confirmed by any competent observer under the same conditions.
conditions: R describes a state of affairs directly accessible to the senses
R is expressed in language free from theoretical interpretation
R can be confirmed by any competent observer under the same conditions
counterexample: Suppose a person reports that a stick half-submerged in water is bent. The report describes something directly accessible to the senses — the stick looks bent. The language is free from theoretical interpretation; no physical theory is invoked. Any competent observer looking at the stick under the same conditions would confirm that it appears bent. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the observation is, in a significant sense, not objective: the stick is straight, and its apparent curvature is an artefact of the refraction of light at the water's surface. The report accurately records an appearance but misrepresents the actual state of affairs.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the state of affairs described by R correspond to the actual condition of the object observed rather than to a perceptual appearance produced by the conditions of observation — that R be corrected for systematic distortions introduced by the medium or circumstances of perception.
passage: One might propose that an objective scientific observation is a report that describes a state of affairs directly accessible to the senses, is expressed without theoretical interpretation, and can be confirmed by any competent observer under the same conditions. This characterisation aims to secure the objectivity and intersubjective accessibility that we expect of observational reports in science. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from an everyday optical phenomenon. Consider a person who reports that a straight stick, half-submerged in a glass of water, is bent. The report describes something directly accessible to the senses: the stick genuinely appears to be bent. The language is free from theoretical interpretation — no mention is made of light, refraction, or optical physics. Any competent observer placed before the same glass of water would confirm the very same appearance. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that this report is not an objective observation in the relevant sense. The stick is in fact straight; its apparent curvature is an artefact produced by the refraction of light at the boundary between water and air. The report faithfully records a perceptual appearance but misrepresents the actual state of affairs. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the report describe a sensory experience, use non-theoretical language, and be intersubjectively confirmable, cannot distinguish reports that accurately characterise the objects they concern from reports that faithfully record a systematically distorted appearance.

definition: A generalisation L qualifies as a scientific law if and only if (i) L is universally quantified over a specified domain, (ii) L supports counterfactual conditionals, and (iii) L has been derived from or confirmed by systematic empirical investigation.
conditions: L is universally quantified over a specified domain
L supports counterfactual conditionals
L has been derived from or confirmed by systematic empirical investigation
counterexample: Consider the generalisation that all the coins in the collection of a certain numismatist are made of silver. The generalisation is universally quantified over the specified domain of coins in the collection. It supports counterfactual conditionals: if a new coin were added to the collection, we may say — given the collector's known preference — that it would be silver. And it has been confirmed by systematic empirical investigation, for the numismatist has catalogued every coin. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not regard this as a scientific law; it describes the contingent preferences of a particular collector and has no nomological force.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that L express a physical or natural necessity rather than a contingent regularity sustained by the accidental or volitional arrangements of a particular situation — that L hold by virtue of the natures of the entities in its domain rather than by virtue of some extrinsic circumstance.
passage: One might characterise a scientific law as a universally quantified generalisation that supports counterfactual conditionals and has been confirmed by systematic empirical investigation. This formulation aims to distinguish laws from accidental generalisations by insisting upon counterfactual support, which is widely regarded as the hallmark of nomological statements. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed from numismatics. Suppose a particular collector has, over many decades, assembled a large collection of coins, every one of which is made of silver. The generalisation that all coins in the collection are silver is universally quantified over a specified domain. It supports a counterfactual: were a coin to be added to this collection, it would be silver, since the collector is known never to acquire non-silver coins. And the generalisation has been confirmed by systematic investigation — the collector has catalogued and tested every piece. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this generalisation as a scientific law. It describes the contingent effects of a particular person's preferences. It holds not because of any physical necessity binding coins to silver but because of the collector's volitional choices. It appears that the definition, by requiring only universal quantification, counterfactual support, and empirical confirmation, cannot distinguish generalisations that express genuine natural necessities from those that owe their universality and counterfactual robustness to the deliberate arrangements of particular agents.

definition: A transition from one body of scientific practice P1 to another P2 constitutes a scientific revolution if and only if (i) P1 and P2 address the same domain of phenomena, (ii) P2 solves problems that were anomalous under P1, and (iii) the transition from P1 to P2 was not gradual but occurred within a relatively brief period.
conditions: P1 and P2 address the same domain of phenomena
P2 solves problems that were anomalous under P1
The transition from P1 to P2 was not gradual but occurred within a relatively brief period
counterexample: Consider a medical faculty that abruptly replaces one textbook of anatomy with another after a publisher's dispute. The old and new textbooks address the same domain — human anatomy. The new textbook corrects several errors that were anomalous under the old one. The transition occurred within a single academic term. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this is not a scientific revolution; it is merely an administrative substitution of one text for another, involving no fundamental reconception of the phenomena.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the transition involve a fundamental reconception of the principles, methods, or categories by which the domain is understood — that the change be conceptually revolutionary rather than merely a rapid correction of errors within an unchanged framework.
passage: One might define a scientific revolution as a transition from one body of practice to another that addresses the same domain, where the new practice solves problems anomalous under the old, and the transition is rapid rather than gradual. This characterisation captures the problem-solving progress and abrupt character that historians of science have associated with revolutionary episodes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from academic administration. Suppose a medical faculty, owing to a dispute with a publisher, abruptly replaces its standard anatomy textbook with a new one. The old textbook contained several errors — certain structures were mislabelled, certain measurements were inaccurate — and these errors had been noted as anomalies by students and instructors. The new textbook corrects these errors. Both textbooks address the same domain of phenomena: human anatomy. The new text solves problems that were anomalous under the old. And the transition occurred within a single academic term, which is certainly a brief period by the standards of scientific change. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that this is not a scientific revolution in any meaningful sense. The faculty has not undergone a fundamental reconception of human anatomy. No new principles, methods, or categories have been introduced. The change amounts to the replacement of a flawed text with a more accurate one, within an entirely unchanged theoretical framework. It appears that the definition, by attending only to domain overlap, anomaly resolution, and rapidity, cannot distinguish genuine intellectual revolutions from rapid but conceptually superficial corrections.

definition: A representation M constitutes a faithful scientific model of a system S if and only if (i) M preserves the relational structure among the components of S, (ii) M enables successful predictions about the future behaviour of S, and (iii) M is expressible in precise mathematical or formal language.
conditions: M preserves the relational structure among the components of S
M enables successful predictions about the future behaviour of S
M is expressible in precise mathematical or formal language
counterexample: Consider a model of the solar system in which each planet is represented by a number equal to its distance from the sun in astronomical units, and suppose the model uses these numbers, combined with a purely curve-fitting formula, to predict the positions of the planets at future dates. The model preserves the relational structure of the solar system — the ordering and spacing of the planets. It enables successful predictions. And it is expressed in mathematical language. All three conditions are met. Yet the model tells us nothing about why the planets move as they do; it merely encodes their past positions in a formula without representing the forces, masses, or dynamical laws that govern their motions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that M represent the causal or dynamical mechanisms responsible for the behaviour of S — that M do more than merely encode the relational structure and predictive regularities of S, and capture the processes by which the system's behaviour is produced.
passage: One might propose that a faithful scientific model is a representation that preserves the relational structure among the components of a system, enables successful predictions about the system's future behaviour, and is expressed in precise mathematical language. This characterisation aims to capture the structural, predictive, and formal virtues that we associate with good scientific modelling. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from astronomy. Suppose a model of the solar system assigns to each planet a number equal to its mean distance from the sun in astronomical units, and suppose a mathematician has devised a purely curve-fitting formula that, given these numbers and historical data about planetary positions, predicts the locations of each planet at future dates with considerable accuracy. The model preserves the relational structure of the solar system — the ordering and relative spacing of the planets are faithfully represented. It enables successful predictions about future planetary positions. And it is expressible in precise mathematical language. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to call this a faithful scientific model. The curve-fitting formula encodes regularities without representing the gravitational forces, planetary masses, or dynamical laws that actually govern the motions. The model is silent on the question of why the planets move as they do. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to structural preservation, predictive success, and formal precision, cannot distinguish a model that captures the causal mechanisms underlying a system's behaviour from one that merely summarises its observable patterns in a convenient mathematical garment.

definition: A classification C of entities in a scientific domain is adequate if and only if (i) C assigns every entity to exactly one category, (ii) the categories in C are defined by observable properties of the entities, and (iii) C facilitates the practical aims of the investigators who employ it.
conditions: C assigns every entity to exactly one category
The categories in C are defined by observable properties of the entities
C facilitates the practical aims of the investigators who employ it
counterexample: Consider a classification of stars that divides them into two categories: those visible from London on a clear night and those not so visible. Every star is assigned to exactly one category. The categories are defined by an observable property — visibility from a specified location. The classification facilitates the practical aim of London-based amateur astronomers who wish to plan their observations. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the classification is scientifically inadequate, for it reflects the accidental geographical position of the observer and the atmospheric conditions of a particular city rather than any intrinsic property of the stars themselves.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the observable properties upon which the categories are based be intrinsic to the entities classified and scientifically significant — that they reflect genuine natural groupings rather than features dependent upon the accidental circumstances of the observer.
passage: One might define an adequate scientific classification as one that assigns every entity to exactly one category, where the categories are defined by observable properties and the classification serves the practical aims of investigators. This formulation captures the exhaustiveness, observational grounding, and utility that we expect of scientific taxonomy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from stellar astronomy. Consider a classification that divides all stars into two groups: those visible from London on a clear night and those not visible from London on a clear night. Every star is assigned to exactly one category, since a star is either visible or not from that location under those conditions. The categories are defined by an observable property — visibility from a specified place. And the classification facilitates the practical aims of London-based amateur astronomers who wish to know which stars they can observe on any given evening. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this classification is scientifically inadequate. It tells us nothing about the physical nature of the stars — their luminosity, composition, temperature, or evolutionary stage. The classification reflects the accidental geographical position of a city and the atmospheric conditions prevailing there, rather than any intrinsic feature of the objects classified. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that categories be defined by observable properties without specifying that those properties be intrinsic and scientifically significant, cannot distinguish taxonomies that reveal the genuine structure of a domain from those that merely reflect the contingent circumstances of the observer.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory explanation of a scientific phenomenon P if and only if (i) A cites a general law or regularity under which P may be subsumed, (ii) the particular conditions cited in A are empirically verified, and (iii) the conjunction of the law and the particular conditions logically entails the occurrence of P.
conditions: A cites a general law or regularity under which P may be subsumed
The particular conditions cited in A are empirically verified
The conjunction of the law and the particular conditions logically entails the occurrence of P
counterexample: Suppose a meteorologist explains why a barometer reading fell by citing the well-confirmed regularity that barometer readings always fall before storms, together with the verified condition that a storm is approaching. The falling reading is logically entailed by the regularity and the condition. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this a satisfactory explanation, for the approaching storm is what causes both the falling barometer reading and the subsequent weather; the barometer reading is not explained by the storm's approach in the requisite sense, since the account fails to identify the mechanism — changes in atmospheric pressure — that actually produces the barometric change.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the law and conditions cited in A identify the causally operative factors responsible for P, rather than merely co-occurring conditions from which P happens to be logically derivable.
passage: One might propose that a satisfactory scientific explanation consists in the citation of a general law together with verified particular conditions from which the phenomenon to be explained may be logically derived. This characterisation captures the deductive structure that we associate with rigorous explanation and ensures that the explanans is both general and empirically grounded. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from meteorological practice. Suppose a meteorologist seeks to explain why a barometer reading has fallen. She cites the well-confirmed regularity that barometer readings invariably fall in advance of storms, together with the verified particular condition that a storm is approaching the region. The falling reading is logically entailed by the conjunction of the regularity and the particular condition. All three requirements of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this account does not constitute a satisfactory explanation of the barometric change. The approaching storm is not what produces the fall in the barometer reading; rather, both the falling reading and the approaching storm are produced by a common cause — a drop in atmospheric pressure. The account identifies a genuine correlation but mistakes it for an explanatory relation. It appears that the definition, by requiring only subsumption under a law, verified conditions, and logical entailment, fails to demand that the cited factors be causally responsible for the phenomenon. Without such a requirement, the definition cannot distinguish genuine explanations from accounts that merely cite reliable indicators or common effects of an unidentified underlying cause.

definition: A generalisation G constitutes a scientific law if and only if (i) G is a universally quantified conditional statement, (ii) G has no known exceptions, and (iii) G has been confirmed by a substantial body of observational evidence.
conditions: G is a universally quantified conditional statement
G has no known exceptions
G has been confirmed by a substantial body of observational evidence
counterexample: Consider the generalisation that all coins in the pocket of a particular philosopher on the fifteenth of June 1952 are silver. This is a universally quantified conditional: for all x, if x is a coin in that pocket on that date, then x is silver. It has no known exceptions, for the philosopher's pocket did indeed contain only silver coins. And it is confirmed by the substantial observational evidence of having examined each coin individually. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not regard this as a scientific law, for it is an accidental generalisation tied to a particular individual and date, lacking the kind of necessity or counterfactual force that distinguishes genuine laws.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that G support counterfactual conditionals — that G would continue to hold under hypothetical variations in initial conditions — thereby distinguishing genuinely lawlike generalisations from merely accidental ones.
passage: One might propose that a scientific law is a universally quantified conditional statement that has no known exceptions and has been confirmed by a substantial body of observational evidence. This characterisation captures the generality, exceptionlessness, and empirical support that we typically associate with laws of nature. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from an everyday situation. Suppose a philosopher, on the fifteenth of June 1952, has in his pocket exactly five coins, all of which happen to be silver. The generalisation that all coins in this philosopher's pocket on that date are silver is universally quantified: for every x, if x is a coin in the pocket on the specified date, then x is silver. The generalisation has no known exceptions, for each coin was examined and found to be silver. And it is confirmed by the observational evidence of having inspected each coin individually, which constitutes a substantial body of evidence relative to the domain in question. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this generalisation as a scientific law.
