It is an accidental truth, tied to the contingent circumstances of a particular person on a particular day. If the philosopher had placed a copper coin in his pocket, the generalisation would have been false. It lacks the counterfactual force that genuine laws possess: the law of gravitation tells us not merely that all unsupported bodies near the earth's surface do fall, but that any unsupported body would fall. The definition, by attending only to universality, exceptionlessness, and evidential support, cannot distinguish between genuine laws and accidental generalisations of this sort.

definition: A report R constitutes a reliable scientific observation if and only if (i) R is produced by a competent observer under appropriate conditions, (ii) R is expressed in the vocabulary of the relevant scientific discipline, and (iii) R can be independently corroborated by other observers using the same methods.
conditions: R is produced by a competent observer under appropriate conditions
R is expressed in the vocabulary of the relevant scientific discipline
R can be independently corroborated by other observers using the same methods
counterexample: Suppose a competent botanist, working under excellent conditions, reports that a certain plant specimen is of the species Quercus robur, using the standard botanical vocabulary. Other competent botanists, employing the same morphological methods, independently corroborate the identification. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the specimen is in fact a hybrid between Quercus robur and Quercus petraea whose morphological features happen to mimic those of pure Quercus robur exactly. The observation is unreliable, for the methods employed are systematically incapable of detecting hybridisation, and the report misidentifies the specimen despite satisfying every stated condition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the methods employed in producing R be capable of detecting the relevant features of the phenomenon being observed — that the observational procedure be sensitive to the distinctions upon which the accuracy of R depends.
passage: One might define a reliable scientific observation as a report produced by a competent observer under appropriate conditions, expressed in the vocabulary of the relevant discipline, and independently corroborable by other observers using the same methods. This characterisation captures the intersubjective, disciplinarily informed, and replicable character of good scientific observation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from botanical taxonomy. Suppose a competent botanist, working in excellent field conditions, examines a plant specimen and reports that it belongs to the species Quercus robur, employing standard botanical terminology. Several other competent botanists, using the same morphological methods of identification, independently examine the specimen and arrive at the same classification. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: the observer is competent, the report employs disciplinary vocabulary, and the observation is corroborated. Nevertheless, one might observe that the report is unreliable. Suppose the specimen is in fact a hybrid between Quercus robur and Quercus petraea — a hybrid whose morphological features happen to mimic those of pure Quercus robur so perfectly that no amount of morphological inspection could reveal the difference. The methods employed by every observer are systematically blind to the hybrid character of the plant; molecular analysis alone could detect it. It appears that the definition, by requiring only competence, disciplinary vocabulary, and independent corroboration by the same methods, fails to require that the methods be capable of detecting the features upon which the accuracy of the report depends. Corroboration by a method that is systematically insensitive to the relevant distinction provides no assurance of reliability.

definition: A framework F constitutes a scientific paradigm if and only if (i) F provides a set of fundamental assumptions shared by a community of practitioners, (ii) F defines the methods and standards by which research within the community is conducted, and (iii) F has guided the production of a substantial body of successful research.
conditions: F provides a set of fundamental assumptions shared by a community of practitioners
F defines the methods and standards by which research within the community is conducted
F has guided the production of a substantial body of successful research
counterexample: Consider the framework of phrenology in the early nineteenth century. Phrenologists shared a set of fundamental assumptions — that mental faculties are localised in specific brain regions and that skull topography reflects brain development. The framework defined the methods and standards of phrenological research: cranial measurement, correlation of skull features with character traits, and the collection of case studies. And it guided a substantial body of research that was regarded as successful by its practitioners, including published studies and institutional recognition. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to regard phrenology as a genuine scientific paradigm, for its successes were illusory, resting upon confirmation bias and the absence of controlled investigation, and the framework was incapable of recognising its own failures.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the successful research guided by F be genuinely progressive — that it produce novel predictions, solve previously intractable problems, or yield discoveries that withstand scrutiny by methods independent of the framework's own standards.
passage: One might characterise a scientific paradigm as a framework providing fundamental assumptions shared by a community, defining the methods and standards of research, and having guided the production of a substantial body of successful work. This formulation captures the communal, methodological, and productive aspects that we associate with paradigmatic science. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of phrenology. In the early nineteenth century, phrenologists constituted a well-defined community of practitioners who shared a set of fundamental assumptions: that mental faculties are localised in specific regions of the brain, and that the topography of the skull reflects the development of the underlying brain regions. The framework defined precise methods — cranial measurement, the systematic correlation of skull features with character traits, the accumulation of case studies — and it established standards by which research was judged adequate or inadequate within the community. Moreover, the framework guided a substantial body of research that was regarded by its practitioners as highly successful: hundreds of case studies were published, phrenological societies were established, and the field attracted both academic and public interest. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard phrenology as a genuine scientific paradigm. Its successes were illusory, sustained by confirmation bias and the systematic neglect of disconfirming evidence. The framework was incapable of recognising its own failures, for its methods and standards were constructed in such a way that virtually any observation could be accommodated. It appears that the definition, by requiring only shared assumptions, defined methods, and a body of research deemed successful, fails to demand that the research be genuinely progressive — that it produce novel discoveries and survive scrutiny by methods independent of the framework's own internal standards.

definition: A system S of classification is adequate for a scientific domain D if and only if (i) every entity in D is assigned to exactly one category by S, (ii) the categories of S are defined by observable features of the entities, and (iii) the categories are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive.
conditions: Every entity in D is assigned to exactly one category by S
The categories of S are defined by observable features of the entities
The categories are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive
counterexample: Suppose a zoologist classifies marine animals into two categories — those with fins and those without fins — based solely on the observable presence or absence of fin-like appendages. Every marine animal is assigned to exactly one category; the categories are defined by observable features; and they are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this classification places dolphins and tuna in the same category while separating dolphins from seals, thereby grouping together organisms with fundamentally different evolutionary histories and physiological constitutions. The classification fails to track the natural kinds that are relevant to biological science.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the categories of S reflect theoretically significant groupings — that they track natural divisions relevant to the explanatory and predictive purposes of the scientific domain, rather than merely partitioning entities by superficial observable similarities.
passage: One might propose that an adequate scientific classification is one in which every entity in the domain is assigned to exactly one category, the categories are defined by observable features, and the categories are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive. This characterisation captures the systematic, observation-based, and comprehensive nature of good classification. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed from marine zoology. Suppose a zoologist devises a classification of marine animals based solely on the observable presence or absence of fin-like appendages. Every marine animal is assigned to exactly one of two categories: those possessing fins and those lacking fins. The categories are defined entirely by an observable morphological feature. They are mutually exclusive — no animal both possesses and lacks fins — and jointly exhaustive, since every marine animal either has fins or does not. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this classification is far from adequate for the purposes of biological science. It places dolphins and tuna in the same category, despite the fact that dolphins are air-breathing mammals with a fundamentally different evolutionary lineage and physiological constitution from tuna. At the same time, it separates dolphins from seals, despite their close phylogenetic relationship. The categories are drawn along lines of superficial morphological resemblance rather than along the theoretically significant divisions — evolutionary descent, physiological organisation, reproductive strategy — that the biological sciences require. It appears that the definition, by requiring only unique assignment, observational criteria, and exhaustiveness, fails to demand that the categories reflect genuinely natural groupings pertinent to the explanatory and predictive aims of the science in question.

definition: A representation M constitutes a useful scientific model of a system S if and only if (i) M reproduces the known behaviour of S under specified conditions, (ii) M is internally consistent, and (iii) M is amenable to mathematical or logical manipulation to derive further consequences.
conditions: M reproduces the known behaviour of S under specified conditions
M is internally consistent
M is amenable to mathematical or logical manipulation to derive further consequences
counterexample: Consider a model of economic markets that simply stores a lookup table of every previously observed price movement and, when queried, returns the historical datum that most closely matches the current conditions. This model reproduces all known market behaviour perfectly. It is internally consistent. And it is amenable to logical manipulation, since one may query it with novel inputs and obtain determinate outputs. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should not regard this as a useful scientific model, for it offers no insight into the mechanisms governing price movements and would fail catastrophically when confronted with genuinely novel market conditions that bear no close resemblance to any historical precedent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that M represent some structural or causal features of the system S, rather than merely cataloguing its past behaviour — that the model embody theoretical principles from which the behaviour of S under novel or unforeseen conditions might be anticipated.
passage: One might define a useful scientific model as a representation that reproduces the known behaviour of a system, is internally consistent, and is amenable to mathematical or logical manipulation for deriving further consequences. This formulation captures the empirical adequacy, logical coherence, and inferential fruitfulness that we expect of scientific models. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from economics. Suppose one builds a model of financial markets that consists entirely of a lookup table containing every previously observed price movement, indexed by the prevailing conditions at the time. When queried about a given set of market conditions, the model returns the historical datum whose associated conditions most closely match the query. This model reproduces all known market behaviour with perfect fidelity. It is internally consistent, since it merely reports historical facts. And it is amenable to logical manipulation: one may pose novel queries and receive determinate answers. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this is not a useful scientific model in any meaningful sense. It offers no insight into the mechanisms that produce price movements — no representation of supply and demand, of investor psychology, or of institutional constraints. When confronted with genuinely novel conditions that bear no close resemblance to any historical precedent, the model would fail entirely. It appears that the definition, by requiring only reproduction of known behaviour, consistency, and manipulability, fails to demand that the model represent structural or causal features of the system from which behaviour under novel conditions might be anticipated.

definition: A hypothesis H is scientifically testable if and only if (i) H makes at least one claim about the observable world, (ii) there is a specifiable observation O that would be logically incompatible with H, and (iii) O is in principle obtainable by some known empirical method.
conditions: H makes at least one claim about the observable world
There is a specifiable observation O that would be logically incompatible with H
O is in principle obtainable by some known empirical method
counterexample: Suppose someone advances the hypothesis that all ravens are black. This makes a claim about the observable world. The observation of a non-black raven is logically incompatible with it. And such an observation is readily obtainable by ornithological survey. All three conditions are met. Now consider the conjunction of this hypothesis with the auxiliary claim that any apparently non-black raven is in fact a different species misidentified as a raven. The conjunction still makes claims about the observable world and still admits of a specifiable incompatible observation, but in practice every putative refutation is deflected by the auxiliary hypothesis. The original hypothesis has been rendered untestable not by failing any of the three stated conditions but by embedding itself within an auxiliary framework that systematically neutralises disconfirming evidence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that H not be conjoined with auxiliary hypotheses whose sole function is to immunise H against refutation — that the testability of H be assessed independently of protective stratagems introduced ad hoc to preserve it.
passage: One might propose that a hypothesis is scientifically testable if it makes claims about the observable world, if there exists a specifiable observation logically incompatible with it, and if that observation is in principle obtainable by some known empirical method. This formulation aims to distinguish genuine scientific hypotheses from unfalsifiable speculation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the testing of simple generalisations. Consider the hypothesis that all ravens are black. It makes an observable claim, admits of a specifiable refuting observation — a non-black raven — and such an observation is readily obtainable. Now suppose the proponent of this hypothesis adds the auxiliary claim that any apparently non-black raven is in fact a member of a different species that has been misidentified. The conjunction of the original hypothesis with this auxiliary still makes claims about the observable world. A specifiable incompatible observation still exists in principle. The observation remains obtainable. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the hypothesis has been rendered effectively untestable, for every putative instance of a non-black raven is deflected by the auxiliary claim of misidentification. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the logical existence of an incompatible observation and its in-principle obtainability, fails to account for the role of ad hoc auxiliary hypotheses in immunising a hypothesis against refutation. Genuine testability, it appears, requires not merely that a refuting observation be logically possible but that the hypothesis not be embedded within a protective framework whose sole purpose is to neutralise any such observation before it can tell against the hypothesis.

definition: A procedure P counts as a controlled experiment if and only if (i) P involves the comparison of at least two groups that differ in exactly one specified variable, (ii) the assignment of subjects to groups is not influenced by the experimenter's expectations, and (iii) the outcome is measured by a predetermined quantitative criterion.
conditions: P involves the comparison of at least two groups that differ in exactly one specified variable
The assignment of subjects to groups is not influenced by the experimenter's expectations
The outcome is measured by a predetermined quantitative criterion
counterexample: Suppose a researcher tests whether a fertiliser promotes plant growth by assigning plants to a treated and an untreated group without any influence from her expectations. The groups differ only in the application of the fertiliser. The outcome — plant height after six weeks — is measured by a predetermined quantitative criterion. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the greenhouse in which the treated group is housed receives substantially more sunlight than the one housing the untreated group. The difference in growth, if any, cannot be attributed to the fertiliser, for a confounding variable — sunlight — varies systematically between the groups despite the experimenter having intended to hold all other factors constant.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that all causally relevant variables other than the one under investigation be effectively held constant or balanced across the groups — that the experimental design include provisions for identifying and controlling potential confounders.
passage: One might define a controlled experiment as a procedure involving the comparison of at least two groups differing in exactly one specified variable, where the assignment of subjects is not influenced by the experimenter's expectations and the outcome is measured by a predetermined quantitative criterion. This characterisation captures the comparative structure, impartiality, and precision that we associate with rigorous experimentation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed from horticultural research. Suppose a botanist wishes to determine whether a new fertiliser promotes plant growth. She assigns a group of plants to receive the fertiliser and another group to receive none, taking care that her expectations do not influence the assignment. The two groups differ only in the application of the fertiliser. After six weeks, she measures the height of each plant according to a predetermined metric. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, suppose the treated plants are housed in a greenhouse that receives substantially more sunlight than the greenhouse containing the untreated plants. The experimenter may have intended to hold all other variables constant, but a causally relevant factor — sunlight — varies systematically between the two groups. Any observed difference in growth may be attributable to sunlight rather than to the fertiliser. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that the groups differ in the specified variable, that the assignment be unbiased, and that the measurement be quantitative, fails to require that all other causally relevant variables be effectively controlled. Without such a requirement, the comparison is vitiated by confounding factors, and the experiment, though nominally controlled, lacks the capacity to isolate the effect of the variable under investigation.

definition: A statement S counts as a novel prediction of a theory T if and only if (i) S describes a phenomenon not yet observed at the time T was formulated, (ii) S is logically deducible from T, and (iii) S is subsequently confirmed by observation.
conditions: S describes a phenomenon not yet observed at the time T was formulated
S is logically deducible from T
S is subsequently confirmed by observation
counterexample: Suppose a theorist, aware that eclipses occur periodically, constructs a theory from which the occurrence of eclipses is logically deducible. Suppose also that the theory happens to entail the existence of a hitherto unobserved minor perturbation in the moon's orbit. This perturbation has not yet been observed. It is logically deducible from the theory. And suppose it is subsequently confirmed. All three conditions are met. Yet the theory was constructed precisely to accommodate the already-known phenomenon of eclipses, and the perturbation is merely an unintended arithmetical by-product of the same equations. The prediction is novel only in a trivial sense — it was not the aim of the theory and its confirmation provides little evidence that the theory captures anything beyond what was already known.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the novel prediction arise from a theoretical mechanism or principle that goes beyond the phenomena the theory was originally designed to accommodate — that the prediction be a substantive consequence of the theory's explanatory content rather than an incidental arithmetical artefact.
passage: One might define a novel prediction as a statement describing an as-yet-unobserved phenomenon that is logically deducible from a theory and subsequently confirmed. This formulation captures the temporal, logical, and empirical elements that lend predictive success its evidential force. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from astronomy. Suppose a theorist, fully aware of the periodic occurrence of eclipses, devises a mathematical theory tailored to accommodate this known phenomenon. The equations of the theory, being complex, happen also to entail a hitherto unobserved minor perturbation in the lunar orbit — a subtle oscillation that is an unintended arithmetical consequence of the same formulae that were fitted to eclipse data. This perturbation qualifies as a phenomenon not yet observed at the time the theory was formulated. It is logically deducible from the theory. And suppose that subsequent precise observation confirms the perturbation. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this prediction is novel only in the most superficial sense. The theory was constructed to fit the known data on eclipses, and the perturbation is merely an incidental by-product of the curve-fitting — not a consequence of any deep theoretical principle about lunar mechanics. Its confirmation provides little reason to think the theory has explanatory power beyond the phenomena it was designed to accommodate. It appears that the definition, by requiring only temporal novelty, deducibility, and subsequent confirmation, cannot distinguish between predictions that arise from genuine theoretical insight and those that are accidental consequences of mathematical apparatus fitted to previously known data.

definition: A scale S provides a valid measure of a property P in a population if and only if (i) S assigns numerical values to members of the population, (ii) different values of S correspond to genuine differences in P among members, and (iii) S produces consistent results upon repeated application to the same member under the same conditions.
conditions: S assigns numerical values to members of the population
Different values of S correspond to genuine differences in P among members
S produces consistent results upon repeated application to the same member under the same conditions
counterexample: Suppose a psychologist devises a scale to measure mathematical aptitude by administering a timed test of arithmetic problems. The scale assigns numerical scores. Different scores do correspond to genuine differences in arithmetic speed. And the scores are consistent upon repeated testing. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the test is administered in a language that some members of the population do not fully understand, so that their lower scores reflect linguistic difficulty rather than inferior mathematical aptitude. The scale is systematically contaminated by a factor extraneous to the property it purports to measure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S measure P to the exclusion of extraneous factors — that variation in S-values reflect differences in P alone rather than in some confounding property that is correlated with but distinct from P.
passage: One might propose that a scale provides a valid measure of a property when it assigns numerical values, when different values correspond to genuine differences in the property, and when it produces consistent results upon repeated application. This characterisation captures the quantitative, discriminating, and reliable features we associate with valid measurement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed from psychometrics. Suppose a psychologist wishes to measure mathematical aptitude in a multilingual population and devises a timed test of arithmetic problems written in a single language. The test assigns numerical scores to each participant. Participants who solve more problems in the allotted time receive higher scores, and there is good reason to believe that differences in scores do correspond, in many cases, to genuine differences in arithmetic ability. Furthermore, when the same participant takes the test on multiple occasions under the same conditions, the scores are highly consistent. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that for those participants whose command of the test's language is limited, the score reflects not mathematical aptitude but linguistic comprehension. A participant with excellent mathematical ability but poor command of the language may score lower than a participant of modest mathematical ability who is fluent. The scale is contaminated by a factor — linguistic proficiency — that is extraneous to mathematical aptitude but systematically depresses the scores of certain members. It appears that the definition, by requiring only numerical assignment, correspondence to genuine differences, and consistency, fails to demand that the scale measure the target property to the exclusion of confounding factors that may covary with but are distinct from that property.

definition: A procedure E constitutes a crucial experiment between two rival theories T1 and T2 if and only if (i) T1 and T2 make incompatible predictions about the outcome of E, (ii) E is carried out under controlled conditions that eliminate confounding variables, and (iii) the outcome of E is unambiguously consistent with one theory and inconsistent with the other.
conditions: T1 and T2 make incompatible predictions about the outcome of E
E is carried out under controlled conditions that eliminate confounding variables
The outcome of E is unambiguously consistent with one theory and inconsistent with the other
counterexample: Suppose two rival theories of heat conduction make incompatible predictions about the rate at which a metal rod will equilibrate when heated at one end. The experiment is performed under carefully controlled conditions, and the measured rate is unambiguously consistent with the first theory and inconsistent with the second. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose both theories rest upon a shared auxiliary assumption about the purity of the metal, and the rod used in the experiment is in fact an alloy. The outcome refutes not the second theory alone but the conjunction of that theory with the auxiliary assumption, and one cannot determine, from the experiment alone, which element of the conjunction is at fault.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the auxiliary assumptions upon which T1 and T2 depend be independently established, so that the outcome of E can be unambiguously attributed to the theories themselves rather than to the possible failure of shared or unshared background assumptions.
passage: One might propose that a crucial experiment is a procedure in which two rival theories yield incompatible predictions, the procedure is conducted under controlled conditions, and the outcome unambiguously favours one theory over the other. This characterisation captures the decisive and discriminating character that we associate with the most celebrated experiments in the history of science. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of heat conduction. Suppose two rival theories of thermal transfer make incompatible predictions about the rate at which a metal rod will reach thermal equilibrium when heated at one end. The experiment is performed with meticulous attention to the elimination of confounding variables, and the measured rate is unambiguously consistent with the first theory and inconsistent with the second. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that both theories depend upon a shared auxiliary assumption concerning the purity of the metal rod employed. If the rod is in fact an alloy whose thermal properties differ from those of the pure metal, the experimental outcome refutes not the second theory in isolation but the conjunction of that theory with the auxiliary assumption about purity. It is by no means certain which element of the conjunction is responsible for the failure. The definition, by requiring only incompatible predictions, controlled conditions, and an unambiguous outcome, neglects the entanglement of theories with auxiliary hypotheses that is characteristic of all empirical testing. Without the requirement that auxiliary assumptions be independently validated, the notion of a crucial experiment remains philosophically incomplete.

definition: A theory T1 is reducible to a theory T2 if and only if (i) every term of T1 can be defined using the vocabulary of T2, (ii) every law of T1 can be logically derived from the laws of T2 together with these definitions, and (iii) T2 is more fundamental than T1 in the sense that T2 describes the constituent parts of the systems described by T1.
conditions: Every term of T1 can be defined using the vocabulary of T2
Every law of T1 can be logically derived from the laws of T2 together with these definitions
T2 is more fundamental than T1 in the sense that T2 describes the constituent parts of the systems described by T1
counterexample: Suppose one seeks to reduce classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. One may define temperature as the mean kinetic energy of molecules and derive the ideal gas law from the statistical behaviour of molecular ensembles. Every term of thermodynamics can be given a statistical-mechanical definition, and every thermodynamic law can be derived from statistical-mechanical principles. Statistical mechanics describes the molecular constituents of thermodynamic systems. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the reduction introduces a fundamental conceptual shift: the second law of thermodynamics, which in the original theory is an exceptionless law, becomes in the reducing theory a merely statistical regularity that admits of exceedingly improbable violations. The reduced and reducing theories do not say quite the same thing, and the reduction alters the modal status of the laws being reduced.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the reduction preserve the modal or nomological status of the laws of T1, so that a law which is exceptionless in T1 is not downgraded to a merely statistical regularity in T2.
passage: One might define intertheoretic reduction as obtaining when every term of the higher-level theory can be defined in the vocabulary of the lower-level theory, every law of the former can be logically derived from the latter together with the bridging definitions, and the lower-level theory describes the constituent parts of the systems treated by the higher-level theory. This characterisation captures the deductive, definitional, and mereological aspects that we naturally associate with reduction. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the relation between classical thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. One may define temperature in statistical-mechanical terms as the mean kinetic energy of molecules and may derive the ideal gas law from the statistical behaviour of molecular ensembles. Every thermodynamic term receives a definition in the vocabulary of statistical mechanics, and every thermodynamic law can be derived from statistical-mechanical principles. Statistical mechanics describes the molecular constituents of which thermodynamic systems are composed. All three conditions are manifestly satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the reduction transforms the character of the laws being reduced. The second law of thermodynamics is, in the original theory, an exceptionless generalisation: entropy never decreases in an isolated system. In statistical mechanics, however, this law becomes a merely statistical regularity, holding with overwhelming probability but admitting of exceedingly improbable violations. The reduced and reducing theories do not say quite the same thing about the necessity of the second law. It appears that the definition, by requiring only definitional translation, logical derivation, and mereological fundamentality, fails to demand that the reduction preserve the nomological or modal status of the laws it purports to reduce.

definition: A procedure M constitutes a valid measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M yields a numerical value that is reproducible under relevantly similar conditions, (ii) M is calibrated against an accepted standard for Q, and (iii) the numerical value produced by M varies systematically with changes in Q.
conditions: M yields a numerical value that is reproducible under relevantly similar conditions
M is calibrated against an accepted standard for Q
The numerical value produced by M varies systematically with changes in Q
counterexample: Suppose a physicist attempts to measure the temperature of a liquid by immersing a mercury thermometer that has been calibrated against an accepted temperature standard. The thermometer yields reproducible readings, and the readings vary systematically with changes in the actual temperature. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the liquid is undergoing a rapid exothermic chemical reaction, so that the act of immersing the thermometer alters the local temperature in the vicinity of the mercury bulb. The reading does not reflect the temperature the liquid would have had in the absence of the measurement. The measurement procedure itself disturbs the quantity it purports to measure, yielding a value that is reproducible and systematically responsive to temperature but that does not correspond to the undisturbed state of the system.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the measurement procedure M not significantly disturb or alter the quantity Q in the process of measuring it, so that the numerical value obtained corresponds to the state of the system as it exists independently of the act of measurement.
passage: One might propose that a valid measurement of a quantity consists in a procedure that yields reproducible numerical values, is calibrated against an accepted standard, and produces values that vary systematically with changes in the quantity being measured. This characterisation captures the precision, standardisation, and responsiveness that we typically demand of measurement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the measurement of temperature in chemically reactive systems. Suppose a physicist seeks to measure the temperature of a liquid by immersing a mercury thermometer that has been calibrated against an accepted temperature standard. The thermometer yields reproducible readings when immersed repeatedly, and the readings vary systematically with changes in the actual temperature of the liquid. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this as a valid measurement if the liquid is undergoing a rapid exothermic chemical reaction, for the act of immersion alters the local temperature in the vicinity of the mercury bulb. The thermometer reads a temperature that is systematically different from the one the liquid would have exhibited had no thermometer been introduced. The reading is reproducible, calibrated, and systematically responsive, yet it does not correspond to the undisturbed state of the system. It appears that the definition, by requiring only reproducibility, calibration, and systematic covariance, fails to demand that the measurement procedure not significantly disturb the very quantity it purports to measure.

definition: A statement P constitutes a genuine scientific prediction if and only if (i) P is derived from a theory T by deductive or mathematical reasoning, (ii) P describes a state of affairs that has not yet been observed at the time of derivation, and (iii) P is subsequently confirmed by observation.
conditions: P is derived from a theory T by deductive or mathematical reasoning
P describes a state of affairs that has not yet been observed at the time of derivation
P is subsequently confirmed by observation
counterexample: Suppose a theory of planetary motion, formulated on the basis of observations of five known planets, is extended by mathematical reasoning to predict that a sixth planet exists at a particular location. An astronomer searches the predicted location and finds a luminous object there. All three conditions are satisfied: the prediction was mathematically derived from the theory, it described a previously unobserved state of affairs, and it was confirmed by observation. Yet suppose the luminous object is not a planet but a distant comet that happens to occupy the predicted position at the time of observation. The observation confirms the prediction only superficially; the object found is not the kind of object the theory predicted. The theory is not genuinely supported by this confirmation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the observed state of affairs match the prediction in all theoretically relevant respects, not merely in superficial or observationally salient features, so that the confirmation bears upon the truth of the theory rather than upon a coincidental resemblance.
passage: One might define a genuine scientific prediction as a statement derived from a theory by deductive reasoning, describing a state of affairs not yet observed at the time of derivation, and subsequently confirmed by observation. This characterisation captures the anticipatory and empirically testable character that we prize in theoretical science. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of planetary astronomy. Suppose a theory of planetary motion, formulated upon the basis of observations of five known planets, is extended by mathematical reasoning to predict the existence of a sixth planet at a particular celestial location. An astronomer directs his telescope to the predicted position and finds a luminous object there. The prediction was derived from the theory; it described an unobserved state of affairs; and it was confirmed by observation. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the luminous object is not in fact a planet but a distant comet that happens, by coincidence, to occupy the predicted position at the time of observation. The observation confirms the prediction only in the superficial sense that something was found where something was predicted; the object found is not the kind of entity the theory required. It appears that the definition, by demanding only derivation, novelty, and observational confirmation, fails to require that the observed state of affairs correspond to the prediction in all theoretically relevant respects. Without such a requirement, a prediction may be confirmed by fortuitous coincidence rather than by the truth of the theory from which it was derived.

definition: A representation M constitutes an adequate scientific model of a system S if and only if (i) M reproduces the known quantitative behaviour of S within acceptable margins of error, (ii) M employs only assumptions that are physically plausible, and (iii) M is simpler than any available alternative that reproduces the same behaviour.
conditions: M reproduces the known quantitative behaviour of S within acceptable margins of error
M employs only assumptions that are physically plausible
M is simpler than any available alternative that reproduces the same behaviour
counterexample: Suppose a model of fluid flow in a pipe reproduces all known quantitative measurements within acceptable error margins. Its assumptions are physically plausible, invoking only standard fluid-mechanical principles. And it is simpler than any available alternative model that achieves the same accuracy. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the model assumes laminar flow throughout, whereas the actual system transitions to turbulent flow under conditions slightly beyond those that have been measured. The model is incapable of representing this transition, and if the system is subjected to even modestly different conditions, its predictions will fail catastrophically. The model is adequate only within the narrow range of existing observations and cannot be relied upon beyond them.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that M correctly represent the qualitative structural features of S — such as phase transitions, bifurcations, or regime changes — that determine the system's behaviour under conditions not yet observed, rather than merely fitting the quantitative data already in hand.
passage: One might propose that an adequate scientific model is a representation that reproduces the known quantitative behaviour of a system within acceptable error, employs only physically plausible assumptions, and is simpler than any available alternative achieving the same accuracy. This characterisation captures the empirical fidelity, physical grounding, and parsimony that we expect of good scientific modelling. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from fluid dynamics. Suppose an engineer constructs a model of fluid flow through a pipe that reproduces every available measurement of flow rate, pressure drop, and velocity profile within the accepted margins of error. The model's assumptions are physically plausible, invoking standard principles of viscous flow. And the model is simpler than any alternative representation that achieves the same quantitative agreement. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this model as adequate if it assumes laminar flow throughout the pipe, when in fact the system undergoes a transition to turbulent flow under conditions only slightly beyond those that have been measured. The model is structurally incapable of representing this transition. Under conditions that differ even modestly from those hitherto observed, its predictions will fail catastrophically. It appears that the definition, by requiring only quantitative reproduction, physical plausibility, and simplicity, fails to demand that the model correctly represent the qualitative structural features of the system — phase transitions, bifurcations, and regime changes — upon which the system's behaviour under novel conditions depends.

definition: A theory T is falsifiable if and only if (i) T entails at least one observation statement that could, in principle, be shown to be false, (ii) the observation statement is sufficiently precise that its falsity could be recognised by competent observers, and (iii) no ad hoc modifications have been introduced into T to shield it from refutation.
conditions: T entails at least one observation statement that could in principle be shown to be false
The observation statement is sufficiently precise that its falsity could be recognised by competent observers
No ad hoc modifications have been introduced into T to shield it from refutation
counterexample: Suppose a geological theory predicts that a specific stratum of rock will be found at a depth of precisely 200 metres beneath a designated site. The prediction is precise and could in principle be shown to be false by drilling. No ad hoc modifications have been introduced into the theory. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the stratum in question lies beneath an ocean trench at a depth that is inaccessible to any existing or foreseeable drilling technology. The observation statement is falsifiable in principle but not in any practically realisable sense. The theory enjoys a kind of immunity from refutation that arises not from any logical deficiency but from the sheer inaccessibility of the relevant evidence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the observation statement entailed by T be testable not merely in logical principle but in a manner that is practically accessible to the scientific community, so that the theory's falsifiability is a genuine rather than merely formal property.
passage: One might characterise a falsifiable theory as one that entails at least one observation statement capable in principle of being shown false, where the statement is sufficiently precise for its falsity to be recognised, and where no ad hoc modifications have been introduced to shield the theory from refutation. This formulation captures the logical openness to empirical challenge that we associate with genuinely scientific theorising. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from geology. Suppose a theory of crustal formation predicts that a specific stratum of rock will be found at a depth of precisely 200 metres beneath a designated site on the ocean floor. The prediction is precise enough that its falsity would be unmistakable: drilling to 200 metres and finding no such stratum would suffice. No ad hoc modifications have been made to the theory. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the designated site lies beneath an ocean trench at a depth inaccessible to any existing or foreseeable drilling technology. The observation statement is falsifiable in the purely logical sense that one can describe what would count as a refutation, yet no such refutation can in practice be carried out. The theory enjoys an immunity from empirical challenge that derives not from any logical manoeuvre but from the contingent inaccessibility of the relevant evidence. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that there exist in principle a falsifying observation, neglects the distinction between formal and practical falsifiability, and thereby fails to ensure that the theory is genuinely open to empirical test.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is internally consistent, (ii) T provides explanations for a range of phenomena by subsuming them under general principles, and (iii) T is expressed in a form that permits the derivation of testable predictions.
conditions: T is internally consistent
T provides explanations for a range of phenomena by subsuming them under general principles
T is expressed in a form that permits the derivation of testable predictions
counterexample: Suppose a numerologist constructs an elaborate system of propositions asserting that the behaviour of physical systems is governed by the mystical properties of certain integers. The system is internally consistent: no proposition contradicts any other. It explains a range of phenomena — the periodicity of chemical elements, the frequencies of spectral lines, the ratios of planetary orbits — by subsuming them under the general principle that nature is governed by numerological harmonies. And it permits the derivation of testable predictions, since the assignment of numerical values to physical quantities yields specific numerical forecasts. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this a scientific theory, for its explanatory principles lack any connection to causal mechanisms or physical processes and amount to nothing more than a descriptive scheme imposed upon the data.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the general principles of T refer to causal mechanisms, physical processes, or structural relations that could in principle be independently investigated, rather than being merely formal or descriptive schemes imposed upon the phenomena from without.
passage: One might propose that a scientific theory is a body of internally consistent propositions that explains a range of phenomena by subsuming them under general principles and that is expressed in a form permitting the derivation of testable predictions. This characterisation captures the logical coherence, explanatory breadth, and empirical accountability that we expect of genuine scientific theories. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering a form of numerological speculation. Suppose a thinker constructs an elaborate system of propositions asserting that the behaviour of physical systems is governed by the mystical properties of certain integers. The system is internally consistent, for no proposition within it contradicts any other. It explains a range of phenomena — the periodicity of chemical elements, the frequencies of spectral lines, the ratios of planetary orbits — by subsuming them under the general principle that nature is governed by numerological harmonies. And the system permits the derivation of testable predictions, for the assignment of particular numerical values to physical quantities yields specific forecasts about measurable outcomes. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this as a scientific theory. Its explanatory principles lack any reference to causal mechanisms, physical processes, or structural relations that could be independently investigated. The numerological harmonies are a descriptive scheme imposed upon the phenomena rather than an account of the processes that produce them. It appears that the definition, by requiring only consistency, explanatory subsumption, and predictive form, fails to demand that the general principles of the theory engage with the causal or structural character of the domain to which it is applied.

definition: A taxonomic category C is natural if and only if (i) the members of C share a cluster of co-occurring properties, (ii) these shared properties are not merely stipulated but are discovered through empirical investigation, and (iii) membership in C supports inductive inferences about further properties of its members.
conditions: The members of C share a cluster of co-occurring properties
These shared properties are not merely stipulated but are discovered through empirical investigation
Membership in C supports inductive inferences about further properties of its members
counterexample: Consider the category of jade, which includes two mineralogically distinct substances, nephrite and jadeite. Objects classified as jade share a cluster of co-occurring properties: a characteristic colour, hardness, lustre, and cultural significance. These properties were discovered through empirical investigation rather than mere stipulation. And membership in the category supports inductive inferences — one can predict with some confidence that a specimen classified as jade will have a particular range of colours and a certain resistance to breakage. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet nephrite and jadeite have entirely different chemical compositions and crystal structures. The category groups together substances that have fundamentally different underlying natures, united only by superficial resemblance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the co-occurring properties shared by members of C be grounded in a common underlying structure, composition, or causal mechanism, so that the classification tracks a single natural kind rather than an accidental convergence of superficial similarities across distinct kinds.
passage: One might define a natural taxonomic category as one whose members share a cluster of empirically discovered co-occurring properties and whose membership supports inductive inferences about further properties. This characterisation captures the empirical, non-arbitrary, and inferentially productive character that we associate with genuine natural categories. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from mineralogy. Consider the category of jade. Specimens classified as jade share a cluster of co-occurring properties: a characteristic green colour, a certain hardness and resistance to breakage, a distinctive lustre, and a long history of cultural and artistic use. These properties were discovered through empirical investigation. And membership in the category supports inductive inferences: upon learning that a specimen is jade, one can predict with some confidence that it will exhibit the familiar colour, hardness, and lustre. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the category of jade encompasses two mineralogically distinct substances — nephrite and jadeite — with entirely different chemical compositions and crystal structures. Nephrite is a calcium-magnesium silicate; jadeite is a sodium-aluminium silicate. The similarities that unite them are superficial convergences rather than manifestations of a common underlying nature. The category, though it satisfies every condition of the proposed definition, fails to track a single natural kind. It appears that the definition, by requiring only shared co-occurring properties, empirical discovery, and inductive support, neglects the requirement that the shared properties be grounded in a common structural or causal basis.

definition: A regularity R constitutes a law of nature if and only if (i) R holds universally across all instances of the relevant kind, (ii) R is expressible as a mathematical equation relating measurable quantities, and (iii) R has been derived from a well-established scientific theory.
conditions: R holds universally across all instances of the relevant kind
R is expressible as a mathematical equation relating measurable quantities
R has been derived from a well-established scientific theory
counterexample: Suppose a regularity is derived from Newtonian mechanics stating that the period of a pendulum of a given length is precisely determined by the local gravitational field strength. This regularity holds universally for all ideal pendulums, is expressible as a precise mathematical equation, and is derived from the well-established framework of classical mechanics. All three conditions are met. Yet the regularity holds only for ideal pendulums — point masses on massless, inextensible strings in the absence of air resistance. No actual pendulum satisfies these idealisations, and the law, strictly speaking, is false of every real pendulum. Its universality extends only across an idealised domain that is nowhere instantiated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that R describe regularities that actually obtain in the physical world, rather than regularities that hold only within an idealised domain whose conditions are never realised, thereby conflating the laws of idealised models with the laws of nature.
passage: One might characterise a law of nature as a regularity that holds universally, is expressible as a mathematical equation, and has been derived from a well-established theory. This formulation captures the universality, mathematical precision, and theoretical grounding that we associate with the fundamental laws of natural science. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from classical mechanics. Consider the regularity, derivable from Newtonian mechanics, that the period of a pendulum of a given length is precisely determined by the local gravitational field strength according to a well-known mathematical equation. This regularity holds universally for all instances of the relevant kind — ideal pendulums consisting of point masses on massless, inextensible strings in the absence of friction and air resistance. It is expressible as a precise mathematical formula. And it is derived from the thoroughly established framework of classical mechanics. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to accept this as a law of nature without qualification. The regularity holds only within an idealised domain whose conditions are nowhere realised. No actual pendulum is a point mass; no string is truly massless or inextensible; no environment is entirely free of friction. The law, strictly speaking, is false of every real pendulum. It appears that the definition, by requiring only universality, mathematical expressibility, and theoretical derivation, fails to distinguish between laws that describe the actual physical world and laws that hold only within the confines of an idealised model.

definition: An observation report R is objective if and only if (i) R describes only what is directly perceived by the senses without inference or interpretation, (ii) R uses terms whose application is determined solely by observable features, and (iii) different observers in the same circumstances would produce the same report R.
conditions: R describes only what is directly perceived by the senses without inference or interpretation
R uses terms whose application is determined solely by observable features
Different observers in the same circumstances would produce the same report R
counterexample: Suppose two competent observers examine a Wilson cloud chamber and both report seeing a white curved track against a dark background. The report describes only what is directly perceived — a white curved line. The terms used refer only to observable features: colour, shape, and contrast. Different observers in the same circumstances produce the same report. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the report fails to constitute an objective scientific observation, for it omits the very content that makes the observation scientifically significant: that the track indicates the passage of a charged particle deflected by a magnetic field. The restriction to purely sensory description strips the observation of its theoretical import and renders it scientifically useless.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that scientific observation necessarily involves theoretically informed categorisation, and that a report which excludes all interpretive and inferential content is not thereby more objective but merely less informative, lacking the theoretical framework required to make the observation scientifically meaningful.
passage: One might define an objective observation report as one that describes only what is directly perceived, uses terms determined solely by observable features, and would be produced identically by any observer in the same circumstances. This characterisation captures the aspiration to eliminate subjective bias and theoretical contamination from the recording of empirical data. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from experimental physics. Suppose two competent observers examine a Wilson cloud chamber in which a charged particle has been deflected by a magnetic field. Both observers, attending strictly to what is directly perceived, report seeing a white curved track against a dark background. The report describes only what the senses deliver: a white curve of a certain shape and thickness. The terms employed — colour, shape, contrast — are determined solely by observable features. And different observers in the same circumstances produce the same report. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that this report, far from being an exemplary scientific observation, is scientifically useless. It omits the very content that makes the cloud chamber observation significant: that the track indicates the passage of a charged particle whose charge and momentum can be inferred from the curvature. The restriction to purely sensory description, intended to guarantee objectivity, strips the observation of all theoretical import. It appears that the definition, by excluding inference and interpretation, fails to recognise that scientific observation necessarily involves theoretically informed categorisation, and that what we call an objective observation in science is not a theory-free sensory report but a report whose theoretical commitments are well-grounded and widely shared.

definition: A statement S constitutes a scientific explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) S describes a general law or regularity under which P can be subsumed, (ii) S specifies the antecedent conditions that, together with the law, entail the occurrence of P, and (iii) the conjunction of the law and the antecedent conditions logically entails P.
conditions: S describes a general law or regularity under which P can be subsumed
S specifies the antecedent conditions that together with the law entail the occurrence of P
The conjunction of the law and the antecedent conditions logically entails P
counterexample: Suppose one seeks to explain why a particular flagpole casts a shadow of a certain length. One may cite the law of rectilinear propagation of light together with the antecedent conditions of the flagpole's height and the sun's angle of elevation. The conjunction of these logically entails the shadow's length, and all three conditions are satisfied. Yet consider the reverse: one may equally well cite the same law together with the shadow's length and the sun's angle to deduce the flagpole's height. This derivation satisfies all three conditions, yet no one would say that the shadow's length explains the flagpole's height. The derivation runs in the wrong direction, from effect to cause, and the definition cannot distinguish genuine explanations from such merely deductive but non-explanatory derivations.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the explanatory relation exhibit an asymmetry corresponding to causal or productive direction, so that mere logical entailment from laws and antecedent conditions, without regard to the direction of dependence, permits non-explanatory derivations to qualify as explanations.
passage: One might propose that a scientific explanation consists in a statement that describes a general law under which the phenomenon can be subsumed, specifies the antecedent conditions that together with the law entail the phenomenon, and that the conjunction of law and conditions logically entails the phenomenon. This characterisation captures the deductive and nomological character that we associate with the most rigorous scientific explanations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the geometry of shadows. Suppose one seeks to explain why a certain flagpole casts a shadow of a certain length. One cites the law of rectilinear propagation of light together with the height of the flagpole and the angle of the sun's elevation. The conjunction of these logically entails the length of the shadow, and all three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the very same law, together with the shadow's length and the sun's angle, logically entails the height of the flagpole. This reverse derivation satisfies every condition of the definition with equal force. Yet we should hesitate to say that the shadow's length explains the flagpole's height, for the derivation runs from effect to cause rather than from cause to effect. It appears that the definition, by requiring only subsumption under a law, specification of antecedent conditions, and logical entailment, fails to demand the asymmetry of explanatory direction that distinguishes genuine explanation from mere deduction. Without such a requirement, the analysis admits cases in which an effect is said to explain its own cause, which is contrary to the purpose of scientific explanation.

definition: A generalisation G constitutes a scientific law if and only if (i) G is a true universal statement of the form 'All Fs are Gs,' (ii) G supports counterfactual conditionals, and (iii) G has been confirmed by a substantial body of observational evidence.
conditions: G is a true universal statement of the form all Fs are Gs
G supports counterfactual conditionals
G has been confirmed by a substantial body of observational evidence
counterexample: Suppose it is true that all the coins in the pocket of a certain eminent physicist are copper. This generalisation is a true universal statement. It supports the counterfactual conditional: if a silver coin had been placed in his pocket, it would have been copper, in the trivial sense that no silver coin was in fact so placed. And suppose a diligent investigator has confirmed on many occasions that every coin drawn from the pocket is indeed copper. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet no one would regard this as a scientific law, for it is merely an accidental truth about a particular collection of objects and has no nomological force whatsoever.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that G hold by virtue of some underlying structural, causal, or nomological necessity, rather than merely by accidental coincidence, so that accidentally true universal generalisations can satisfy all three conditions without constituting laws.
passage: One might define a scientific law as a true universal statement that supports counterfactual conditionals and has been confirmed by substantial observational evidence. This characterisation captures the universality, modal robustness, and empirical support that we naturally associate with the great laws of nature. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from a mundane example. Suppose it is true that all the coins in the pocket of a certain eminent physicist are copper. This generalisation is a true universal statement of the required logical form. It appears to support the relevant counterfactual, inasmuch as no non-copper coin is in the pocket to serve as a falsifying instance. And suppose a diligent investigator has confirmed on numerous occasions, by emptying the pocket and inspecting its contents, that every coin therein is copper. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this as a scientific law. The generalisation is merely an accidental truth about a particular collection of objects. It has no nomological force; it does not reflect any underlying physical or structural necessity. It appears that the definition, by demanding only truth, counterfactual support, and observational confirmation, fails to require that the generalisation hold by virtue of some deeper structural or causal necessity. Without such a requirement, the analysis admits accidental truths that no scientist would recognise as laws of nature.

definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is internally consistent, (ii) T entails a set of observational consequences that have been confirmed by experiment, and (iii) T provides a unified account of phenomena that were previously explained by separate and unrelated hypotheses.
conditions: T is internally consistent
T entails a set of observational consequences that have been confirmed by experiment
T provides a unified account of phenomena previously explained by separate hypotheses
counterexample: Suppose a theorist constructs a body of propositions that is internally consistent, entails a wide range of confirmed observational consequences, and unifies phenomena that were previously accounted for by separate hypotheses. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the theory achieves its unification by postulating a different ad hoc mechanism for each unified phenomenon, connected only by a vague overarching principle that any observed regularity is a manifestation of a single cosmic force. The unification is nominal rather than genuine: the theory provides no single explanatory mechanism from which the diverse phenomena follow, but merely relabels each separate explanation under a common heading.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the unification be achieved through a common explanatory mechanism or set of principles from which the diverse phenomena can be independently derived, rather than through a merely nominal or verbal unification that relabels separate explanations without genuinely connecting them.
passage: One might propose that a scientific theory is a body of propositions that is internally consistent, entails observational consequences confirmed by experiment, and provides a unified account of phenomena previously explained by separate hypotheses. This characterisation captures the logical coherence, empirical adequacy, and explanatory scope that we associate with the most successful theories. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be imagined as follows. Suppose a theorist constructs a system of propositions that is internally consistent and entails a wide range of confirmed observational consequences. The system unifies phenomena that were previously accounted for by independent hypotheses: the behaviour of falling bodies, the motion of the planets, and the tides of the ocean are all brought under a single theoretical umbrella. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the theory achieves this unification by postulating a separate ad hoc mechanism for each phenomenon, connected only by the vague declaration that every regularity is a manifestation of a single cosmic force. The unification is nominal rather than genuine. The theory does not provide a common explanatory mechanism from which each phenomenon can be independently derived; it merely relabels the existing separate explanations under a shared heading. It appears that the definition, by requiring only consistency, empirical confirmation, and unification, fails to demand that the unification be achieved through a genuine common mechanism rather than through verbal redescription.

definition: A hypothesis H is scientifically meaningful if and only if (i) H makes at least one prediction that could in principle be shown false by observation, (ii) the class of potential falsifiers of H is clearly specified, and (iii) no ad hoc modification has been introduced to shield H from refutation.
conditions: H makes at least one prediction that could in principle be shown false by observation
The class of potential falsifiers of H is clearly specified
No ad hoc modification has been introduced to shield H from refutation
counterexample: Suppose a chemist proposes that a certain compound dissolves in water at temperatures above fifty degrees. The hypothesis makes a clear prediction that could be falsified by observation: one need only heat water above fifty degrees, add the compound, and observe whether it dissolves. The class of potential falsifiers is clearly specified: any instance in which the compound fails to dissolve above fifty degrees. And no ad hoc modification has been introduced. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the compound is chemically unstable and decomposes upon contact with water at any temperature, so that the test cannot in practice be carried out. The hypothesis is in principle falsifiable, but the physical nature of the compound renders every attempt at testing self-defeating. The hypothesis occupies a peculiar status: it satisfies the formal requirements of meaningfulness yet cannot be subjected to the empirical scrutiny that those requirements are meant to ensure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the potential falsifiers be practically realisable, so that a hypothesis whose predictions are in principle observable but in practice impossible to test owing to the nature of the entities involved may satisfy all conditions without being genuinely open to empirical adjudication.
passage: One might propose that a hypothesis is scientifically meaningful if it makes at least one prediction that could in principle be shown false by observation, if the class of potential falsifiers is clearly specified, and if no ad hoc modification has been introduced to protect it from refutation. This characterisation captures the empirical vulnerability and intellectual honesty that we demand of scientific claims. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from chemistry. Suppose a chemist hypothesises that a certain newly synthesised compound dissolves in water at temperatures above fifty degrees. The hypothesis makes a clear prediction amenable to observational test. The class of potential falsifiers is well specified: any instance of the compound failing to dissolve above the stated temperature. And no ad hoc modification shields the hypothesis from refutation. All three conditions are met. Nevertheless, suppose the compound is chemically unstable in such a manner that it decomposes immediately upon contact with water at any temperature, rendering the dissolution test physically impossible to carry out. The hypothesis remains in principle falsifiable, yet no experiment can in practice test it, for the very entity whose behaviour is predicted cannot survive the conditions required for testing. It appears that the definition, by requiring only in-principle falsifiability, a clearly specified class of falsifiers, and the absence of ad hoc modifications, fails to demand that the testing conditions be practically realisable. Without such a requirement, a hypothesis may satisfy every formal condition of scientific meaningfulness while remaining permanently beyond the reach of empirical scrutiny.

definition: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) the categories of C are mutually exclusive, so that no object belongs to more than one category, (ii) the categories of C are jointly exhaustive, so that every object in the domain is assigned to some category, and (iii) the classification is based upon properties that are intrinsic to the objects classified.
conditions: The categories of C are mutually exclusive
The categories of C are jointly exhaustive
The classification is based upon properties intrinsic to the objects classified
counterexample: Suppose a mineralogist devises a classification of crystals in which the categories are mutually exclusive, jointly exhaustive, and based entirely upon intrinsic properties such as crystal structure and chemical composition. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the classification groups together substances that share the same crystal structure but have entirely different chemical compositions and physical behaviours, while separating substances that have different crystal structures but nearly identical chemical and physical properties. The classification carves nature at joints that are scientifically uninteresting and fails to group together the substances whose similarities are most explanatorily important.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the properties upon which the classification is based be those that are scientifically significant or explanatorily relevant, so that a classification founded upon intrinsic but scientifically trivial properties may satisfy all conditions while failing to group objects in ways that reflect natural kinds or support scientific inference.
passage: One might define a scientifically adequate classification as one whose categories are mutually exclusive, jointly exhaustive, and based upon properties intrinsic to the objects classified. This characterisation captures the logical rigour and objectivity that we expect of any sound taxonomic scheme. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from mineralogy. Suppose a mineralogist constructs a classification of crystalline substances in which every crystal is assigned to exactly one category, no crystal is left unclassified, and the basis of classification is entirely intrinsic: the crystal structure and chemical composition of each specimen. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the classification groups together substances sharing the same crystal structure despite their having entirely different chemical compositions and physical behaviours. At the same time, it separates substances with different crystal structures that possess nearly identical chemical and physical properties and that respond similarly in experimental contexts. The classification carves the domain at joints that are scientifically uninteresting.
