It appears that the definition, by requiring only mutual exclusivity, joint exhaustiveness, and intrinsic basis, fails to demand that the classifying properties be scientifically significant or explanatorily relevant. Without such a requirement, a classification may satisfy every formal condition while grouping objects in ways that neither reflect natural kinds nor support productive scientific inference.

definition: A conceptual framework F constitutes a scientific paradigm if and only if (i) F provides the fundamental concepts and categories within which a community of practitioners formulates its problems, (ii) F is accepted by a majority of practitioners in the relevant field, and (iii) F has generated a record of successful problem-solving that serves as a model for future research.
conditions: F provides the fundamental concepts and categories within which a community formulates its problems
F is accepted by a majority of practitioners in the relevant field
F has generated a record of successful problem-solving that serves as a model for future research
counterexample: Suppose a community of natural philosophers in the seventeenth century adopts a conceptual framework according to which all physical phenomena are to be explained by the direct contact and collision of particles. This framework provides the fundamental concepts within which the community formulates its problems; it is accepted by the majority of practitioners; and it has a record of successful problem-solving in mechanics and optics. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the framework is incapable of accommodating gravitational attraction, which acts at a distance without any intervening contact. When Newton proposes his theory of gravitation, the framework lacks the conceptual resources even to formulate the problem of action at a distance, let alone to solve it. The paradigm is not merely incomplete; it actively excludes the phenomena that drive the next major advance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that F possess the capacity to evolve or accommodate anomalous phenomena that arise from within or beyond its domain, so that a framework which rigidly excludes entire classes of significant phenomena may satisfy all conditions while impeding rather than enabling scientific progress.
passage: One might propose that a scientific paradigm is a conceptual framework that provides fundamental concepts for problem-formulation, is accepted by a majority of practitioners, and has generated a record of successful problem-solving. This characterisation captures the communal, conceptual, and historical dimensions that we associate with paradigmatic science. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from seventeenth-century natural philosophy. Suppose a community of natural philosophers adopts the framework of contact mechanics, according to which all physical phenomena are to be explained by the direct collision and contact of particles. This framework provides the fundamental concepts in which the community formulates its problems. It is accepted by the majority of practitioners. And it has a record of successful problem-solving in mechanics and in certain domains of optics. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the framework is constitutionally incapable of accommodating gravitational attraction, which acts at a distance without any intermediary contact. When Newton proposes his theory of universal gravitation, the contact-mechanical framework lacks the conceptual resources even to formulate the problem. The paradigm actively excludes the phenomena that drive the next major advance in physics. It appears that the definition, by requiring only conceptual provision, majority acceptance, and a successful track record, fails to demand that the framework possess the capacity to accommodate or at least engage with anomalous phenomena. Without such a requirement, a paradigm may satisfy every stated condition while rigidly impeding the scientific progress it is meant to enable.

definition: A report R constitutes a reliable scientific observation if and only if (i) R describes what was directly perceived by the observer under the relevant conditions, (ii) R is expressed in terms that are intersubjectively accessible so that other observers could in principle verify it, and (iii) the observer was in a state of normal perceptual functioning at the time of observation.
conditions: R describes what was directly perceived by the observer under relevant conditions
R is expressed in intersubjectively accessible terms
The observer was in a state of normal perceptual functioning
counterexample: Suppose an astronomer observes a nebula through a telescope and reports that it appears as a faint smudge of light in a particular region of the sky. The report describes what was directly perceived, is expressed in intersubjectively accessible terms, and the astronomer's perceptual faculties were functioning normally. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the astronomer's theoretical commitments lead her to see the smudge as an unresolved star cluster rather than as a cloud of gas. Another astronomer, with different theoretical commitments, observes the same object through the same instrument and sees a gaseous cloud. Both reports satisfy all three conditions, yet they disagree about the nature of what was observed. The theoretical commitments of each observer have shaped what each perceives, and the definition cannot distinguish between observation reports that are and are not contaminated by theoretical presupposition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the observation be independent of the observer's theoretical commitments, so that two observers with different theoretical backgrounds may both satisfy all conditions yet deliver incompatible reports owing to the theory-ladenness of perception.
passage: One might define a reliable scientific observation as a report describing what was directly perceived by the observer, expressed in intersubjectively accessible terms, made by an observer in a state of normal perceptual functioning. This characterisation captures the directness, communicability, and physiological adequacy that we demand of observational reports. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from astronomical practice. Suppose an astronomer observes a nebula through a telescope and reports that she perceives a faint smudge of light in a particular region of the sky. The report describes what was directly perceived; it is couched in terms accessible to other observers; and the astronomer's perceptual faculties were functioning normally. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the astronomer's theoretical commitments lead her to perceive the smudge as an unresolved star cluster rather than as a cloud of gas. A second astronomer, holding different theoretical views, observes the same object through the same instrument and perceives a gaseous cloud. Both reports satisfy every condition of the definition, yet they disagree about the nature of the observed phenomenon. It appears that the definition, by requiring only directness, intersubjective accessibility, and normal functioning, fails to demand that the observation be independent of the observer's theoretical presuppositions. Without such a requirement, the analysis admits cases in which equally qualified observers deliver incompatible reports, each satisfying every stated condition, because their differing theoretical commitments shape what they perceive.

definition: A procedure E qualifies as a controlled experiment if and only if (i) E includes both an experimental group subjected to the variable under investigation and a control group not subjected to it, (ii) assignment of subjects to the experimental and control groups is random, and (iii) the experimenter is unaware of which subjects belong to which group during the collection and analysis of data.
conditions: E includes an experimental group and a control group
Assignment of subjects to groups is random
The experimenter is unaware of group assignments during data collection and analysis
counterexample: Suppose a botanist seeks to test whether a certain soil additive promotes the growth of wheat. She divides her plots randomly into experimental and control groups, applies the additive to the experimental plots, and arranges for a colleague to collect and record the growth data without knowing which plots received the additive. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the experimental plots are located on the eastern side of a field where they receive more morning sunlight than the control plots on the western side. The randomisation of assignment was genuine at the level of plot labelling, but the spatial arrangement introduces a systematic confound that the randomisation was unable to eliminate because the number of plots was too small to distribute the spatial effect evenly.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the randomisation procedure be adequate to eliminate systematic confounds arising from the physical or environmental arrangement of the experimental setup, so that a genuinely random assignment may nonetheless leave in place a confounding variable that vitiates the comparison between experimental and control groups.
passage: One might propose that a controlled experiment is a procedure that includes experimental and control groups, assigns subjects randomly to these groups, and ensures that the experimenter is unaware of group assignments during data collection. This characterisation captures the comparative, randomised, and blinded structure that we demand of the most rigorous experimental designs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from agricultural field trials. Suppose a botanist tests whether a soil additive promotes the growth of wheat by dividing plots randomly into experimental and control groups, applying the additive to the experimental plots, and arranging for a colleague to collect data without knowledge of which plots received the treatment. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the experimental plots happen to be located on the eastern side of the field, where they receive more morning sunlight, while the control plots occupy the western side. The randomisation was genuine at the level of plot labelling, but the number of plots was too small for the random assignment to distribute the spatial effect evenly. The additional sunlight confounds the effect of the additive. It appears that the definition, by requiring only randomisation without demanding its adequacy, fails to ensure that systematic confounds arising from the physical arrangement of the experiment are eliminated. Without such a requirement, a genuinely random assignment may leave in place a confounding variable that undermines the validity of the comparison.

definition: A scientific explanation of a phenomenon is an account that (i) cites a general law under which the phenomenon falls, (ii) specifies initial conditions from which the phenomenon can be deduced, and (iii) renders the occurrence of the phenomenon expected rather than surprising.
conditions: The account cites a general law under which the phenomenon falls
The account specifies initial conditions from which the phenomenon can be deduced
The account renders the occurrence of the phenomenon expected rather than surprising
counterexample: Suppose that a barometer falls sharply and one cites the general law that a drop in atmospheric pressure causes barometric readings to fall, together with the initial condition that atmospheric pressure has dropped. This account cites a general law, specifies initial conditions from which the barometric drop can be deduced, and renders the reading expected. Yet it does not explain the ensuing storm, even though the same initial conditions and law could be marshalled to deduce the storm. The barometric reading is merely a symptom, not an explanation, of the storm.
missing_condition: The account must cite conditions that stand in a genuine causal or explanatory relation to the phenomenon, not merely conditions that are nomologically correlated with it.
passage: It has long been supposed that to explain a phenomenon is to show that it was to be expected on the basis of certain laws and antecedent conditions. One might observe that this conception has considerable intuitive appeal, for when we explain why a bridge collapsed we do indeed point to physical laws governing stress and load, together with the particular conditions of the structure in question. Yet we should hesitate to say that every deduction from laws and initial conditions constitutes a genuine explanation. Consider a case in which a meteorologist notes a sharp fall in the barometer and, invoking the well-established regularity that barometric drops accompany declining atmospheric pressure, together with the observed pressure decline, deduces the occurrence of a storm. Here the barometric drop is cited, the relevant law is invoked, and the storm is rendered expected. Nevertheless, it appears that the barometer reading does not explain the storm; it merely signals it. The reading and the storm are joint effects of a common cause, namely the pressure change. We are led to conclude that an analysis which demands only subsumption under law and deduction from initial conditions is insufficient to distinguish genuine explanatory relations from cases of mere nomological correlation. What is wanted, it seems, is a further requirement that the cited conditions stand in a properly causal or productive relation to the explanandum.

definition: A statement is a scientific law if and only if (i) it is a true universal generalisation, (ii) it supports counterfactual conditionals, and (iii) it has been confirmed by repeated observation.
conditions: The statement is a true universal generalisation
The statement supports counterfactual conditionals
The statement has been confirmed by repeated observation
counterexample: Consider a case in which the statement 'All spheres of enriched uranium have a mass of less than one hundred thousand kilograms' is true, universal, and confirmed by every observation of enriched uranium spheres. Moreover, it supports the counterfactual that if there were another such sphere, it too would have a mass below the threshold, since physical constraints on critical mass ensure this. Yet one might observe that this statement appears accidental in character, being a consequence of practical and physical limitations on assembling such spheres, rather than expressing a fundamental regularity of nature in the manner of, say, Newton's law of gravitation.
missing_condition: The generalisation must be integrated into a broader theoretical framework and not merely hold as a consequence of contingent practical limitations.
passage: The question of what distinguishes a genuine law of nature from a merely accidental generalisation has exercised philosophers of science for some time. It is commonly held that a scientific law must be a true universal generalisation which supports counterfactual reasoning and which has been confirmed by observation. One might observe that this analysis serves tolerably well in straightforward cases: the statement that all metals expand when heated is true, supports counterfactuals, and has been abundantly confirmed. Yet we should hesitate to say that every statement meeting these three conditions thereby qualifies as a law. Suppose that one considers the proposition that all spheres of enriched uranium possess a mass below one hundred thousand kilograms. This is true without exception, for no such sphere of greater mass has ever existed or, given the constraints of critical mass, could stably exist. The statement supports counterfactuals in an obvious sense, and it has been confirmed by every relevant observation. Nonetheless, it appears that this generalisation lacks the character of a fundamental law; it holds not because of a deep regularity in nature but because of the contingent impossibility of assembling such an object. We are led to conclude that the analysis requires a further condition, namely that the generalisation be integrated into a systematic theoretical framework rather than holding merely as a consequence of practical or contingent physical limitations.

definition: A body of propositions constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) it provides a unified account of a range of phenomena, (ii) it generates testable predictions, and (iii) it is internally consistent.
conditions: It provides a unified account of a range of phenomena
It generates testable predictions
It is internally consistent
counterexample: Suppose that an elaborate astrological system provides a unified account of personality traits, career outcomes, and interpersonal compatibility, generates specific testable predictions about individuals born under certain planetary alignments, and is internally consistent in its axioms and derivations. This system meets all three conditions, yet one might hesitate to call it a scientific theory, for it lacks any well-confirmed mechanism connecting planetary positions to human behaviour and its predictions, when rigorously tested, fare no better than chance.
missing_condition: The propositions must be connected to the phenomena through well-confirmed causal or nomological mechanisms, and the predictions must have survived serious empirical testing.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a scientific theory is simply a consistent body of propositions that unifies diverse phenomena and yields testable predictions. This analysis captures much of what we ordinarily mean: the kinetic theory of gases, for instance, provides a unified account of pressure, temperature, and diffusion, generates precise predictions, and is internally consistent. Yet we should hesitate to say that these three conditions are jointly sufficient. Consider a case in which an astrological system is developed with considerable formal rigour. Its axioms concern the positions of celestial bodies at the moment of birth, and from these axioms one may derive a unified account of personality, vocation, and personal relations. The system is internally consistent, for its authors have taken care to eliminate contradictions among its postulates. Moreover, it generates testable predictions: it tells us, for example, that persons born under a particular conjunction of planets will exhibit a specific temperament. Here, then, is a body of propositions that is unified, predictive, and consistent. Nevertheless, it appears that we should not regard this system as a scientific theory in any proper sense. Its predictions, when subjected to rigorous examination, are not borne out, and no credible mechanism links its postulates to the phenomena they purport to explain. We are led to conclude that a scientific theory must not only predict but must have its predictions survive serious test, and must be connected to the phenomena through well-confirmed nomological or causal relations.

definition: An observation report is scientifically objective if and only if (i) it describes what is directly perceived through the senses, (ii) it is expressible in language that any competent observer could understand, and (iii) it is independent of the theoretical commitments of the observer.
conditions: It describes what is directly perceived through the senses
It is expressible in language that any competent observer could understand
It is independent of the theoretical commitments of the observer
counterexample: Suppose that a trained microbiologist, peering through a microscope, reports seeing a Golgi apparatus in a cell preparation. She describes what she directly perceives, and her report is in language any competent biologist could understand. Yet her identification of the structure as a Golgi apparatus is thoroughly dependent on her theoretical training; an untrained observer would perceive only an amorphous dark region. The observation report appears to satisfy the first two conditions yet fails the third, revealing that the conditions may never be jointly satisfiable in scientific practice.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to acknowledge that perception in science is always theory-laden, so the condition of theoretical independence cannot be met in any observation of scientific significance.
passage: A persistent aspiration in the philosophy of science has been to identify a class of observation reports that are wholly objective, serving as a neutral foundation upon which theoretical structures may be erected. One might propose that such a report must describe what is directly perceived, must employ language accessible to any competent observer, and must be free from the theoretical commitments of the person making the report. This analysis has an appealing simplicity. Yet we should hesitate to accept it without examination. Consider a case in which a trained microbiologist examines a cell preparation under a powerful microscope and reports that she observes a Golgi apparatus. She describes what she perceives through the eyepiece, and she employs standard biological terminology that any competent colleague would understand. It appears, however, that her observation is far from theoretically innocent. Her identification of the perceived structure as a Golgi apparatus depends upon years of training in cell biology; an observer lacking this theoretical background would perceive only a darkened region of no particular significance. One is led to recognise that the third condition, independence from theoretical commitments, is not merely sometimes difficult to achieve but may be in principle unattainable for observations of any scientific interest. The analysis thus reveals its own insufficiency, for it demands a kind of perceptual neutrality that the practice of science does not and perhaps cannot afford.

definition: A higher-level scientific theory T1 is reduced to a lower-level theory T2 if and only if (i) the terms of T1 can be translated into the vocabulary of T2 by means of bridge laws, (ii) the laws of T1 can be logically derived from the laws of T2 together with these bridge laws, and (iii) T2 provides a more fundamental account of the phenomena described by T1.
conditions: The terms of T1 can be translated into the vocabulary of T2 by bridge laws
The laws of T1 can be logically derived from T2 together with the bridge laws
T2 provides a more fundamental account of the phenomena described by T1
counterexample: Suppose that one attempts to reduce classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. Bridge laws are proposed connecting temperature to mean molecular kinetic energy, and from these laws together with the principles of statistical mechanics one can derive the ideal gas law. Statistical mechanics provides a more fundamental account. Yet the second law of thermodynamics, stated as an exceptionless law in the higher-level theory, corresponds in statistical mechanics only to an overwhelmingly probable tendency, not to a strict derivation. The reduction satisfies all three conditions only by subtly altering the content of the law being reduced.
missing_condition: The reduction must preserve the modal strength and exact content of the higher-level laws, not merely approximate them or alter their character from exceptionless to probabilistic.
passage: The classical account of intertheoretic reduction holds that a higher-level theory is reduced to a more fundamental one when the terms of the former are connected to those of the latter by bridge laws, and the laws of the higher-level theory are then derivable from the lower-level theory together with these connecting principles. One might observe that this model has guided much discussion of the unity of science. Yet we should hesitate to suppose that it captures all that is required for genuine reduction. Consider a case in which one undertakes to reduce classical thermodynamics to statistical mechanics. Bridge laws are constructed: temperature is identified with mean molecular kinetic energy, pressure with the average force per unit area exerted by molecular collisions, and so forth. From the principles of statistical mechanics together with these bridge laws, one can derive such results as the ideal gas law, and statistical mechanics plainly provides the more fundamental account. It appears, however, that the second law of thermodynamics presents a difficulty. In its classical formulation, this law is exceptionless: entropy in an isolated system never decreases. In statistical mechanics, the corresponding principle is that entropy decrease is extraordinarily improbable but not strictly impossible. The derivation succeeds only by replacing an exceptionless law with a probabilistic tendency. We are thus led to conclude that the analysis of reduction must include a further requirement, namely that the modal character and precise content of the higher-level laws be preserved, not merely approximated, in the derivation.

definition: An experimental result confirms a hypothesis if and only if (i) the experiment was designed to test the hypothesis, (ii) the observed outcome was predicted by the hypothesis, and (iii) the outcome would not have occurred had the hypothesis been false.
conditions: The experiment was designed to test the hypothesis
The observed outcome was predicted by the hypothesis
The outcome would not have occurred had the hypothesis been false
counterexample: Suppose that a chemist designs an experiment to test the hypothesis that a novel compound dissolves in water at room temperature. She places the compound in water and observes that it dissolves. The experiment was designed for this purpose, dissolution was predicted, and had the hypothesis been false the compound would not have dissolved. Yet unbeknownst to her, the water had been accidentally heated above room temperature, and the compound dissolves only at elevated temperatures. The observed outcome coincides with the prediction, but the hypothesis as stated is in fact false; the experiment confirms nothing, despite satisfying all three conditions.
missing_condition: The experimental conditions must actually instantiate the circumstances specified by the hypothesis, so that the outcome is brought about by the factors the hypothesis identifies rather than by extraneous ones.
passage: It is widely held that an experiment confirms a hypothesis when the experiment is designed to test it, the predicted outcome obtains, and the outcome would not have occurred were the hypothesis false. One might observe that this tripartite analysis accords well with many paradigmatic cases of experimental confirmation. Yet we should hesitate to accept it as fully adequate. Consider a case in which a chemist wishes to determine whether a newly synthesised compound dissolves in water at room temperature. She places a sample in a beaker of what she believes to be water at twenty degrees Celsius and observes that the compound dissolves completely. The experiment was designed to test her hypothesis, dissolution was predicted by it, and had the compound been insoluble at room temperature one would expect it not to dissolve. All three conditions appear to be met. However, let us suppose that, owing to a fault in the laboratory equipment, the water had in fact been heated to sixty degrees, a temperature at which the compound dissolves regardless. The hypothesis that the compound dissolves at room temperature is false, yet every condition in our analysis is ostensibly satisfied. We are led to conclude that the analysis requires a further stipulation: the actual experimental conditions must correspond to those specified by the hypothesis, so that the outcome is produced by the very factors the hypothesis identifies and not by some uncontrolled and unrecognised variable.

definition: A measurement of a quantity Q is accurate if and only if (i) the measuring instrument is calibrated against a known standard, (ii) the numerical value obtained corresponds to the true value of Q within the instrument's margin of error, and (iii) the measurement procedure is repeatable under the same conditions.
conditions: The measuring instrument is calibrated against a known standard
The numerical value obtained corresponds to the true value of Q within the margin of error
The measurement procedure is repeatable under the same conditions
counterexample: Suppose that a physicist calibrates a voltmeter against a known standard and then measures the voltage across a circuit element. The value obtained, say 5.00 volts, is within the margin of error of the true voltage, and repeated measurements under the same conditions yield the same result. Yet the calibration standard itself was defective, having been corrupted by a manufacturing flaw, so that the instrument's calibration is merely coincidentally correct for this particular measurement range. The measurement satisfies all three conditions but is accurate only by accident.
missing_condition: The calibration standard must itself be reliable and traceable to an authoritative reference, not merely coincidentally correct.
passage: The concept of accuracy in measurement is of fundamental importance in the sciences, and it is natural to analyse it in terms of calibration, correspondence to true values, and repeatability. One might observe that a measurement is typically called accurate when the instrument has been calibrated, the reading matches the true value within accepted tolerances, and the result can be reproduced. Yet we should hesitate to suppose that these conditions are jointly sufficient. Consider a case in which a physicist undertakes to measure the voltage across a circuit element using a voltmeter that has been calibrated against what is taken to be a known standard. She obtains a reading of 5.00 volts, which corresponds to the true voltage within the instrument's stated margin of error. She repeats the measurement several times under identical conditions and obtains the same result. It appears that the measurement is accurate in every respect demanded by our analysis. Suppose, however, that the calibration standard itself was defective, having been damaged during manufacture, and that it happened to give correct values only within the narrow range in which the physicist was working. The calibration was, as it were, accidentally right. It seems clear that we should not regard this as a genuinely accurate measurement procedure, even though the result happens to be correct. We are thus led to conclude that the analysis must require not merely that the instrument be calibrated against a standard, but that the standard itself be reliable and traceable to an authoritative reference.

definition: A scientific prediction is successful if and only if (i) the prediction is derived from a theory prior to the observation of the predicted event, (ii) the predicted event is subsequently observed to occur, and (iii) the prediction specifies the event with sufficient precision to distinguish it from alternative outcomes.
conditions: The prediction is derived from a theory prior to the observation of the predicted event
The predicted event is subsequently observed to occur
The prediction specifies the event with sufficient precision to distinguish it from alternative outcomes
counterexample: Suppose that a cosmologist derives from a speculative theory the prediction that a certain type of particle will be detected in a particular energy range. The prediction is made well before the experiment, and subsequently a particle is indeed detected at the predicted energy. The prediction is precise enough to distinguish this outcome from alternatives. Yet the particle was produced not by the mechanism the theory posits but by an entirely different and well-understood process that the cosmologist had overlooked. The prediction succeeds by coincidence, not because the theory is correct.
missing_condition: The observed event must be brought about by the mechanism or process that the theory identifies, not merely coincide with the prediction by virtue of an unrelated cause.
passage: It is generally supposed that the success of a scientific prediction lends support to the theory from which it is derived. The standard analysis holds that a prediction is successful when it is derived in advance from a theory, the predicted event is subsequently observed, and the prediction is precise enough to be genuinely informative. One might observe that this analysis serves well in many cases: the prediction of Neptune's position from Newtonian mechanics, for example, was derived in advance, confirmed by observation, and specified with considerable precision. Yet we should hesitate to conclude that these conditions are sufficient in all cases. Consider a case in which a cosmologist, working from a speculative theory of particle physics, predicts that a novel particle will be detected at a specified energy level in a forthcoming experiment. The prediction is made months before the experiment, and when the data are analysed, a particle is indeed detected at precisely the predicted energy. The prediction is sufficiently precise to rule out competing outcomes. It appears that the prediction is successful in every sense our analysis requires. Suppose, however, that the detected particle was produced not by the mechanism the speculative theory describes but by an entirely different and well-understood process, one which the cosmologist had failed to consider. The coincidence between prediction and observation is just that, a coincidence. We are led to conclude that the analysis must be supplemented with a requirement that the observed event be produced by the causal process the theory identifies.

definition: An organism belongs to a given biological species if and only if (i) it shares a set of morphological characteristics diagnostic of that species, (ii) it is capable of interbreeding with other members of the species to produce fertile offspring, and (iii) it occupies the same ecological niche as other members of the species.
conditions: It shares morphological characteristics diagnostic of the species
It is capable of interbreeding with other members to produce fertile offspring
It occupies the same ecological niche as other members of the species
counterexample: Consider a case in which two populations of birds share identical morphological characteristics, are capable of interbreeding to produce fertile offspring when brought together in captivity, and occupy the same ecological niche in overlapping geographical regions. Yet they never interbreed in the wild, maintaining strict behavioural isolation through different mating songs. One might argue they satisfy all three conditions yet constitute distinct species by virtue of their reproductive isolation in natural conditions.
missing_condition: The interbreeding must occur under natural conditions, not merely be possible in artificial circumstances, and behavioural or other prezygotic isolation mechanisms must be taken into account.
passage: The classification of organisms into species is among the most fundamental tasks of the biological sciences, and the question of what it is that makes an organism a member of a given species has generated much discussion. A familiar analysis holds that species membership requires shared morphological features, the capacity to interbreed with fellow members to yield fertile offspring, and occupancy of a common ecological niche. One might observe that this tripartite criterion appears to capture what is essential about the species concept as it is employed in practice. Yet we should hesitate to regard it as wholly adequate. Consider a case in which two populations of songbirds are found in overlapping territories. They are morphologically indistinguishable even to the trained ornithologist; when brought together in captivity they produce fertile hybrid offspring without difficulty; and they feed upon the same insects and nest in the same types of tree. By our analysis, they belong to a single species. However, in their natural habitat these populations never interbreed, for their mating songs are entirely different, and each population responds only to the songs of its own kind. The behavioural barrier is complete and has persisted over many generations. It appears that we have two groups which satisfy every stated condition and yet are, by any reasonable biological judgement, distinct species. We are led to conclude that the analysis must be supplemented with a condition requiring that interbreeding take place under natural conditions, and that mechanisms of reproductive isolation be explicitly considered.

definition: A scientific model is adequate for a given domain if and only if (i) it represents the relevant structural relations among the phenomena in that domain, (ii) it yields quantitatively accurate predictions within the domain, and (iii) its assumptions are consistent with established background theories.
conditions: It represents the relevant structural relations among the phenomena
It yields quantitatively accurate predictions within the domain
Its assumptions are consistent with established background theories
counterexample: Suppose that Ptolemaic epicycle models of planetary motion represent the structural relations among observed planetary positions, yield quantitatively accurate predictions of where planets will appear in the night sky, and employ assumptions about circular motion that were consistent with the background physics of the time. These models satisfy all three conditions, yet they are widely regarded as inadequate because they achieve accuracy only through the ad hoc addition of epicycles and do not represent the actual causal structure of the solar system.
missing_condition: The model must achieve its accuracy through principled rather than ad hoc means, and its structural representations must correspond to the actual causal or physical structure of the phenomena, not merely to their observable patterns.
passage: The adequacy of a scientific model is a matter of considerable philosophical interest. It is tempting to analyse adequacy in terms of three conditions: the model must represent the structural relations among the relevant phenomena, it must yield accurate predictions, and its assumptions must cohere with established background theory. One might observe that this analysis has an appealing comprehensiveness. Yet we should hesitate to regard it as fully satisfactory. Consider a case in which the Ptolemaic system of epicycles is employed to predict planetary positions. This system represents the angular relations among observed positions with some fidelity, yields predictions that are quantitatively accurate to within acceptable tolerances, and rests upon assumptions about circular motion that were entirely consistent with the natural philosophy accepted in antiquity and through the mediaeval period. By the proposed analysis, the model is adequate. Nevertheless, it appears that the Ptolemaic system achieves its accuracy only through the repeated ad hoc introduction of additional epicycles, each added to accommodate a recalcitrant observation rather than derived from a principled theoretical foundation. Moreover, the structural relations it represents are relations among appearances, not among the actual physical bodies and forces responsible for planetary motion. We are thus led to conclude that model adequacy requires more than structural representation and predictive accuracy; the model must achieve these through principled means, and its structures must correspond to the genuine causal or physical architecture of the domain in question.
